- Requirements are **embodied in physical law or regulation** (medical device firmware per FDA 21 CFR Part 820, avionics per DO-178C, automotive MISRA-C).
- **Safety-critical certification** demands exhaustive documentation trails.
- **Contractual milestones** are tied to government procurement (e.g., DRDO, ISRO subsystems, defense command-and-control systems).

In such contexts, Agile's late-stage requirement changes would be catastrophic — making the Waterfall's rigidity a *feature, not a bug*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-of-Change Multiplier

The **Cost-of-Change (CoC)** curve is the most-tested concept in KTU examinations. Below is the complete, step-by-step derivation. Every algebraic transition is explicitly written out, and the multi-line equation is contained within a single `aligned` block separated by blank lines.

### Step 1 — Establish the Baseline Cost

Let $C_1$ be the baseline cost of fixing a single defect discovered during the **Requirements** phase. This is the cheapest possible correction point because the defect exists only on paper and no code, design, or test artifacts are polluted.

$$C_{requirements} = C_1$$

### Step 2 — Define the Geometric Growth Hypothesis

Empirical software-engineering studies (Boehm 1981, McConnell 1996) observe that downstream correction cost grows **geometrically** with phase distance. Formally, if a defect migrates $k$ phases downstream, the cost is amplified by a factor of $10^k$.

$$C_{phase\,n} = C_1 \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Formula to All 5 Waterfall Phases

We substitute $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ into the closed-form expression. The following block contains every numerical evaluation in a single alignment environment with explanatory text on each row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{requirements} &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(1-1)} = C_1 \cdot 10^{0} = 1 \cdot C_1 \\
C_{design}       &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(2-1)} = C_1 \cdot 10^{1} = 10 \cdot C_1 \\
C_{implementation} &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(3-1)} = C_1 \cdot 10^{2} = 100 \cdot C_1 \\
C_{integration}  &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(4-1)} = C_1 \cdot 10^{3} = 1000 \cdot C_1 \\
C_{maintenance}  &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(5-1)} = C_1 \cdot 10^{4} = 10{,}000 \cdot C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reading the result:** A single requirement defect left uncorrected in Phase 1, which would cost $C_1 = \text{₹1,000}$ to fix, will cost approximately **₹1,00,00,000** (one crore in Indian notation) to correct after deployment in Phase 5. This is the **10,000× amplification** that justifies the Waterfall Model's emphasis on thorough Phase 1 elicitation.

### Step 4 — Derive the Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Worked Example

Suppose a project releases a system with the following defect profile:
- Defects found before release: $D_{pre} = 870$
- Defects found within 6 months after release: $D_{post} = 130$

Substitute directly into the DRE formula defined in Section 2.3.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRE} &= \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 \\
           &= \frac{870}{870 + 130} \times 100 \\
           &= \frac{870}{1000} \times 100 \\
           &= 87\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Interpretation:** A DRE of **87%** is considered **good** for a Waterfall project (industry benchmark: 85%–95%). Below 75% indicates weak Phase 1–3 V&V activities.

### Step 5 — Maintenance Cost Ratio Worked Example

If a 5-year project has the following phase-wise effort distribution (in person-months):
- Requirements: $E_1 = 8$
- Design: $E_2 = 14$
- Implementation: $E_3 = 36$
- Integration: $E_4 = 22$
- Maintenance: $E_5 = 120$

First, compute the total effort, then derive the maintenance ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + E_4 + E_5 \\
          &= 8 + 14 + 36 + 22 + 120 \\
          &= 200 \text{ person-months}
\end{aligned}
$$

The maintenance ratio is then obtained by simple division.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{maintenance} &= \frac{E_5}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
                &= \frac{120}{200} \times 100 \\
                &= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Interpretation:** This matches the **Lientz & Swanson (1980)** empirical study which found that **60%–80%** of total software lifecycle cost is spent on **maintenance** — a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use the Waterfall Model

The following matrix is **examination-grade** material. KTU examiners regularly award 7 marks for this kind of contextual analysis in 14-mark questions.

| **Criterion** | **Waterfall Suitable ✓** | **Waterfall Unsuitable ✗** |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements clarity** | Well-understood, stable, regulatory | Ambiguous, evolving, exploratory |
| **Project size** | Small-to-medium with frozen scope | Large, multi-year, distributed |
| **Customer availability** | Limited access after kick-off | Continuous, on-site, embedded |
| **Technology stack** | Mature, well-known | New, untested, R&D-heavy |
| **Domain** | Defense, aerospace, embedded, medical | Web, mobile, consumer apps |
| **Risk profile** | Low-to-medium, predictable | High, novel, market-driven |
| **Documentation needs** | Heavy contractual obligations | Minimal, working-software-first |
| **Change frequency** | < 5% requirements change | > 20% requirements change |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Linear Waterfall Flow

The following Mermaid diagram renders the canonical 5-phase Waterfall flow with explicit **Verification milestones** between each phase and an annotated **Feedback Loop (Royce Critique)** showing the iteration Royce actually proposed in 1970.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqA[Phase 1: Requirements Analysis] --> verA{V&V Review 1}
    verA -->|Customer signs SRS| desB[Phase 2: System and Software Design]
    verA -->|Rejected| reqA

    desB --> verB{V&V Review 2}
    verB -->|Design approved| impC[Phase 3: Implementation and Unit Testing]
    verB -->|Rejected| desB

    impC --> verC{V&V Review 3}
    verC -->|Code complete| intD[Phase 4: Integration and System Testing]
    verC -->|Defects found| impC

    intD --> verD{V&V Review 4}
    verD -->|Acceptance passed| opsE[Phase 5: Operation and Maintenance]
    verD -->|Test failures| intD

    opsE --> fbLoop((Feedback Loops: Royce 1970))
    fbLoop -.->|Iterative refinement| reqA
    fbLoop -.->|Iterative refinement| desB
    fbLoop -.->|Iterative refinement| impC
    fbLoop -.->|Iterative refinement| intD

    style reqA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style desB fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style impC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    style intD fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style opsE fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    style fbLoop fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#d63384,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Viva:**
> 1. The **solid arrows** represent the *primary sequential flow* between phases.
> 2. The **diamond nodes (V&V Review)** are the *gate-keeping milestones* — a phase cannot proceed without sign-off.
> 3. The **dashed pink arrows** forming the `fbLoop` represent the *iterative feedback loops* that Royce himself advocated — distinguishing the **idealized linear Waterfall** from the **Royce-corrected iterative Waterfall**. This is a 7-mark territory in KTU essays.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram provides a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the actors, inputs, processing, and outputs of each Waterfall phase. This is the recommended substitute for physical drawings in KTU Module 1 answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    custA[Customer / Stakeholder] -->|Needs| reqP1[Phase 1: Requirements]
    reqP1 -->|SRS Document| custA
    reqP1 -->|SRS Document| desP2[Phase 2: Design]
    desP2 -->|SDD Document| archT[Architecture Team]
    desP2 -->|SDD Document| devT[Development Team]
    devT -->|Source Code| impP3[Phase 3: Implementation]
    impP3 -->|Unit Test Report| qaT[QA Team]
    impP3 -->|Integrated Code| intP4[Phase 4: Integration and Testing]
    intP4 -->|Test Reports| qaT
    intP4 -->|Acceptance Sign-off| custA
    intP4 -->|Released System| opsP5[Phase 5: Operations and Maintenance]
    opsP5 -->|Defect Logs, Patches| devT
    opsP5 -->|Release Notes| custA

    style reqP1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style desP2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style impP3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style intP4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style opsP5 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
```

> **Reading Guide:** This diagram traces the **information flow** between the customer, development teams, and the QA team. Note that the **customer is "batched"** — they are heavily involved only at Phase 1 (SRS) and Phase 4 (Acceptance). In Agile, this customer would be continuously involved — a contrast the KTU Module 1 examiner expects you to articulate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAIT413]`
**"Define the Waterfall Model. List its main phases."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**
The Waterfall Model is a **linear-sequential, plan-driven software process model** in which each phase of the SDLC must be completed and approved before the next begins. It was described by Winston Royce in 1970. The five main phases are:
1. **Requirements Analysis & Definition** (produces SRS)
2. **System & Software Design** (produces SDD)
3. **Implementation & Unit Testing** (produces source code)
4. **Integration & System Testing** (produces test reports)
5. **Operation & Maintenance** (produces release notes & patches)

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Naming all 5 phases (1) + Naming deliverables (1).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAIT413]`
**"State any two advantages and two disadvantages of the Waterfall Model."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages:**
1. **Simplicity and ease of project management** — due to the fixed milestone-driven plan.
2. **Strong documentation discipline** — every phase produces a formal artifact, ideal for regulatory and defense domains.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Late discovery of defects** — testing is delayed until Phase 4, so high-severity bugs surface too late.
2. **High cost of change** — the cost-of-change curve amplifies corrections by 10× per phase, making late changes prohibitively expensive.

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 advantages (1 mark) + 2 disadvantages (1 mark) + Clarity/examples (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAIT413 — Module 1, Choice A]`

> **(a) Explain the Waterfall Model in detail. Describe each phase with its deliverables and the verification activities performed at the end of each phase. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Waterfall Model**, introduced by Winston Royce in 1970, is a **plan-driven, sequential software life-cycle model** where development flows strictly downward through five phases, each concluding with a formal verification and validation (V&V) gate.

**Phase 1 — Requirements Analysis and Definition (1 mark):**
The customer's needs are elicited via interviews, workshops, and feasibility studies. The output is the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** per IEEE 830. V&V activity: a **requirements review** by stakeholders; each requirement is checked for *correctness, feasibility, verifiability, and traceability*. Exit criterion: customer sign-off.

**Phase 2 — System and Software Design (1.5 marks):**
The SRS is translated into a complete design — both **high-level (architectural)** and **low-level (detailed module)** designs. Artifacts include **DFDs, ER diagrams, UML class/sequence diagrams, and the Software Design Description (SDD)**. V&V activity: **design walkthroughs** and **inspection reviews** to verify that the design correctly implements the SRS and to validate feasibility, modularity, and coupling-cohesion.

**Phase 3 — Implementation and Unit Testing (1.5 marks):**
Designs are coded using the chosen language. Each module undergoes **unit testing** (white-box) using tools such as JUnit/NUnit. V&V activity: **code reviews**, **static analysis**, and **unit test reports**. Exit criterion: all modules pass unit tests with ≥ 80% code coverage.

**Phase 4 — Integration and System Testing (1.5 marks):**
Modules are integrated (top-down, bottom-up, or sandwich strategy) and tested against the SRS. Test levels include **integration → system → acceptance (UAT)**. V&V activity: **black-box test execution, regression testing, performance/load testing, and acceptance sign-off by the customer**.

**Phase 5 — Operation and Maintenance (1.5 marks):**
The system is deployed in the production environment. Four types of maintenance are performed: **corrective (bug fixes), adaptive (OS/platform changes), perfective (new features), and preventive (refactoring)**. V&V activity: **post-release defect tracking, change-request management, and periodic maintenance releases**.

> **Conclusion:** The Waterfall Model's strength is its discipline; its weakness is its rigidity in accommodating late requirements changes — the very limitation that motivated the rise of Agile methodologies in the 1990s–2000s.

---

> **(b) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Waterfall Model. Under what conditions is the Waterfall Model the most appropriate choice for a software project? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Project management clarity** — milestones, Gantt charts, and Earned Value Analysis (EVA) work seamlessly with the fixed-phase structure.
2. **Documentation rigor** — every phase produces a tangible, reviewable artifact, satisfying regulatory and contractual needs.
3. **Resource specialization** — analysts, designers, coders, and testers can be assigned to their respective phases without overlap.
4. **Customer sign-off ensures alignment** — frozen requirements eliminate ambiguity about the deliverable.
5. **Suitable for outsourcing** — a vendor can deliver a project solely against the SRS and SDD documents.

**Disadvantages (2.5 marks):**
1. **Late discovery of defects** — the testing phase occurs after all coding is done, multiplying fix costs.
2. **High cost of change** — the **10× per phase** cost-of-change multiplier makes late changes almost economically infeasible.
3. **Customer feedback is delayed** — the customer only sees a working product at the end, increasing the risk of "that's not what I wanted."
4. **Not suitable for complex, object-oriented projects** — modern systems require iterative refinement that the linear model forbids.
5. **Risk of long feedback cycles** — issues remain latent for months.

**Suitability Conditions (1.5 marks):**
The Waterfall Model is most appropriate when:
- **Requirements are stable, clear, and well-understood** (e.g., tax-calc software, payroll systems).
- **The technology is mature** and the team has prior experience.
- **The domain is safety- or mission-critical** with **regulatory compliance** requirements (aerospace, medical devices, defense).
- **Project scope and budget are fixed** by external contracts.
- **Short projects** with low complexity (≤ 6 months).
- **Maintenance-only or re-engineering projects** where the existing system is the SRS.

> **Conclusion:** The Waterfall Model remains a valid choice in **regulated and small-to-medium, well-defined projects**, but for modern, dynamic, customer-facing software, **Agile methodologies** are preferred — a point you must contrast in your answer.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAIT413 — Module 1, Choice B]`

> **(a) Explain the Cost-of-Change curve in the context of the Waterfall Model. Show that a defect caught in the maintenance phase is approximately 10,000 times more expensive to fix than the same defect caught in the requirements phase. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Cost-of-Change (CoC) curve** is a fundamental software-engineering concept that quantifies the relative cost of fixing a defect at different stages of the Waterfall life cycle. Empirical studies by Barry Boehm (1981) and Steve McConnell (1996) observed that **the cost of correcting a defect grows geometrically (approximately 10× per phase)** as the defect migrates downstream.

**Derivation of the 10,000× ratio (5 marks):**
Let $C_1$ be the baseline cost of fixing a defect during the **Requirements** phase ($n = 1$). The closed-form geometric growth expression is:

$$C_n = C_1 \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$$

For the Maintenance phase, $n = 5$. Substituting and simplifying step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{maintenance} &= C_1 \cdot 10^{(5-1)} \\
                &= C_1 \cdot 10^{4} \\
                &= 10{,}000 \cdot C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the ratio of maintenance-phase cost to requirements-phase cost is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{C_{maintenance}}{C_{requirements}} = \frac{C_1 \cdot 10^{4}}{C_1 \cdot 10^{0}} = 10^{4} = 10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical illustration (2 marks):** If fixing a missing requirement at the requirements stage costs ₹1,000 (analyst time to update the SRS), the same defect, if missed and discovered post-deployment, would cost ₹1,00,00,000 — covering regression testing, redeployment, customer support, possible compensation, and reputational damage.

> **Conclusion:** The CoC curve is the **primary justification** for the Waterfall Model's front-loaded requirements phase, and the **primary motivation** for Agile's emphasis on continuous testing and early delivery to flatten the cost curve.

---

> **(b) Compare the Waterfall Model and the Agile Model across at least six dimensions. Which model is more suitable for a startup building a consumer-facing mobile application, and why? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Waterfall Model** | **Agile Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Sequential, linear | Iterative, incremental |
| Requirements | Fixed upfront | Evolving, refined per sprint |
| Customer involvement | At start (SRS) and end (UAT) | Continuous (every sprint review) |
| Documentation | Heavy, formal, contract-grade | Lightweight, "working software over docs" |
| Delivery | Single final release | Frequent incremental releases (every 2–4 weeks) |
| Change tolerance | **Low** — change requests are formal & costly | **High** — changes welcomed in every sprint |
| Testing | Deferred to Phase 4 | Continuous (Test-Driven Development, CI/CD) |
| Risk handling | Late discovery of defects | Early discovery via short feedback loops |
| Team structure | Specialized (analyst/designer/coder/QA) | Cross-functional, self-organizing |
| Best for | Defense, medical, regulated, fixed-scope | Startups, web/mobile, dynamic markets |

**Suitability for a consumer mobile app startup (3 marks):**
The **Agile Model** is overwhelmingly more suitable for a startup building a consumer-facing mobile application, for the following reasons:

1. **Dynamic, evolving requirements** — consumer preferences, UI/UX trends, and platform APIs (iOS/Android) change frequently. Waterfall's frozen SRS would lock the startup into obsolete assumptions.
2. **Time-to-market pressure** — startups must launch a **Minimum Viable Product (MVP)** in weeks, not months. Agile's 2-week sprints allow rapid user feedback.
3. **Continuous user feedback** — Agile's sprint reviews let the startup pivot based on real user metrics (DAU, retention, App Store reviews), whereas Waterfall would have no real users until launch.
4. **Uncertain scope** — startup product roadmaps are hypotheses, not contracts. Waterfall's change-control board would slow innovation.
5. **Cross-functional teams** — modern mobile teams blend design, frontend, backend, and QA, which aligns with Agile's team structure.

> **Conclusion:** While the Waterfall Model excels in **regulated, contract-driven, large-scale systems**, the **Agile Model** is the right choice for any environment characterized by **uncertainty, rapid change, and user-centric iteration** — exactly the conditions a startup faces.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Waterfall Model questions — Examiner's Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Misattributing the model to Pressman or Sommerville** — Dr. **Winston Royce (1970)** is the originator. Naming the wrong author costs you a full mark in definition questions.
> 2. **Treating the Waterfall as strictly linear with NO iteration** — Royce's *original* paper actually proposed an **iterative** version with feedback loops. KTU answers that simply draw a straight arrow chain and call it "Waterfall" lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the deliverables of each phase** — SRS for Phase 1, SDD for Phase 2, source code for Phase 3, test reports for Phase 4, maintenance logs for Phase 5. Examiners explicitly look for these.
> 4. **Confusing the "Cost-of-Change" formula** — the **10× per phase** heuristic must be stated clearly, not just "the cost increases." Examiners want the geometric $10^{(n-1)}$ form.
> 5. **Writing a generic "advantages/disadvantages" essay without domain context** — KTU Module 1 specifically demands a **comparison with Agile**. A Waterfall answer that *does not* contrast with Agile is marked down by 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion** — always end with a one-line takeaway that ties the concept back to **Agile Methodologies** (the course title). This shows syllabus awareness.
> 7. **Drawing the Mermaid/flowchart with reserved keywords as node IDs** — using `end`, `subgraph`, or `style` as a node name will crash the renderer and cost you diagram marks. Always prefix with letters (`e.g., node1`, `phaseA`).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Waterfall Model (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Originator:** Dr. **Winston W. Royce (1970)**, paper titled *"Managing the Development of Large Software Systems"* at IEEE WESCON.
- ☐ **Definition:** Linear-sequential, plan-driven, document-driven, phase-gated SDLC model.
- ☐ **Five Phases:** Requirements → Design → Implementation → Integration/Testing → Maintenance. (Memorize the order.)
- ☐ **Phase 1 Deliverable:** **SRS** (IEEE 830). Phase 2: **SDD + DFDs + ER + UML**. Phase 3: Source code + unit tests. Phase 4: Test reports + UAT sign-off. Phase 5: Release notes + patches.
- ☐ **Cost-of-Change Formula:** $C_n = C_1 \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$. **Maintenance cost ≈ 10,000× requirements cost.**
- ☐ **Royce Paradox:** The original paper **critiqued** the strict linear model and proposed iteration. The "no-going-back" version is a popular simplification.
- ☐ **Maintenance Cost Ratio:** **60%–80%** of total lifecycle cost (Lientz & Swanson, 1980).
- ☐ **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE):** Industry benchmark **85%–95%** for mature Waterfall projects.
- ☐ **Suitable Domains:** Defense, aerospace (DO-178C), medical devices (FDA 21 CFR Part 820), embedded firmware, payroll, government projects.
- ☐ **Unsuitable Domains:** Consumer mobile apps, web startups, R&D, exploratory R&D, AI/ML products.
- ☐ **Key Contrast with Agile:** Waterfall is **plan-driven, document-heavy, late-testing, change-resistant**; Agile is **value-driven, working-software-first, continuous-testing, change-embracing**.
- ☐ **Mandatory KTU Question Hook:** Always end any Waterfall answer by linking it to the **emergence of Agile** — this earns the examiner's "course-coherence" mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incremental Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Incremental Development** is a software engineering approach in which the system is designed, implemented, and tested *incrementally* (in small pieces), with each increment adding a functional subset of the total system functionality. Rather than delivering the complete system at the end of the project life cycle, the system is built and delivered in successive, operational versions where **each version is a complete, working subset** of the entire system.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Incremental Development is positioned as one of the **foundational precursors to modern Agile practices**. It is classified as an evolution of the **plan-driven software development paradigm** that responds to the well-known shortcomings of the traditional **Waterfall Model**, particularly the long delivery delay and the inability to respond to changing user requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Incremental Development is one of the *three core software development process models* that students must master before transitioning into pure Agile frameworks (Scrum, XP, etc.). It is a direct bridge concept between the classical plan-driven methods and modern iterative Agile methods.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **multi-storey residential house** for a client.

- **Waterfall Approach (NOT Incremental):** You would dig the entire foundation, raise all walls, plaster every room, install all electrical wiring and plumbing, paint the whole building, and only at the very end hand over the keys. The client cannot move in or *live in any part* of the house until the entire structure is finished. If the client changes their mind about the kitchen layout after the walls are plastered, the rework cost is **catastrophic**.

- **Incremental Approach:** You would build the house **floor by floor** (or wing by wing). After finishing the *Ground Floor (Increment 1)*, the client can immediately move into that portion, live in it, give feedback, and even earn rental income. While they occupy the ground floor, you continue building the *First Floor (Increment 2)*, and so on. At any point, the system (house) is **partially operational and usable**.

The core insight: **deliver value early, gather feedback, and add value continuously.**

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Incremental Development trades the *single, big, late delivery* of the Waterfall model for *multiple, smaller, earlier deliveries*. The "system" is always in a working state — just with growing functionality.

### 1.3 Position in the KTU Software Process Landscape

Incremental Development sits between the **traditional plan-driven** models and the **purely Agile/iterative** models.

> **Process Model Hierarchy (KTU Module 1 Perspective):**
>
> - **Plan-Driven Models** (Predictive)
>   - Waterfall Model *(single delivery, no increments)*
>   - **Incremental Development** *(planned, scheduled increments)*
>   - Iterative Development *(planned iterations of refinement)*
> - **Agile Models** (Adaptive)
>   - Scrum, XP, Kanban, Crystal, FDD, DSDM

Incremental Development, therefore, is a **transitional model** — it keeps the discipline and planning of Waterfall, but borrows the *time-boxed delivery* philosophy that later mature into full Agile methods.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Increment** | A self-contained, working subset of the system functionality delivered in a defined time-box. |
| **Functional Baseline** | A frozen point in development beyond which the requirements of an increment cannot be changed. |
| **System Architecture Skeleton** | The minimal core architecture established during the *first* increment upon which all subsequent increments are layered. |
| **Service-Based Architecture** | A common technical enabler for incremental development where the system is decomposed into loosely coupled services. |
| **Incremental Delivery** | The act of deploying each increment into the operational environment for end-user use. |

### 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory numerical thresholds that KTU examiners frequently test in 3-mark questions:**
>
> - The **first increment** must always contain a **minimal but functional core architecture** — this is the *non-negotiable architectural skeleton* required by the syllabus.
> - A typical project is decomposed into **3 to 6 increments** in a controlled, plan-driven incremental approach (this is **not** the 2-week Sprint of Agile — that is a *separate* concept).
> - Each increment must follow the **Mini-Waterfall sequence**: *Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Delivery* internally, but the *overall* project flow is interleaved across increments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How Incremental Development Actually Works

The Incremental Development approach is best understood by breaking it into **five operational pillars**, each of which is a high-frequency KTU question topic.

#### Pillar 1: Requirements Partitioning

The complete set of system requirements is **decomposed (partitioned)** into a logical sequence of *requirement groups*. Each group defines what one increment will deliver.

- Partitioning can be **function-based** (e.g., Order Processing, Payment, Reporting).
- Or **priority-based** (e.g., high-priority features first, then nice-to-haves).
- Or **risk-based** (high-risk requirements are delivered first to de-risk the project).

> **The "Why":** Not all requirements carry the same business value or the same technical risk. By partitioning, the team can deliver the *most valuable* and *least risky* slice first.

#### Pillar 2: Architectural Skeleton (Increment 0)

Before any user-facing increment, a **skeletal version of the system** is built. This skeleton provides:
- The basic structure of the system.
- Core cross-cutting services (logging, security, database).
- An executable, deployable, but functionally thin baseline.

> **The "How":** The skeleton is the *seed* from which all future increments grow. Without it, integration of later increments becomes chaotic.

#### Pillar 3: Increment-by-Increment Construction

For each increment $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$):

1. The requirements for that increment are finalized.
2. A **functional baseline** is frozen for that increment.
3. A *mini-Waterfall cycle* runs inside the increment: Design → Code → Test.
4. The increment is integrated with the prior baseline.
5. The new, larger baseline is **deployed and demonstrated** to stakeholders.

#### Pillar 4: Continuous Integration & Validation

After each increment is built, it must be **integrated with the previously delivered system** to produce a *new, complete, working system version*.

$$
S_{k} = S_{k-1} \cup I_{k}
$$

where $S_{k}$ is the system state after increment $k$, $S_{k-1}$ is the previous system state, and $I_{k}$ is the new increment being integrated.

#### Pillar 5: Progressive Stakeholder Feedback

After each increment, **user feedback is collected** and used to:
- Refine the requirements of *future* increments.
- Occasionally, *with controlled scope-change discipline*, modify the requirements of an in-flight increment (this is *not* the same as Waterfall's stage-gate freeze).

### 2.2 Three Architectural Variants of Incremental Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently frame a 14-mark question around the architectural decomposition strategy.** Memorize these three variants.

| Variant | Core Idea | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Increment by Feature (Horizontal)** | Each increment delivers a *slice* of every layer (UI, business logic, data) for a chosen feature. | Features are loosely coupled; user-facing delivery is the priority. |
| **Increment by Layer (Vertical / Tiers)** | Each increment delivers a *complete layer* (e.g., data layer, then service layer, then UI layer). | Infrastructure / non-functional priorities dominate (e.g., platform migrations). |
| **Increment by Service (Service-Oriented)** | Each increment is a *standalone service* plugged into a service bus. | Large enterprise systems with stable, long-lived service contracts. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is *conceptual*, not numerical, but the KTU Board consistently tests structured comparative knowledge. The following table consolidates the **essential comparison data points** that appear in nearly every KTU answer script.

**Comparison: Waterfall vs. Incremental Development**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Waterfall Model} & \textbf{Incremental Development} \\
\hline
\text{Delivery cadence} & \text{Single delivery at end} & \text{Multiple deliveries, end of each increment} \\
\hline
\text{Customer feedback} & \text{Only at the end} & \text{After every increment} \\
\hline
\text{Risk exposure} & \text{Late discovery of defects} & \text{Risks surfaced and addressed early} \\
\hline
\text{Requirements freeze} & \text{Early, strict} & \text{Per-increment, semi-flexible} \\
\hline
\text{Cost of change} & \text{Very high in later phases} & \text{Moderate, controlled by baseline freezes} \\
\hline
\text{System usability during project} & \text{None — only at the end} & \text{Partial — from Increment 1 onwards} \\
\hline
\text{Time-to-first-value} & \text{Very long} & \text{Relatively short} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Comparison: Incremental vs. Iterative (a classic 3-mark trap question)**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Dimension} & \textbf{Incremental Development} & \textbf{Iterative Development} \\
\hline
\text{Focus} & \text{Adding new \textit{features}} & \text{Refining and improving \textit{existing features}} \\
\hline
\text{Output of each cycle} & \text{A new slice of functionality} & \text{A more complete, refined version} \\
\hline
\text{Analogy} & \text{Adding rooms to a house} & \text{Renovating a house repeatedly} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Pro tip for marks:** When the question asks "distinguish between incremental and iterative", always state explicitly: *Incremental = feature-driven, Iterative = refinement-driven.* Examiners allocate marks to this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incremental Development is the **dominant pattern in modern enterprise software** even outside formally Agile teams. Examples:

- **Operating Systems:** Windows, macOS, and Linux distributions release *major feature updates annually* — each update is effectively an "increment" of the OS.
- **SaaS Platforms:** Salesforce, SAP, and ServiceNow deliver functionality in *quarterly releases* that are increment-based.
- **Embedded Systems:** Automotive infotainment systems, where new driver-assistance features are released *over the vehicle's life cycle* via over-the-air (OTA) updates.

> **Engineering Insight:** The architectural move toward *microservices* has effectively made Incremental Development the *de facto default*, because each microservice is a natural increment boundary.

### 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages (Board-Exam Favourite — 14 Markers)

**Advantages:**
1. **Early delivery of value** — the first usable system is available early.
2. **Reduced risk** — high-risk features are delivered and validated first.
3. **Lower cost of change** — defects in earlier increments are cheaper to fix.
4. **Continuous user engagement** — feedback is collected throughout.
5. **Easier progress monitoring** — measurable, demonstrable progress per increment.
6. **Better resource utilization** — parallel work across increments is feasible.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Architectural rigidity trap** — if the early skeleton is poorly designed, all subsequent increments suffer.
2. **Conflicting requirements across increments** — features in later increments may invalidate assumptions made earlier.
3. **Integration complexity** — each new increment must be cleanly integrated with all prior increments.
4. **Scope creep risk** — without disciplined baseline management, the project can expand endlessly.
5. **Higher up-front cost of planning** — the partition and architectural design must be carefully done *upfront*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: System State Evolution Across Increments

The KTU Board often asks: *"Explain how the system evolves across the increments in the Incremental Development approach."* The standard analytical answer is built around the following stepwise derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the initial state.**

Before Increment 1 begins, only the architectural skeleton exists. The skeleton provides structure but no user-facing business value.

$$
S_{0} = \text{Architectural Skeleton (no business features)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the general increment integration step.**

Each increment $I_{k}$ contains a *coherent, tested, documented* subset of business functionality. When $I_{k}$ is integrated into the existing system $S_{k-1}$, the new system state $S_{k}$ is formed.

$$
S_{k} = S_{k-1} \cup I_{k}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the recurrence explicitly for the first three increments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{1} &= S_{0} \cup I_{1} \quad \text{(Skeleton + Increment 1 features)} \\
S_{2} &= S_{1} \cup I_{2} = S_{0} \cup I_{1} \cup I_{2} \quad \text{(Cumulative system)} \\
S_{3} &= S_{2} \cup I_{3} = S_{0} \cup I_{1} \cup I_{2} \cup I_{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to the $n$-th increment.**

By induction, after $n$ increments, the complete system is:

$$
S_{n} = S_{0} \cup \bigcup_{k=1}^{n} I_{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the constraint that the order of delivery is fixed by priority.**

If increments are ordered by business priority $p$ such that $p(I_{1}) > p(I_{2}) > \dots > p(I_{n})$, then the *value* delivered to the user grows monotonically:

$$
V(S_{k}) > V(S_{k-1}) \quad \forall \; k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}
$$

This monotonicity is what makes the Incremental model *more valuable* than Waterfall, where $V(S) = 0$ until the very end.

**Step 6 — Express the cost-of-change advantage.**

In a Waterfall model, the cost of fixing a defect grows roughly exponentially as the project progresses. In the Incremental model, since each increment undergoes its own validation cycle, the cost of change in increment $k$ is *localized*:

$$
C_{\text{change}}(I_{k}) \ll C_{\text{change}}(S_{\text{full}}) \quad \text{when } k \ll n
$$

This is the formal basis for the KTU Board's claim that *"incremental development reduces the cost of change"*.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose Incremental vs. Waterfall

The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU answer for a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-marker.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Project Characteristic} & \textbf{Choose Waterfall} & \textbf{Choose Incremental} \\
\hline
\text{Requirements are well-understood and stable} & \checkmark &  \\
\hline
\text{Requirements are expected to evolve} &  & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Customer cannot engage frequently} & \checkmark &  \\
\hline
\text{Customer can engage frequently for demos} &  & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Large, complex enterprise system} &  & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Small, short, well-defined project} & \checkmark &  \\
\hline
\text{High technical risk (new architecture)} &  & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Tight regulatory freeze on requirements} & \checkmark &  \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: An Online Banking System (KTU Board Style)

A KTU examiner might present a 7-mark problem like: *"A bank wants to develop an online banking system. Briefly describe how you would plan it as an Incremental Development project, listing the contents of each increment."*

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Skeleton ($S_0$):** Build the basic web portal infrastructure, security framework, database schema, login/logout services, and an empty navigation shell.

**Step 2 — Increment 1 ($I_1$):** Account Inquiry and Profile Management — view balance, view mini-statement, update personal details, change password. *Delivers immediate value to all customers.*

**Step 3 — Increment 2 ($I_2$):** Funds Transfer — internal transfer, NEFT/IMPS, beneficiary management. *Adds transactional capability.*

**Step 4 — Increment 3 ($I_3$):** Bill Payments and Recharges — utility bills, mobile recharge, credit card bill payment.

**Step 5 — Increment 4 ($I_4$):** Loan Services — loan eligibility check, EMI calculator, application tracking.

**Step 6 — Increment 5 ($I_5$):** Customer Support and Analytics — chat support, complaint tracking, personalized insights.

**Step 7 — Validation:** After each increment, the system is integrated, tested, and deployed to a subset of users for feedback. The bank's regulatory reporting is added in the final consolidation phase.

This example demonstrates the KTU-favoured pattern: *start with foundational / high-value features, then add breadth, then add depth*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Process Flow — Incremental Development Lifecycle

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** of an Incremental Development project, from requirements partitioning to the final increment. Every node uses a purely alphanumeric identifier to comply with the KTU-Premier-Engine rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Initiation] --> A2[Requirements Elicitation]
    A2 --> A3[Identify System Architecture]
    A3 --> A4[Decompose Requirements into Increment Set]
    A4 --> A5[Build Skeleton Increment Zero]
    A5 --> B1[Increment 1 Requirements Freeze]
    B1 --> B2[Increment 1 Design]
    B2 --> B3[Increment 1 Code and Test]
    B3 --> B4[Integrate Increment 1 with Skeleton]
    B4 --> B5[Deploy Increment 1 and Gather Feedback]
    B5 --> C1[Increment 2 Requirements Freeze]
    C1 --> C2[Increment 2 Design]
    C2 --> C3[Increment 2 Code and Test]
    C3 --> C4[Integrate Increment 2 with Prior System]
    C4 --> C5[Deploy Increment 2 and Gather Feedback]
    C5 --> D1[Increment N Requirements Freeze]
    D1 --> D2[Increment N Design]
    D2 --> D3[Increment N Code and Test]
    D3 --> D4[Integrate Increment N with Prior System]
    D4 --> D5[Deploy Increment N and Gather Feedback]
    D5 --> E1[Final System Validation and Handover]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decomposition Strategy

This block diagram shows **how a system is partitioned into increments** at the architectural level. The diagram captures the *horizontal (feature-based)* decomposition strategy, which is the most commonly tested variant in KTU questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Complete Requirements] --> B1[Requirements Partitioning Logic]
    B1 --> C1[Increment 1: Core Login and Profile]
    B1 --> C2[Increment 2: Search and Catalog]
    B1 --> C3[Increment 3: Cart and Checkout]
    B1 --> C4[Increment 4: Payment Gateway]
    B1 --> C5[Increment 5: Analytics and Reports]
    C1 --> D1[Integrated System State S1]
    C2 --> D2[Integrated System State S2]
    C3 --> D3[Integrated System State S3]
    C4 --> D4[Integrated System State S4]
    C5 --> D5[Integrated System State S5 Final]
    D1 --> E1[Continuous User Feedback Loop]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Updated Future Increment Requirements]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Waterfall vs. Incremental

The following block diagram offers a side-by-side visualization of how **time-to-value** and **feedback points** differ between the two models. This is a frequent visual component in 14-mark KTU answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WATERFALL
        W1[Requirements] --> W2[Design]
        W2 --> W3[Implementation]
        W3 --> W4[Testing]
        W4 --> W5[Single Delivery at End]
        W5 --> W6[Customer Feedback Late]
    end

    subgraph INCREMENTAL
        I1[Requirements Partition] --> I2[Skeleton Increment 0]
        I2 --> J1[Increment 1: Design Code Test]
        J1 --> J2[Deploy Increment 1]
        J2 --> J3[Customer Feedback 1]
        J3 --> K1[Increment 2: Design Code Test]
        K1 --> K2[Deploy Increment 2]
        K2 --> K3[Customer Feedback 2]
        K3 --> L1[Increment N: Design Code Test]
        L1 --> L2[Deploy Increment N]
        L2 --> L3[Final Handover]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always narrate the diagram in your answer. Do not draw a Mermaid-style block and leave it unexplained. Each block in your diagram must be referenced in the textual answer with a sentence of explanation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1. Define Incremental Development. Mention any two of its advantages.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Incremental Development is a software development approach in which the system is built and delivered in successive functional pieces (increments). Each increment adds a working subset of the overall functionality, and the system becomes progressively more complete.*
>
> *Two advantages:*
> 1. *Early delivery of business value — the first increment provides usable functionality to the customer.*
> 2. *Reduced risk — high-risk features are delivered early and validated before further investment.*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Incremental Development and Iterative Development.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Incremental Development | Iterative Development |
> |---|---|---|
> | Focus | Adds **new features** in each cycle | **Refines and improves** the same features across cycles |
> | Output of cycle | A new functional slice | A more mature version of existing features |
> | Analogy | Adding new rooms to a house | Renovating and redecorating existing rooms repeatedly |
>
> *In practice, modern Agile methods are **both incremental and iterative** — they add new features and refine existing ones in the same Sprint.*

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **any one** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Incremental Development model in detail, with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the *architectural skeleton* and *functional baseline* in the model. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare Incremental Development with the Waterfall Model across any **four** parameters. Mention two scenarios in which the Waterfall Model would still be preferred over Incremental Development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Incremental Development is a plan-driven software process model in which the system is built and delivered in successive, working increments. Each increment delivers a self-contained slice of system functionality.

2. **Process Flow (2 marks):** The complete requirements are first partitioned into logical groups. A *skeleton* (architectural baseline) is built first. Then increments are delivered in priority order, each going through a *mini-Waterfall* of design → implementation → test → integration → deployment. After each increment, the new system is *demonstrated* to stakeholders and feedback is collected.

3. **Role of Architectural Skeleton (2 marks):** The skeleton is the minimal, executable version of the system that establishes the *core architecture* — directory structure, database schema, security layer, build pipeline. Without a well-designed skeleton, integration of later increments becomes painful and the system suffers architectural drift.

4. **Role of Functional Baseline (2 marks):** A functional baseline is a *frozen set of requirements* for a specific increment. It protects the increment from mid-cycle scope creep and enables the team to deliver a tested, demonstrable version. The baseline of an increment can be re-opened *only* in the next increment cycle.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Comparison across 4 parameters (4 marks = 1 mark each):**
   - *Delivery:* Waterfall = single delivery; Incremental = multiple deliveries.
   - *Customer Feedback:* Waterfall = at the end; Incremental = after every increment.
   - *Risk:* Waterfall = late risk discovery; Incremental = early and continuous risk management.
   - *Cost of Change:* Waterfall = very high late; Incremental = controlled and moderate.

2. **Two scenarios favouring Waterfall (2 marks = 1 mark each):**
   - *Short, well-defined projects with stable, frozen requirements* — e.g., a small internal payroll module where requirements are fully understood upfront.
   - *Strict regulatory or contractual environments* where the *process* itself is as important as the product — e.g., safety-critical aerospace software under DO-178C compliance, where each phase must be formally signed off.

3. **Concluding remark (1 mark):** Despite its age, the Waterfall model remains relevant for projects where *stability and traceability* outweigh the need for early value delivery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - Do not confuse *architectural skeleton* with *prototype*. The skeleton is **production-grade architecture with minimal features**, not a throwaway prototype. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> - In part (b), the 7 marks are split as *4 marks for comparison + 2 marks for scenarios + 1 mark for conclusion*. Students who skip the conclusion often lose 1 easy mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q3 Modified]**
>
> **(a)** List and briefly explain the **three architectural strategies** for partitioning a system into increments. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A university wants to develop an **online examination portal** using the Incremental Development approach. Propose **four suitable increments** with clear justification for the ordering, and explain why the *first increment* must always contain the architectural skeleton. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Strategy 1 — Horizontal (Feature-Based) Partitioning (≈2 marks + advantage/limitation):**
   - *Idea:* Each increment delivers a *vertical slice* of all layers (UI, business logic, data) for a chosen feature.
   - *Advantage:* Each increment is end-to-end demonstrable; users see real value quickly.
   - *Limitation:* Cross-cutting concerns (security, logging) may be inconsistent across increments if not centralized in the skeleton.

2. **Strategy 2 — Vertical (Layer-Based) Partitioning (≈2 marks + advantage/limitation):**
   - *Idea:* Each increment delivers a *complete layer* of the system — e.g., first the database, then the API layer, then the UI.
   - *Advantage:* Strong infrastructure is built first; non-functional qualities (performance, security) are established early.
   - *Limitation:* No end-to-end working system is available until several layers are completed; user feedback is delayed.

3. **Strategy 3 — Service-Based Partitioning (≈2 marks + advantage/limitation):**
   - *Idea:* Each increment is a *standalone service* registered in a service bus (e.g., microservices).
   - *Advantage:* Maximum parallelism, independent deployability, and technology heterogeneity per service.
   - *Limitation:* Requires mature DevOps, service governance, and contract discipline; high initial overhead.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Increment 1 — Authentication and User Profile (≈1.5 marks):** Login, role-based access (Student, Faculty, Admin), profile management. *Justification:* The system is meaningless without identity management; it is also the foundation for security and personalization.

2. **Increment 2 — Question Bank and Exam Authoring (≈1.5 marks):** Faculty can create, edit, categorize, and version questions. *Justification:* This is the *content backbone* of the system; without it, no exam can be authored.

3. **Increment 3 — Exam Scheduling and Live Test-Taking (≈1.5 marks):** Schedule exams, time-bound attempts, randomized question selection, auto-submit. *Justification:* This is the *core business functionality* — without it, the system does not deliver its primary value.

4. **Increment 4 — Result Processing, Analytics, and Reporting (≈1.5 marks):** Auto-grading (objective), manual grading (subjective), result publication, analytics dashboards, transcript generation. *Justification:* Closes the *value loop* — students and faculty need to consume exam outcomes.

5. **Justification of the Skeleton (1 mark):** The first increment must contain the architectural skeleton because all later increments depend on the database schema, security layer, deployment pipeline, and modular structure. Without the skeleton, integration of later increments becomes chaotic and the cost of late architectural rework is extremely high.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - Students often propose increments that *overlap* in functionality. Each increment must be **disjoint** in its primary deliverable. Examiners deduct marks for overlap.
> - The justification for *ordering* is mandatory. Marks are awarded for *why* an increment comes before another, not just for *what* it contains.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Incremental Development**

- **Core Definition (must memorize verbatim):** Incremental Development delivers the system as a sequence of *working, functional slices*, each adding a portion of the total functionality.

- **Three Non-Negotiable Pillars:**
  1. *Architectural Skeleton* in Increment 0.
  2. *Mini-Waterfall* inside each increment.
  3. *Functional Baseline* frozen per increment.

- **Three Partitioning Strategies:** Horizontal (feature-based), Vertical (layer-based), Service-based.

- **Incremental vs. Iterative — never confuse:**
  - Incremental = *add new features*.
  - Iterative = *refine existing features*.

- **Five Key Advantages:** Early value, lower risk, lower cost of change, continuous feedback, easier progress tracking.

- **Five Key Disadvantages:** Architectural rigidity, conflicting requirements, integration complexity, scope creep, higher upfront planning cost.

- **Typical Increment Count:** 3 to 6 increments for a plan-driven incremental project (do **not** confuse with Agile Sprint length, which is typically 1–4 weeks).

- **Mandatory Formula (recurrence):** $S_{k} = S_{k-1} \cup I_{k}$, with $S_{0} = \text{Skeleton}$ and final state $S_{n} = S_{0} \cup \bigcup_{k=1}^{n} I_{k}$.

- **Monotonic Value Delivery:** $V(S_{k}) > V(S_{k-1})$ — this is the *defining economic advantage* over Waterfall.

- **Real-World Anchors to Drop in Answers for Extra Credit:** OS feature updates, SaaS quarterly releases, microservices, OTA automotive updates.

- **The "Skeleton Trap":** Examiners *love* testing whether students understand that the skeleton is a *production-grade architecture*, not a prototype. Memorize this distinction.

- **Board Pattern for 14-Markers:** Expect *one part* (7 marks) on conceptual explanation + diagram, and *the other part* (7 marks) on comparison or application-style (scenario-based) question. Always include a diagram, a comparison table, and a 1-line conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Activities - Software Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Process Activities - Software Specification # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Process Activities? In software engineering, every software development process — whether it is the **Waterfall Model**, **Incremental Development**, or an **Agile Methodology** — is fundamentally composed of **four universal process activities** that describe the lifecycle of a software system from its inception to its retirement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Universal Process Activities (KTU Syllabus Highlight)** > 1. **Software Specification** — *What* the system should do > 2. **Software Design and Implementation** — *How* the system is built > 3. **Software Validation** — *Checking* the system against requirements > 4. **Software Evolution** — *Adapting* the system to changing needs These four activities are not strictly sequential; in **Agile Methodologies**, they are interleaved and iterated continuously. ## 1.2 Defining Software Specification > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > **Software Specification** (also called **Requirements Engineering**) is the process of understanding and defining what services are required from a system, and identifying the constraints on the system's operation and development. It is the foundational process activity that answers the question: *"What must the system do, and under what conditions must it operate?"* It is the **most critical phase** because errors introduced during specification propagate through the entire project lifecycle, making them the **most expensive** to fix later (typically **50–200x** the cost if detected post-deployment, per industry studies by IBM Systems Sciences Institute). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" 🏠 Imagine you want to build your **dream house**. Before a single brick is laid: | House Construction | Software Specification Equivalent | |---|---| | Discussing needs with an architect | **Requirements Elicitation** | | Checking if the budget allows it | **Feasibility Study** | | Drafting detailed blueprints | **Requirements Specification** | | Getting blueprints approved by the client | **Requirements Validation** | | Managing changes (e.g., adding a floor later) | **Requirements Management** | > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** Just as a poorly designed blueprint ruins an entire house, a poorly specified software system leads to **costly rework, missed deadlines, and unsatisfied stakeholders**. In Agile, instead of a single big blueprint, you build a **series of small, evolving blueprints** (User Stories) iteratively. ## 1.4 Why Software Specification Matters in Agile In traditional (plan-driven) approaches, specification is a **heavyweight, document-centric** activity performed upfront. In **Agile Methodologies**, the philosophy shifts: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Agile Principle (Beck et al., 2001 — Agile Manifesto):** > *"Working software over comprehensive documentation."* > This does NOT mean **no documentation** — it means *just-enough* specification, captured in lightweight artifacts like **User Stories**, **Use Cases**, and **Acceptance Criteria**, refined continuously through **Backlog Grooming**. ## 1.5 Key Terminology Snapshot - **Functional Requirements (FR):** Services the system must provide (e.g., *"The system shall allow users to reset their password"*). - **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Constraints on the system (e.g., performance, security, usability — often called the **"ilities"**). - **User Requirements:** High-level statements in natural language describing what the user expects. - **System Requirements:** Detailed, structured descriptions of the system's functions, constraints, and interfaces. - **Stakeholder:** Any person or organization affected by the system (users, managers, regulators, developers). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Process Activities — A Closer Look While our primary focus is **Software Specification**, understanding its position in the broader process model is essential. ### Activity 1: Software Specification (Our Focus) This is the **requirements engineering** phase, comprising four sub-activities: 1. **Feasibility Study** (Economic, Technical, Legal, Operational, Schedule feasibility) 2. **Requirements Elicitation & Analysis** (Discovering requirements through stakeholder interaction) 3. **Requirements Specification** (Documenting requirements formally) 4. **Requirements Validation** (Checking requirements for correctness, completeness, consistency) 5. **Requirements Management** (Handling changing requirements) ### Activity 2: Software Design & Implementation Translating the specification into executable software. In Agile, this happens in **Sprints**. ### Activity 3: Software Validation Verification and Validation (V&V) — confirming the system meets the customer's needs. Includes **Unit Testing, Integration Testing, System Testing, Acceptance Testing**. ### Activity 4: Software Evolution Software is inherently flexible and must adapt to changing requirements. Agile is built around this principle. ## 2.2 The Requirements Engineering Process — Detailed Breakdown ### Stage 1: Feasibility Study The **first checkpoint** after a project is proposed. It answers: *"Should we proceed with this project?"* | Feasibility Type | Question Answered | Typical Metric | |---|---|---| | **Economic** | Will the benefits outweigh the costs? | **ROI**, **NPV**, **Payback Period** | | **Technical** | Can it be built with current technology? | Technical risk score, capability matrix | | **Legal** | Does it comply with laws/regulations? | Compliance checklist (e.g., GDPR, IT Act 2000) | | **Operational** | Will it be used by the organization? | User adoption rate, workflow integration | | **Schedule** | Can it be delivered on time? | Critical path analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula: Payback Period** > $$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$ This tells the stakeholder **how long** it will take to recover the initial cost. ### Stage 2: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis This is the **discovery phase** — understanding the *real* (often unstated) needs of stakeholders. **Techniques used:** - **Interviews** (structured/unstructured) - **Questionnaires** & Surveys - **Observation / Ethnography** (shadowing users) - **Workshops / Focus Groups** - **Use Case Analysis** - **Prototyping** (especially in Agile — wireframes, mockups) - **Storyboarding** and **Scenarios** > [!TIP] > **The "Iceberg Model" of Requirements:** What stakeholders initially *say* they want is just the **10%** above the water. The real requirements — edge cases, exceptions, non-functional concerns — are the **90% below**. Skilled analysts dive deep! ### Stage 3: Requirements Specification The activity of **documenting** the requirements in a clear, precise, and verifiable form. | Specification Type | Audience | Format | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | **User Requirements** | End-users, managers | Natural language, User Stories | Agile + Traditional | | **System Requirements** | Developers, testers | Structured document (SRS) | Traditional | | **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** | All stakeholders | IEEE 830/29148 standard | Traditional | | **Product Backlog Items** | Agile teams | User Stories + Acceptance Criteria | Agile (Scrum) | ### Stage 4: Requirements Validation Checks whether the requirements actually define the system the customer wants. **Key Validation Techniques:** - **Reviews & Inspections** (peer review of requirements documents) - **Prototyping** (animated/throwaway prototypes) - **Test-case generation** (deriving tests from requirements) - **Requirements traceability matrix** - **Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)** in Agile > [!NOTE] > **Requirements Validation Checklist (KTU Exam Favorite):** > 1. **Correctness** — Does each requirement reflect what the user needs? > 2. **Unambiguity** — Can it be interpreted in only one way? > 3. **Completeness** — Are all possible scenarios covered? > 4. **Consistency** — Do any requirements conflict? > 5. **Verifiability** — Can the requirement be tested? > 6. **Traceability** — Can we trace each requirement back to its source? > 7. **Modifiability** — Can it be easily changed? ### Stage 5: Requirements Management The process of **managing changes** to requirements as the project evolves. In Agile, this is handled by the **Product Owner** and the **Product Backlog**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Code Table | Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Rule | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **Payback Period** | Time to recover initial investment | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Cost}}{\text{Annual Cash Flow}}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High | | **ROI** | Return on Investment | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High | | **Effort Estimation** (COCOMO basic) | Person-months required | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ | ⭐⭐ Medium | | **Defect Amplification** | Cost multiplier per phase | $E = \dfrac{\text{Cost}_\text{post-release}}{\text{Cost}_\text{requirements}}$ | ⭐⭐ Medium | | **4 Process Activities** | Universal software process | Spec → Design → Validation → Evolution | ⭐⭐⭐ Must know | | **5 Stages of RE** | Requirements Engineering | Feasibility → Elicitation → Specification → Validation → Management | ⭐⭐⭐ Must know | | **5 Feasibility Types** | Project viability check | Economic, Technical, Legal, Operational, Schedule | ⭐⭐⭐ Must know | | **User Story Format** | Agile specification | *As a [user], I want [goal], so that [reason]* | ⭐⭐⭐ Must know | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Questions on **Payback Period** and the **5 Stages of Requirements Engineering** appear in almost every exam cycle. Memorize the order and the definitions precisely. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare IT:** Specification phase ensures patient-data systems comply with **HIPAA/GDPR** before a single line is coded. - **Aerospace:** Specification of **fail-safe** requirements (e.g., DO-178C standard for avionics) is non-negotiable. - **Banking:** Functional specs for **transactional integrity** and **audit trails** begin here. - **Agile Startups:** Lightweight **User Stories** enable **MVP (Minimum Viable Product)** delivery in weeks, not months. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Application: Feasibility Study using Payback Period **Problem:** A startup is considering building a custom CRM tool. - **Initial Investment (I):** ₹10,00,000 (10 Lakhs) - **Expected Annual Cash Inflow (A):** ₹3,00,000 (3 Lakhs) - **Project Life (n):** 5 years - **Required Payback Threshold:** 3 years - **Question:** Is this project economically feasible? ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Identify the variables. $$I = ₹10{,}00{,}000, \quad A = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Payback Period formula. $$\text{Payback Period (PP)} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the values. $$\text{PP} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the result. $$\text{PP} = 3.33 \text{ years}$$ **Step 5:** Compare with threshold. $$\text{PP (3.33 years)} > \text{Threshold (3 years)} \implies \text{Project is NOT economically feasible on a pure payback basis.}$$ **Step 6:** Compute ROI for deeper insight. $$\text{Net Benefit over 5 years} = (3{,}00{,}000 \times 5) - 10{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$ $$\text{ROI} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 50\% \text{ over 5 years}$$ $$\text{Annualized ROI} = \frac{50\%}{5} = 10\% \text{ per year}$$ > [!TIP] > **Decision Insight:** Although payback period is 3.33 years, the **10% annualized ROI** might be acceptable depending on the company's hurdle rate. The feasibility study synthesizes **multiple metrics**, not just one. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Application: Requirements Defect Cost Amplification The **IBM Systems Sciences Institute** found that the cost of fixing a defect increases dramatically as it propagates through phases. This is a classic **KTU 14-mark** topic. | Phase Detected | Relative Cost to Fix (Multiplier) | |---|---| | Requirements (Specification) | **1×** (baseline) | | Design | **3–6×** | | Coding | **10×** | | Integration Testing | **15–40×** | | System Testing | **30–70×** | | Post-Release / Production | **40–1000×** | **Worked Example:** A defect is introduced in the **Specification Phase** at a cost of ₹1,000 to fix. **Step 1:** If detected in **Design** phase: $$C_{\text{design}} = 1{,}000 \times 3 = ₹3{,}000$$ **Step 2:** If detected in **Coding** phase: $$C_{\text{coding}} = 1{,}000 \times 10 = ₹10{,}000$$ **Step 3:** If detected in **Post-Release**: $$C_{\text{post}} = 1{,}000 \times 100 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 4:** Compute the **amplification factor** from spec to post-release: $$\text{Amplification Factor} = \frac{C_{\text{post}}}{C_{\text{spec}}} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}000} = 100\times$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A defect in the **specification** phase — the very topic of this module — can become **100 times more expensive** to fix if it slips to production. This is why **thorough specification and validation** is a non-negotiable engineering discipline. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Agile vs. Traditional Specification | Dimension | Traditional (Plan-Driven) | Agile (Iterative) | |---|---|---| | **Specification Artifact** | SRS Document (IEEE 830) | Product Backlog + User Stories | | **Timing** | Upfront (Big Design Up Front) | Continuous (Backlog Refinement) | | **Change Handling** | Formal change control board | Welcomed; part of Sprint cycle | | **Stakeholder Involvement** | Periodic reviews | Continuous collaboration | | **Documentation Level** | Heavy, comprehensive | Lightweight, "just enough" | | **Validation Method** | Inspections, walkthroughs | Acceptance Tests (ATDD/BDD) | | **Best For** | Large, regulated, safety-critical systems | Dynamic, evolving, user-facing products | | **Risk Profile** | Late discovery of requirements errors | Continuous risk management | ## 3.4 The User Story Format — Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1:** Identify the **Actor** (user role). $$\text{Actor: "registered customer"}$$ **Step 2:** Identify the **Goal** (desired outcome). $$\text{Goal: "track my order status"}$$ **Step 3:** Identify the **Business Value** (reason). $$\text{Value: "so I can plan my day around the delivery"}$$ **Step 4:** Combine using the canonical template. $$\boxed{\text{As a [actor], I want [goal], so that [value].}}$$ **Step 5:** Add **Acceptance Criteria** (Given–When–Then format, BDD). $$\text{Given: the customer is logged in}$$ $$\text{When: the customer clicks "Track Order"}$$ $$\text{Then: the current order status is displayed within 2 seconds}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Acceptance Criteria transform a *vague* user story into a *testable* requirement — bridging the gap between specification and validation. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Four Universal Process Activities This block diagram illustrates the **four fundamental activities** of any software process, with Software Specification highlighted as the focus of this module. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT: Stakeholder Needs and Business Goals] --> B B[ACTIVITY 1: SOFTWARE SPECIFICATION
What should the system do?] B --> C C[ACTIVITY 2: SOFTWARE DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION
How is the system built?] C --> D D[ACTIVITY 3: SOFTWARE VALIDATION
Does the system meet requirements?] D --> E E[ACTIVITY 4: SOFTWARE EVOLUTION
How does the system adapt to change?] E --> F F[OUTPUT: Deployed, Maintained Software System] E -.Feedback Loop.-> B D -.Defect Feedback.-> C C -.Spec Update.-> B style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style F fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Requirements Engineering Process (Five Stages) This flowchart decomposes the **Software Specification activity** into its five core stages, showing inputs, outputs, and iterative feedback. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Problem Definition and Scope] --> S1 S1[STAGE 1: FEASIBILITY STUDY
Economic, Technical, Legal, Operational, Schedule] --> D1 D1{Feasible?} -->|No| STOP1[Project Halted: Document Findings] D1 -->|Yes| S2 S2[STAGE 2: REQUIREMENTS ELICITATION AND ANALYSIS
Interviews, Workshops, Observation, Prototyping] --> S3 S3[STAGE 3: REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION
SRS Document, User Stories, Use Cases] --> S4 S4[STAGE 4: REQUIREMENTS VALIDATION
Reviews, Prototyping, Acceptance Tests] --> D2 D2{Validated?} -->|No| S2 D2 -->|Yes| S5 S5[STAGE 5: REQUIREMENTS MANAGEMENT
Change Control, Traceability] --> OUT1[Approved Requirements Baseline] S5 -.Continuous Updates.-> S3 style S1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style S3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style S4 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style S5 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style OUT1 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style STOP1 fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Requirements Elicitation Techniques (Block Architecture) A functional block-level architecture showing the **inputs**, **elicitation techniques**, **processing**, and **outputs** of the elicitation stage. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUTS A1[Stakeholders: Users, Managers, Regulators] A2[Domain Documents: Manuals, Policies, Legacy Systems] A3[Business Goals and Constraints] end subgraph ELICITATION_TECHNIQUES B1[Interviews and Questionnaires] B2[Observation and Ethnography] B3[Workshops and Focus Groups] B4[Prototyping and Scenarios] B5[Use Case Analysis] end subgraph PROCESSING C1[Analyst Synthesizes and Categorizes] C2[Identifies Functional and Non-Functional Needs] C3[Resolves Conflicts and Negotiates Priorities] end subgraph OUTPUTS D1[Documented User Requirements] D2[Initial Use Case Diagrams] D3[User Stories and Personas] end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A2 --> B3 A2 --> B4 A3 --> B5 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C2 B5 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> D1 C3 --> D2 C3 --> D3 style C1 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style C2 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style C3 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Specification Lifecycle in Agile (Sprint-Based) This diagram shows how **Software Specification** operates inside an Agile Sprint, contrasting it with the traditional upfront approach. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Product Vision] --> B[Product Backlog: Prioritized User Stories] B --> C1[Sprint Planning: Select Stories for Sprint] C1 --> D1[Sprint Backlog: Refined Stories with Acceptance Criteria] D1 --> E1[Development: Build and Test] E1 --> F1[Sprint Review: Demonstrate to Stakeholders] F1 --> G1[Sprint Retrospective: Improve Process] G1 -.Feedback.-> B F1 -.New Requirements.-> B subgraph TRADITIONAL_APPROACH X1[Big SRS Document] --> X2[Design] --> X3[Code] --> X4[Test] --> X5[Deliver] end style D1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 style E1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 style F1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the Diagram:** Notice how in Agile, **Specification (refining the user story with acceptance criteria)**, **Design**, **Implementation**, and **Validation** all happen **inside the same Sprint**. The traditional approach performs all specification *before* any design or coding. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Software Specification. List any two techniques used for requirements elicitation.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Software Specification** is the process of understanding and defining what services are required from a system, and identifying the constraints on its operation and development. It is the first of the four universal process activities (Specification, Design & Implementation, Validation, Evolution) and forms the foundation of the entire software engineering process. **Two Requirements Elicitation Techniques:** 1. **Interviews:** The analyst conducts structured or unstructured conversations with stakeholders to discover their needs, expectations, and constraints. *Example:* Interviewing bank tellers to understand ATM software requirements. 2. **Observation (Ethnography):** The analyst observes users in their actual working environment to uncover *tacit* (unstated) requirements. *Example:* Shadowing a nurse in a hospital to find workflow bottlenecks for a new patient-management system. *(Other valid techniques: Questionnaires, Workshops, Prototyping, Use Case Analysis, Scenarios — any two accepted.)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Statements of *what* the system must do — its features and behavior. | Constraints on *how* the system performs — its qualities. | | **Focus** | Services, functions, business rules. | Performance, security, usability, reliability. | | **Verification** | Through functional/black-box tests. | Often through metrics, benchmarking, and load tests. | | **Example** | *"The system shall allow the user to reset their password via email."* | *"The password reset email must be delivered within 30 seconds."* | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Functional requirements describe **verbs/actions** (login, calculate, generate), while non-functional requirements describe **qualities/constraints** (fast, secure, available, portable). Both are equally critical. --- ## 📝 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **four universal process activities** of a software process in detail. (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **five stages of the Requirements Engineering (Software Specification) Process**. (7 Marks) --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Question A: #### (a) The Four Universal Process Activities (7 Marks) Every software engineering process — whether traditional (Waterfall) or Agile — is built on four universal activities: **1. Software Specification (1.5 Marks)** The process of understanding and defining *what* services the system should provide and the constraints under which it must operate. It is the foundation for all subsequent activities. Outputs: User Requirements, System Requirements, Software Requirements Specification (SRS), User Stories (Agile). **2. Software Design and Implementation (1.5 Marks)** The process of *developing* the software system — converting the specification into an executable system. - *Design:* Architectural design (overall structure) + Detailed design (per-module logic). - *Implementation:* Translating design into code (programming), and unit testing of individual modules. - *In Agile:* Happens within each Sprint. **3. Software Validation (2 Marks)** Verification and Validation (V&V) — checking that the system conforms to its specification and meets the customer's needs. - **Verification:** *"Are we building the product right?"* (Does it match the spec?) - **Validation:** *"Are we building the right product?"* (Does it meet user needs?) - Activities: Unit Testing → Integration Testing → System Testing → Acceptance Testing. - **V-Model:** Each development stage has a corresponding test stage. **4. Software Evolution (2 Marks)** Software is inherently flexible and must adapt to changing requirements, technologies, and environments.
