This phase covers: - Maintenance (corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive). - Major rewrites or replacements. - **In Agile:** Continuous evolution via Sprint feedback loops. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Always mention **how each activity applies in BOTH traditional AND Agile** to score full marks. Modern KTU papers reward comparative answers. --- #### (b) The Five Stages of Requirements Engineering (7 Marks) **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Problem Definition and Scope] --> B[Stage 1: Feasibility Study] B --> C[Stage 2: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis] C --> D[Stage 3: Requirements Specification] D --> E[Stage 4: Requirements Validation] E --> F[Stage 5: Requirements Management] F --> G[Approved Requirements Baseline] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 style B fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style C fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style D fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style E fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style F fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style G fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` **Detailed Explanation of Each Stage (1.4 Marks each):** **Stage 1 — Feasibility Study:** Determines whether the proposed system is *worth* developing. Five types are evaluated: **Economic** (cost-benefit, ROI, payback), **Technical** (can it be built?), **Legal** (regulatory compliance), **Operational** (will the organization use it?), and **Schedule** (can it be delivered on time?). A feasibility report is the deliverable. **Stage 2 — Requirements Elicitation and Analysis:** This is the *discovery* phase where analysts work with stakeholders to understand their real needs. Techniques include **interviews, observation, workshops, prototyping, and use case analysis**. Outputs: a set of *informal* (unstructured) requirements ready to be formalized. **Stage 3 — Requirements Specification:** The requirements discovered in Stage 2 are documented as a **precise specification**. Two levels exist: **User Requirements** (high-level, natural language for end-users) and **System Requirements** (detailed, structured, for developers). The document produced is the **SRS** (per IEEE 830/29148 standard) or, in Agile, refined **User Stories** with **Acceptance Criteria**. **Stage 4 — Requirements Validation:** Checks that the requirements *actually* define the system the customer wants. Properties checked: **correctness, completeness, consistency, unambiguity, verifiability, traceability**. Techniques: **peer reviews, walkthroughs, prototyping, and test-case generation**. In Agile, validation is continuous via **Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)** and **Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)**. **Stage 5 — Requirements Management:** Handles *changes* to requirements throughout the project. In a traditional project, a **Change Control Board (CCB)** reviews and approves modifications. In Agile, the **Product Owner** manages the **Product Backlog** continuously, reprioritizing as feedback arrives. Traceability matrices ensure each requirement can be traced back to its origin and forward to design, code, and tests. --- ### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]` **Q. (a)** What is a **Feasibility Study**? Explain the different types of feasibility with examples. (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** Discuss the **techniques used for Requirements Elicitation** in detail. How do elicitation techniques differ between traditional and agile approaches? (7 Marks) --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Question B: #### (a) Feasibility Study (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** A Feasibility Study is a **preliminary investigation** conducted at the start of a software project to assess whether the proposed system is *practical, viable, and worth the investment*. It is the first step of the Requirements Engineering process and the first decision gate in a software project. **Why is it important?** (1 Mark) - Avoids costly mistakes by identifying non-viable projects early. - Helps management make a **go / no-go** decision. - Provides a high-level estimate of cost, time, and benefits. **Types of Feasibility (6 types, 1 Mark each for the first 5, 1 Mark for examples):** | Type | Question Answered | Example | |---|---|---| | **1. Economic Feasibility** | Will the financial benefits outweigh the costs? | A CRM system costing ₹10 Lakh with ₹3 Lakh annual benefit has a payback of 3.33 years — *marginally feasible*. | | **2. Technical Feasibility** | Can the system be built with current technology and skills? | Implementing an AI-based medical diagnosis tool may not be technically feasible if the team lacks ML expertise. | | **3. Legal Feasibility** | Does the system comply with laws and regulations? | A payment system must comply with **RBI guidelines** and the **IT Act 2000**; non-compliance leads to legal rejection. | | **4. Operational Feasibility** | Will the system be used effectively by the target users? | A complex inventory system may fail if shop-floor staff lack the training to operate it. | | **5. Schedule Feasibility** | Can the system be delivered within the required timeline? | A 12-month project proposal may not be feasible if market conditions demand delivery in 6 months. | **Deliverable:** A **Feasibility Report** with recommendations — *Proceed / Modify / Abandon*. --- #### (b) Requirements Elicitation Techniques (7 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Requirements Elicitation is the process of **discovering, gathering, and understanding** the needs and constraints of stakeholders for a software system. It is often the most challenging phase because users may not know what they want, may express needs unclearly, or may have conflicting requirements. **Traditional Elicitation Techniques (3.5 Marks):** 1. **Interviews** — One-on-one conversations; can be *structured* (fixed questions), *semi-structured*, or *unstructured* (open-ended). Most direct technique, but time-consuming and may miss group dynamics. 2. **Questionnaires/Surveys** — Distributed to many stakeholders; cost-effective for large user bases but low response rates. 3. **Observation (Ethnography)** — Analyst watches users in their natural environment. Reveals *tacit* requirements and workflow realities that users themselves cannot articulate. 4. **Document Analysis** — Studying existing manuals, forms, legacy systems, and regulations to extract implied requirements. 5. **Workshops/Focus Groups** — Group sessions with diverse stakeholders to brainstorm and prioritize needs jointly. **Agile Elicitation Techniques (3.5 Marks):** In Agile Methodologies, elicitation is **continuous, collaborative, and lightweight**: 1. **User Stories** — Captured in the format: *As a [user], I want [goal], so that [reason].* Owned by the **Product Owner**, who elicits them from stakeholders continuously. 2. **Backlog Grooming (Refinement)** — Ongoing meeting where the team collaborates with the Product Owner to clarify, estimate, and re-prioritize backlog items. 3. **Story Mapping** — Visual arrangement of user stories into a narrative flow of user activities, helping to identify gaps and dependencies. 4. **Personas** — Fictional archetypes representing key user types; help the team empathize with and elicit needs for specific user groups. 5. **Acceptance Criteria (Given–When–Then)** — Co-created with stakeholders to specify when a user story is "done" — also serves as a validation tool. 6. **Customer Demos & Sprint Reviews** — Live demonstrations elicit *new* requirements via stakeholder feedback, feeding the next Sprint's backlog. **Key Differences (Bonus Point, 1 Mark):** | Aspect | Traditional | Agile | |---|---|---| | **Frequency** | One-time, upfront | Continuous, per Sprint | | **Stakeholder Role** | Interviewees | Active collaborators | | **Documentation** | Heavy interview transcripts, minutes | Lightweight user stories | | **Output** | Comprehensive requirements catalog | Prioritized product backlog | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "Verification" with "Validation":** Verification = *"Are we building the product right?"* (spec match). Validation = *"Are we building the right product?"* (user need match). This is a **5-mark trap** in Part B questions. > 2. **Forgetting Non-Functional Requirements:** Most students list only functional requirements. Always mention the **"ilities"** (usability, reliability, performance, security, maintainability). > 3. **Writing "User Requirements" and "System Requirements" as if they are the same:** They are NOT. User Requirements = high-level, natural language. System Requirements = detailed, structured, technical. > 4. **Omitting the Agile perspective:** KTU 2024 Scheme *specifically* tests Agile Methodologies. Always contrast traditional with agile for full marks. > 5. **Skipping the Feasibility Study:** Many students jump directly to "elicitation" — but feasibility comes **first** in the order. > 6. **Forgetting the formula in the Feasibility question:** If a numerical question is asked, you *must* show the **Payback Period formula** and the **ROI formula**. Skipping the formula = losing 2–3 marks. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize This Before the Exam:** > > ✅ The **four universal process activities** are: **Specification → Design & Implementation → Validation → Evolution.** > > ✅ Software Specification is also called **Requirements Engineering (RE)**. > > ✅ The **five stages of RE** are: **Feasibility Study → Elicitation & Analysis → Specification → Validation → Management.** > > ✅ The **five types of feasibility** are: **Economic, Technical, Legal, Operational, Schedule** (mnemonic: **E-T-L-O-S**). > > ✅ Key formulas: **Payback Period = Initial Investment / Annual Cash Inflow** and **ROI = (Net Benefit / Cost) × 100%**. > > ✅ **Functional Requirements** = *what* the system does. **Non-Functional Requirements** = *how well* it does it. > > ✅ **User Requirements** are written for end-users (natural language). **System Requirements** are written for developers (structured). > > ✅ In Agile, specification is **continuous, lightweight, and captured as User Stories** with **Acceptance Criteria**. > > ✅ The **canonical User Story format** is: *"As a [user], I want [goal], so that [value]."* > > ✅ **Defect cost amplification:** A specification defect fixed post-release can be **100×** more expensive than fixing it during specification. > > ✅ Always **validate requirements** for: **Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, Traceability, Modifiability** (7 properties). > > ✅ In Agile, the **Product Owner** is responsible for the Product Backlog and requirement prioritization. > > ✅ The **SRS** document follows the **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** standard.

topics.title: Software Design and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Design and Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Design and Implementation** in the Agile context refers to the **iterative, evolutionary, and collaborative process** of translating user requirements into working software through continuous cycles of planning, designing, coding, testing, and refactoring. Unlike traditional predictive methodologies that demand a **Big Design Up Front (BDUF)**, Agile design embraces **emergent design**, where the system's architecture and detailed design evolve incrementally across iterations, driven by **working software**, **customer feedback**, and **resilience to change**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Design in Agile is not an isolated phase — it is a **continuous activity** woven into every iteration. The **4 core values** and **12 principles** of the Agile Manifesto (2001) form the philosophical backbone: *"Working software over comprehensive documentation"* and *"Responding to change over following a plan."*

The key engineering activities that constitute Design and Implementation in Agile include:

- **Iterative Architecture & Design** (Iteration 0 spike + emergent design)
- **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** — Red → Green → Refactor
- **Refactoring** for code quality without changing behavior
- **Continuous Integration (CI)** and **Continuous Deployment (CD)**
- **Pair Programming** and **Collective Code Ownership**
- **Design Simplification** via SOLID, DRY, KISS, YAGNI principles
- **Spike Solutions** for technical risk reduction

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 Real-World Analogy: Building a Pizza vs. Cooking a 5-Course Meal**

| Approach | Traditional (BDUF) | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooking Style** | 5-course meal — full menu planned months in advance, every ingredient pre-bought | Pizza — start with a base, add toppings **just-in-time** as customers order |
| **Customer Feedback** | Served only at the end | Tasted at every iteration, recipe adjusted instantly |
| **Failure Cost** | If the customer dislikes a dish, the entire meal is wasted | If a topping is rejected, you simply swap it for the next pizza |

**The Pizza Shop is Agile**:
- You don't buy 10 kg of mushrooms before knowing what the customer wants.
- You make one pizza, serve it, learn, and improve the next one.
- The dough (architecture) is prepared once (Iteration 0), but the toppings (detailed design) emerge per iteration.

This is precisely how Agile teams handle software: a **lightweight architectural skeleton** is established up front (to avoid chaos), and the **detailed design emerges** through the iterations as knowledge deepens.

## 1.3 Critical Terminology Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Key Terms Every KTU Student Must Memorize**

- **BDUF (Big Design Up Front)** — A heavy, front-loaded design phase; explicitly rejected by Agile.
- **Emergent Design** — Design that *arises* from the codebase itself over time, guided by principles and refactoring.
- **Iteration 0 / Sprint 0** — A short, time-boxed preparatory phase to set up the architecture, toolchain, and initial backlog.
- **Spike** — A time-boxed experiment (typically 1–4 days) to explore a technical unknown before committing to a design.
- **Refactoring** — Restructuring existing code **without changing its external behavior** to improve non-functional attributes.
- **Technical Debt** — The implicit cost of future rework caused by choosing an easy (limited) solution today.
- **Collective Code Ownership** — Any developer on the team is *authorized* and *expected* to modify any part of the code.
- **YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)** — Do not add functionality until it is *actually* required.
- **Sustainable Pace** — Teams should work at a pace that can be maintained indefinitely (Manifesto Principle 8).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-weightage, high-scoring area** in the PEAIT413 syllabus because it tests the candidate's understanding of the **practical engineering backbone** of Agile. Expect direct questions on:
- Differences between Agile and traditional design approaches
- SOLID principles and their application
- The role of TDD, refactoring, and CI/CD
- Architectural decisions in Iteration 0
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agile Design Philosophy — "Just Enough, Just In Time"

The central dogma of Agile design is captured by **Ron Jeffries' Three Rules of Simple Design** (widely tested):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ron Jeffries' Three Rules (in order of priority)**
> 1. The code **passes all tests** (verified behavior).
> 2. The code **reveals intention** (clear, expressive naming).
> 3. The code **contains no duplication** (DRY).
> 4. The code **contains the fewest number of classes and methods** possible.

Any rule violation must be justified by a higher-priority rule.

## 2.2 The SOLID Principles — Decomposed

The **SOLID** acronym (coined by Robert C. Martin — *Uncle Bob*) is the **single most-asked design principle set** in KTU Agile papers.

### **S — Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**
*A class should have **only one reason to change**.*  
Every class/module must serve **one actor** (one stakeholder). If a `Report` class is being changed by both the *Finance Department* and the *Marketing Department*, it violates SRP. Solution: split it into `FinancialReport` and `MarketingReport`.

### **O — Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**
*Software entities should be **open for extension** but **closed for modification**.*  
Add new behavior by writing **new code** (subclasses, strategy implementations) rather than editing existing, tested code. Achieved via **polymorphism, interfaces, and design patterns like Strategy**.

### **L — Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**
*Objects of a superclass shall be **replaceable** with objects of a subclass **without breaking** the application.*  
A subclass must honor the contract of its parent. The classic violation: a `Square` extending `Rectangle` where setting width doesn't independently set height.

### **I — Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)**
*Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do **not use**.*  
Prefer many **small, role-specific interfaces** over one fat general-purpose interface. Example: split a fat `IMachine` interface into `IPrinter`, `IScanner`, `IFax`.

### **D — Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**
*High-level modules must **not** depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on **abstractions**.*  
Depend on interfaces/abstract classes, not on concrete classes. This is the foundation of **Dependency Injection (DI)** frameworks.

## 2.3 The Supporting "Agile Axioms"

| Acronym | Full Form | Meaning | Example Violation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DRY** | Don't Repeat Yourself | Every piece of knowledge has a single, authoritative representation | Copy-pasting the same tax-calculation logic in 5 files |
| **KISS** | Keep It Simple, Stupid | Most systems work best when kept simple, not complex | Writing a custom state machine for a 2-state toggle |
| **YAGNI** | You Aren't Gonna Need It | Always implement things when you *actually* need them | Building a multi-tenant architecture for a single-user app |
| **BDUF** | Big Design Up Front (anti-pattern) | Exhaustive upfront design for months | Spending 6 months on UML before writing a line of code |

## 2.4 Emergent Design — The Engine

Emergent design is **not unplanned design**; it is **disciplined, principles-driven design that grows over time**.

**The 4-Stage Emergent Design Cycle:**

1. **Sketch** — Initial rough design using CRC cards, whiteboards, or simple UML. *Intent*: communicate, not specify.
2. **Implement** — Write the simplest code that could possibly work, driven by TDD.
3. **Refactor** — Continuously improve the design while keeping tests green.
4. **Crystallize** — Patterns and abstractions naturally emerge; document them as architectural decisions (ADRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Emergent design is used by leading tech organizations (Spotify, Netflix, Amazon) because their domains (streaming, recommendations) change so rapidly that any BDUF-based architecture would be obsolete within months. By letting the design emerge, the codebase stays lean and adaptable.

## 2.5 The Role of Iteration 0 / Sprint 0

**Iteration 0 is a critical concept for KTU exams.** It is a *short* (typically 1–2 weeks) preparatory iteration that establishes:

- Initial high-level architectural skeleton (the "shape" of the system)
- Development environment & toolchain setup
- Initial Product Backlog grooming
- Initial risk list and spike experiments
- CI/CD pipeline scaffold

**Critical Rule:** Iteration 0 must **NOT** become a hidden waterfall phase. If it exceeds 2–3 sprints, the team has slipped into BDUF.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Implementation Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 At-a-Glance Reference for Exam Day**

| Metric / Concept | Definition / Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Debt Ratio** | $\text{TDR} = \dfrac{\text{Cost to Fix}}{\text{Cost to Build}}$ | Quantifies "messy" code. Ideal: < **5%** |
| **Code Coverage** | $\text{CVC} = \dfrac{\text{Lines Executed by Tests}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100$ | TDD target: **80–100%** for critical modules |
| **Refactoring Safety** | Must preserve behavior: $\text{Output}_{\text{before}} \equiv \text{Output}_{\text{after}}$ | Verified by **regression test suite** |
| **YAGNI Cost Function** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{unused}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i + M_i) \times P(\text{never used})$ | Where $D_i$ = dev cost, $M_i$ = maintenance cost |
| **Sprint Velocity** | $\text{V} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{SP}_i}{n}$ | Story points completed per sprint |
| **Pair Programming ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Defect Reduction} + \text{Knowledge Transfer}}{\text{1.15} \times \text{Solo Time}}$ | Industry empirical factor: **~15%** overhead |
| **CI Pipeline Frequency** | $f_{\text{CI}} \geq 1$ integration per developer per day | Avoids "Integration Hell" |
| **TDD Red→Green→Refactor** | Time distribution: $\sim 30\% \text{R} + 50\% \text{G} + 20\% \text{Rf}$ | Empirical; varies by domain |

**Boundary Conditions / Acceptance Criteria:**
- $f_{\text{CI}} \geq 1$ per developer per day (Kent Beck)
- Refactoring must be **behavior-preserving** (no functional change)
- SOLID violations: $\geq 1$ violation per 100 LOC triggers a design review
- Spikes are strictly time-boxed (typically **1–4 days max**)

## 2.7 The Implementation Phase — Detailed Breakdown

The Implementation phase in Agile is **multi-disciplinary and continuous**. It is not a single activity but a **set of intertwined practices**:

| Practice | Primary Purpose | Frequency | Tooling Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TDD** | Drive design via tests | Every feature | JUnit, pytest, Mocha |
| **Refactoring** | Improve design | Continuous | IDE refactoring, Martin Fowler catalog |
| **Pair Programming** | Knowledge sharing, real-time review | 50–100% of time | Tuples, screen sharing |
| **Code Reviews** | Catch defects, enforce standards | Per pull request | GitHub PR, GitLab MR |
| **CI/CD** | Early defect detection, fast feedback | Per commit | Jenkins, GitHub Actions |
| **Collective Ownership** | No silos, high bus-factor | Always | Git, clear coding standards |
| **Spike Solutions** | Reduce technical risk | Pre-implementation | Throw-away prototypes |
| **Storybook / Living Docs** | Document UI components | Per component | Storybook, Confluence |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Agile Methodologies* is a **process and design engineering course**, the "derivations" here take the form of **step-by-step methodological workflows**, **principle application logic**, and **decision matrices**. All mathematical expressions are included where required by the KTU formula sheet in §2.6.

---

## 3.1 Worked Walkthrough #1 — Applying the TDD Red → Green → Refactor Cycle

**Scenario:** A retail app must compute the final price of a product after applying a 10% discount.

**Step 1: Write a Failing Test (RED phase)**
The developer writes the *smallest* possible test that expresses the desired behavior. The test **must fail** because the code does not exist yet.

> **Test logic:** Given a product price of ₹1000, when the discount is applied, then the final price should be ₹900.

**Conversion Logic (English → Code Intent):**
- Input domain: $P \in \mathbb{R}^+$
- Discount rate: $d = 0.10$
- Expected output: $P_{\text{final}} = P \times (1 - d) = 1000 \times 0.90 = 900$

**Step 2: Make the Test Pass (GREEN phase)**
Write the **simplest possible code** that makes the test pass — even if it's not elegant.

The developer's only goal here is to turn the red light green. The "simplest" implementation may be as naive as returning a hard-coded value initially, but is then generalized to handle the parameter.

**Step 3: Refactor (REFACTOR phase)**
Now improve the code's internal structure:
- Rename variables for clarity (`price` instead of `p`).
- Extract the discount logic into a reusable method `applyDiscount(price, rate)`.
- Eliminate duplication (DRY).
- **Constraint:** All existing tests must still pass. The external behavior is unchanged: $\text{Output}_{\text{before}} = \text{Output}_{\text{after}}$.

**Step 4: Repeat for the next test case** (e.g., boundary case: 0% discount, 100% discount, negative price validation).

**Why this matters for KTU:** TDD embodies *Iterative Design* — every test forces a tiny design decision. The codebase's architecture is **discovered**, not invented. This is the antithesis of BDUF.

---

## 3.2 Worked Walkthrough #2 — Identifying a SOLID Violation

**Scenario:** A KTU exam question presents the following class design and asks: *"Identify which SOLID principle is violated and refactor."*

A single class `Employee` is doing:
- Calculating payroll (Finance Dept concern)
- Generating performance reports (HR Dept concern)
- Saving to database (IT Ops concern)

**Step-by-Step Analysis (the KTU valuation expects this exact logic):**

**Step 1: Identify the actors.**  
Finance, HR, and IT Ops are three distinct stakeholders → three distinct reasons to change.

**Step 2: Match to a SOLID principle.**  
The class has multiple responsibilities that align with different actors → **SRP (Single Responsibility Principle)** is violated.

**Step 3: Apply the refactoring.**

**Step 4: Verify the refactor.**  
- Each new class has **one** reason to change.
- The OCP is also implicitly improved — adding a new department's report doesn't require modifying the existing classes.

---

## 3.3 Worked Walkthrough #3 — Computing Technical Debt Ratio (TDR)

**Given:**
- Cost to rebuild the system from scratch: $C_{\text{build}} = \text{₹} 1{,}00{,}000$
- Estimated cost to fix all known code-quality issues: $C_{\text{fix}} = \text{₹} 12{,}000$

**Find:** Technical Debt Ratio (TDR).

**Step 1: Recall the formula.**

$$
\text{TDR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fix}}}{C_{\text{build}}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{TDR} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$
\text{TDR} = 0.12 = 12\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result (KTU expects this!).**
- Industry standard (per SonarQube): maintain TDR $\leq 5\%$ for green, $5\% \text{–} 10\%$ for yellow, $> 10\%$ for red.
- $12\% \Rightarrow$ **RED** — immediate refactoring sprint is required.
- **Action item:** Allocate capacity in the next sprint to reduce TDR below 5%.

---

## 3.4 Worked Walkthrough #4 — The Emergent Design Decision Matrix

When a developer faces a design choice in an Agile iteration, the following decision matrix guides them:

| Decision Criteria | Heuristic / Formula | KTU-Expected Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Should I build a new abstraction now? | Apply **YAGNI**: build only if a *concrete* use case exists today | If no use case → **do not build** |
| Should I refactor this code? | Check **3 Rules of Simple Design** (tests, intention, no duplication) | Violation count $\geq 1$ → refactor |
| Should I add a new pattern? | Apply **Rule of Three** — wait until pattern appears 3 times | Third occurrence → extract pattern |
| Should I use inheritance or composition? | **"Favor composition over inheritance"** (GoF principle) | Choose composition unless Liskov-safe |
| Is this design testable? | TDD-first check: can I write a test *before* the code? | If no → redesign |

---

## 3.5 Worked Walkthrough #5 — Sprint Velocity & Capacity Planning

**Given:**
- Team completed **30, 28, 32, 35** story points in the last 4 sprints.

**Find:** Expected velocity and capacity for the next sprint.

**Step 1: Compute average velocity.**

$$
\bar{V} = \dfrac{30 + 28 + 32 + 35}{4} = \dfrac{125}{4} = 31.25 \text{ SP}
$$

**Step 2: Round to a whole number (Story Points are integers).**

$$
V_{\text{commit}} = 31 \text{ SP}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the 80% rule for sustainable commitment.**

$$
V_{\text{safe}} = 0.80 \times \bar{V} = 0.80 \times 31.25 = 25 \text{ SP}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**  
Commit the team to ~25 SP to leave 20% buffer for spillovers, defects, and technical debt repayment.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Agile vs. Traditional Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is one of the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize this matrix.**

| Dimension | Traditional (BDUF) | Agile (Emergent Design) |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Timing** | Complete before coding | Continuous, evolves per iteration |
| **Documentation** | Heavy (full UML, specs) | Lightweight (just enough, living docs) |
| **Change Response** | Expensive (change requests) | Welcomed (embraces change) |
| **Customer Involvement** | Mostly at start & end | Daily / per iteration |
| **Risk Profile** | Late discovery | Early & continuous mitigation |
| **Architecture** | Predicted, fixed | **Iteration 0 skeleton + emergent** |
| **Test Strategy** | Big-bang at the end | TDD, continuous testing |
| **Delivery** | Single, at project end | Frequent, working increments |
| **Feedback Loop** | Months | Days to weeks |
| **Failure Cost** | High (late) | Low (early) |

## 3.7 Design Decision Records (ADR) — The KTU Hidden Gem

A modern Agile team captures every significant design decision in a lightweight document called an **Architectural Decision Record (ADR)**. An ADR contains:

- **Title** — short, declarative ("We will use PostgreSQL for primary storage")
- **Status** — Proposed / Accepted / Deprecated / Superseded
- **Context** — What is the issue we're addressing?
- **Decision** — What did we choose?
- **Consequences** — Trade-offs, both positive and negative

This is the Agile answer to the BDUF problem: **design knowledge is captured, not over-engineered**.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Emergent Design. How is it different from Big Design Up Front (BDUF)?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Emergent Design** is an Agile design philosophy where the system's architecture and detailed design *evolve incrementally* across iterations rather than being fully specified upfront. It is guided by **principles** (SOLID, DRY, KISS, YAGNI), **continuous testing**, and **refactoring**.

| Aspect | Emergent Design (Agile) | BDUF (Traditional) |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Continuous, per iteration | Exhaustive upfront |
| Flexibility | High — embraces change | Low — change requests are costly |
| Risk Discovery | Early | Late (during coding/testing) |
| Documentation | Just-in-time, lightweight | Heavy, detailed specs |

Emergent design is **disciplined**, not unplanned — it relies on TDD, refactoring, and continuous integration to keep the codebase healthy.

---

### **Question 2: List and briefly explain any three SOLID principles.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP):** A class should have only one reason to change. Each class serves one stakeholder or one responsibility. *(1 mark)*

2. **Open/Closed Principle (OCP):** Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. New behavior is added via new code (e.g., subclasses), not by editing tested existing code. *(1 mark)*

3. **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):** Objects of a superclass must be replaceable with objects of a subclass without breaking the application. Subclasses must honor the parent's contract. *(1 mark)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, OR a single comprehensive question with two distinct aspects.

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

#### **(a) Explain the Agile approach to software design. Discuss the concept of Iteration 0 and the role of spikes in risk reduction. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Agile Design Philosophy (3 marks):**
Agile design is **iterative, evolutionary, and principles-driven**. Instead of a single exhaustive design phase, design activities are distributed across all iterations. The team establishes a **lightweight architectural skeleton** in Iteration 0 and then allows **detailed design to emerge** through:
- Test-Driven Development (TDD)
- Continuous Refactoring
- Application of SOLID, DRY, KISS, YAGNI principles
- Collective code ownership and pair programming

The guiding motto is *"Just Enough Design, Just In Time."*

**2. Iteration 0 / Sprint 0 (2 marks):**
Iteration 0 is a **short preparatory iteration (typically 1–2 weeks)** that sets the foundation. Its deliverables include:
- High-level architectural sketch (not detailed UML)
- Initial Product Backlog with prioritized epics
- Development environment & CI/CD toolchain
- Initial spike results and risk register

**Critical constraint:** Iteration 0 must **not** exceed 2–3 sprints — otherwise the team has regressed into BDUF.

**3. Spike Solutions (2 marks):**
A **spike** is a **time-boxed research task** (1–4 days) used to explore a technical unknown *before* committing to a design. There are two types:
- **Functional Spike:** Investigates a user-facing unknown (e.g., *"How will customers interact with the refund flow?"*)
- **Technical Spike:** Investigates a technical unknown (e.g., *"Can our database handle 10K concurrent writes?"*)

Spikes produce **throwaway code or documented findings** that de-risk the upcoming iteration.

---

#### **(b) With neat diagrams, explain the Test-Driven Development (TDD) Red-Green-Refactor cycle. Why is TDD considered a design activity rather than a testing activity? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The TDD Cycle (4 marks):**

| Phase | Activity | Duration | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RED** | Write the smallest failing test that defines new behavior | ~30% | A red light on the test runner |
| **GREEN** | Write the *simplest* possible code to make the test pass | ~50% | All tests green |
| **REFACTOR** | Improve the design while keeping tests green | ~20% | Cleaner code, same behavior |

The cycle repeats every few minutes per new requirement.

**2. Why TDD is a Design Activity (3 marks):**

- **Forces Decisions Early:** Writing the test *before* the code forces the developer to think about the **API surface, naming, and behavior contracts** — pure design decisions.
- **Executable Specifications:** The tests become a living, executable design document that *cannot lie* (unlike outdated UML diagrams).
- **Crystallizes Interfaces:** TDD naturally pushes developers toward **loose coupling and dependency injection** (DIP), because tightly-coupled code is hard to test.
- **Continuous Validation:** The test suite becomes a **safety net** that enables fearless refactoring — the *true* enabler of emergent design.

> As Kent Beck famously said: *"I'm not a great programmer; I'm just a good programmer with great habits."*

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Adapted, 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

#### **(a) Explain the SOLID principles in detail with examples. How do they support emergent design in Agile? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Single Responsibility Principle — SRP (1.5 marks):**
A class should have **one and only one reason to change**. Each class should serve a single actor (stakeholder). Example: Splitting a fat `Employee` class into `PayrollCalculator`, `PerformanceReporter`, and `EmployeeRepository` ensures each class changes for one reason only.

**2. Open/Closed Principle — OCP (1.5 marks):**
Classes should be **open for extension but closed for modification**. New behavior is added via inheritance, composition, or strategy pattern. Example: Adding a new `DiscountType` (e.g., `SeasonalDiscount`) by writing a new class implementing the `IDiscountStrategy` interface — no changes to the `OrderProcessor`.

**3. Liskov Substitution Principle — LSP (1 mark):**
Subtypes must be **behaviorally compatible** with their base types. The classic violation: a `Square` subclass of `Rectangle` where independent width/height setters break the parent's contract. Solution: Prefer composition (`Square` *has-a* `Rectangle`).

**4. Interface Segregation Principle — ISP (1 mark):**
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Example: A fat `IMachine` interface split into `IPrinter`, `IScanner`, `IFax` — a basic printer doesn't need to implement `scan()`.

**5. Dependency Inversion Principle — DIP (1 mark):**
High-level modules must not depend on low-level modules; both depend on **abstractions**. Example: `OrderService` depends on `IPaymentGateway` interface, not on a concrete `StripeGateway` class — this makes the service testable and swappable.

**6. How SOLID Supports Emergent Design (1 mark):**
- They provide **objective criteria** to evaluate design decisions as the codebase grows.
- They **prevent architecture rot** by ensuring each new feature preserves extensibility.
- They make **refactoring safe** — small, principle-aligned changes keep the system clean.
- They **align with YAGNI** — no speculative abstractions are built; only those that pass the principles are introduced.

---

#### **(b) Discuss the role of Continuous Integration (CI), Continuous Deployment (CD), and Refactoring in Agile implementation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Continuous Integration — CI (2.5 marks):**
CI is the practice of **merging all developer working copies to a shared mainline multiple times a day**, with each merge triggering an **automated build and test suite**.

- **Benefits:** Detects integration conflicts within *hours* instead of weeks; reduces the "Integration Hell" of waterfall projects.
- **Best Practices:** Maintain a single source repository, automate the build, make the build self-testing, keep the build fast (< 10 minutes ideal), test in a clone of production.
- **Frequency Metric:** $f_{\text{CI}} \geq 1$ per developer per day (Kent Beck's rule).

**2. Continuous Deployment / Delivery — CD (2.5 marks):**
CD extends CI by **automatically deploying every passing build** to staging (Continuous Delivery) or production (Continuous Deployment).
- **Benefits:** Drastically shortens feedback loop; reduces manual deployment errors; enables A/B testing and feature flagging.
- **Prerequisites:** Comprehensive automated test suite, feature flags, observability, and a robust rollback strategy.

**3. Refactoring (2 marks):**
**Refactoring** is the disciplined technique of **restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior** to improve non-functional attributes (readability, complexity, extensibility).
- **The Refactoring Catalog** (Martin Fowler) provides 70+ named techniques (e.g., Extract Method, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, Move Method).
- **Critical Rule:** Refactoring is **never** mixed with new feature development in the same commit. The "boy scout rule" applies: leave the code cleaner than you found it.
- **Safety Net:** The test suite is the safety net — if all tests still pass post-refactor, behavior is preserved.

Together, CI + CD + Refactoring form the **technical triad** that makes Agile's *"responding to change"* principle operationally possible.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Software Design & Implementation Questions**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "Agile = No Design":** Many students wrongly write that Agile skips design. The correct answer: Agile does *iterative* design with an Iteration 0 skeleton, not *no* design. **Penalty: 2–3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Mixing Refactoring with Bug Fixing:** Refactoring must be *behavior-preserving*. If a student writes "refactor to fix a bug," the examiner will mark it wrong. **Penalty: 1–2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Citing SOLID without examples:** Listing the acronyms fetches ~2 marks; explaining with a real class example (e.g., SRP via `Employee` class) fetches the full 7 marks. **Always use a concrete example.**
> 
> 4. **Confusing TDD Phases:** Writing "Red → Refactor → Green" is wrong. The correct order is **Red → Green → Refactor**. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> 5. **Saying "Iteration 0 = Waterfall Phase":** Iteration 0 is strictly *time-boxed* to 1–2 weeks. Mentioning the time-box fetches 2 extra marks.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to Mention YAGNI:** Almost every design question expects a reference to YAGNI as the justification for not over-engineering. Forgetting it = -1 mark.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Print This Section**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] **Emergent Design** is Agile's answer to BDUF — design evolves per iteration, guided by tests and refactoring.
- [ ] **Iteration 0** is a 1–2 week preparatory phase for architecture skeleton, toolchain, backlog, and spikes.
- [ ] **Spike** = time-boxed (1–4 days) technical/functional experiment to reduce risk.
- [ ] **TDD** is a *design* activity, not a testing activity. Cycle: **Red → Green → Refactor**.
- [ ] **Refactoring** is *behavior-preserving* code restructuring. It is **NOT** bug-fixing or feature-adding.
- [ ] **CI** = integrate & test multiple times a day. **CD** = auto-deploy passing builds.
- [ ] **Collective Code Ownership** + **Pair Programming** + **Sustainable Pace** = Agile cultural triad.

### 🔑 SOLID Principles
- [ ] **S** — Single Responsibility (one class, one actor)
- [ ] **O** — Open/Closed (extend, don't modify)
- [ ] **L** — Liskov Substitution (subtypes honor contracts)
- [ ] **I** — Interface Segregation (small, role-based interfaces)
- [ ] **D** — Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions)

### 🔑 Supporting Axioms
- [ ] **DRY** — Don't Repeat Yourself
- [ ] **KISS** — Keep It Simple
- [ ] **YAGNI** — You Aren't Gonna Need It
- [ ] **Rule of Three** — Extract a pattern only on its third appearance

### 🔑 Three Rules of Simple Design (Ron Jeffries — in priority order)
1. Passes all tests
2. Reveals intention
3. No duplication
4. Fewest classes & methods

### 🔑 Key Formulas to Memorize
- $\text{TDR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fix}}}{C_{\text{build}}}$ — Target $\leq 5\%$
- $\text{CVC} = \dfrac{\text{Lines Tested}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100$ — Target $80\%+$
- $f_{\text{CI}} \geq 1$ per developer per day
- $V_{\text{commit}} = 0.80 \times \bar{V}$ (sustainable commitment rule)

### 🔑 High-Probability KTU Exam Questions
- Compare Emergent Design vs. BDUF *(14 marks — guaranteed)*
- Explain SOLID with examples *(14 marks — guaranteed)*
- Describe TDD cycle and justify why it's a design activity *(7 marks — very common)*
- Role of CI, CD, and Refactoring *(7 marks — common)*
- Iteration 0 + Spikes *(3 marks or 7 marks sub-question — common)*

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** Always anchor your answer to **a concrete example** (e.g., `Employee` class for SRP, `Order` class for OCP). Abstract answers fetch 2–3 marks; illustrated answers fetch the full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Software Validation** is the process of evaluating software at the end of the software development life cycle (SDLC) to ensure that it meets the *intended business requirements, user needs, and real-world operational expectations*. In the context of **Agile Methodologies**, software validation is treated as a *continuous, iterative, and collaborative* activity that runs in parallel with development rather than as a one-time terminal phase.

According to the **IEEE 1012-2016 Standard for System, Software, and Hardware Verification and Validation**, validation is defined as:

> *"Confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that the requirements for a specific intended use or application have been fulfilled."*

In Agile, validation is operationalized through the **Definition of Done (DoD)**, **Acceptance Criteria**, and **Working Software** deliverables at the end of every Sprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield Line):**
> *"Software Validation answers the question: Are we building the RIGHT product? It demonstrates that the software fulfils its intended use and meets stakeholder needs in the operational environment."*

## 1.2 Verification vs. Validation – The Foundational Distinction

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Core Question | *Are we building the product **right**?* | *Are we building the **right** product?* |
| Focus | Internal consistency, design, code | Real user needs, business goals |
| Approach | **Static** (reviews, inspections, walkthroughs) | **Dynamic** (execution, testing) |
| Timing | Throughout development (early) | Late stage + each Sprint demo |
| Output | Reviewed documents, code walkthroughs | Working, demonstrated software |
| KTU Example | Code review of a Java class | Customer accepts the login feature in UAT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **V&V = Right-Right (Verification = Right method, Validation = Right product)**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** preparing a dish for a customer:
- **Verification** is the *chef tasting the dish* with a thermometer, checking internal temperature, ingredients list, and recipe compliance — *"Is the cooking process correct?"*
- **Validation** is the *customer actually eating the dish* in the dining area — *"Does this dish satisfy my hunger and taste expectation?"*

A dish may pass verification (correct temperature, correct ingredients) but fail validation (customer wanted spicy, not sweet). Similarly, software may pass all unit tests (verification) but fail validation (user cannot complete checkout on mobile).

## 1.4 Why Software Validation Matters in Agile

In traditional (Waterfall) models, validation is a terminal activity — bugs discovered at acceptance testing are **50–100× more expensive** to fix than at requirements stage. Agile compresses this by validating **every increment** (every 1–4 weeks), enforcing:

- **Continuous Customer Collaboration** (one of the four Agile values)
- **Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation**
- **Early and Frequent Delivery** for stakeholder feedback
- **Sustainable Development Pace** that maintains quality

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trigger Phrase:** Whenever a question mentions *"customer feedback"*, *"Sprint Review"*, *"Acceptance Criteria"*, or *"Definition of Done"* — the expected answer is **Software Validation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Philosophical Pillars of Validation

### Pillar 1: Dynamic Validation (Execution-Based)
The software is **executed** with sample inputs and outputs are observed.

**Levels of Dynamic Validation (V-Model Mapping):**

1. **Unit Validation** — Smallest testable components (functions, methods)
2. **Integration Validation** — Interaction between modules/services
3. **System Validation** — End-to-end behavior of the full application
4. **Acceptance Validation (UAT)** — User-side verification in production-like environment

### Pillar 2: Static Validation (Non-Execution-Based)
- **Walkthroughs** — Author leads the team through the artifact
- **Inspections** — Formal, roles-based defect detection (Fagan Inspection)
- **Technical Reviews** — Peer evaluation of design/code
- **Static Analysis Tools** — SonarQube, ESLint, FindBugs

## 2.2 Agile-Specific Validation Techniques

### A. Test-Driven Development (TDD)
A **Red → Green → Refactor** cycle:
1. Write a **failing test** (Red)
2. Write **minimum code** to pass (Green)
3. **Refactor** for cleanliness while keeping tests green

### B. Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)
Uses **Gherkin syntax**: `Given-When-Then` to express validation in natural language.

$$
\text{GIVEN (precondition)} \rightarrow \text{WHEN (action)} \rightarrow \text{THEN (expected outcome)}
$$

### C. Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)
The *customer, developer, and tester* collaboratively write **automated acceptance tests** before coding begins. These tests **ARE** the validation contract.

### D. Exploratory Testing
Skilled testers simultaneously *design and execute* tests based on domain knowledge — no pre-written scripts.

### E. Continuous Validation in DevOps
- **CI/CD pipelines** automatically trigger regression suites on every commit
- **Smoke tests** validate the build within minutes
- **Canary releases** validate behavior with 1–5% of real users

## 2.3 The Definition of Done (DoD) – Agile's Validation Contract

A living checklist enforced at the Sprint boundary:

> *"Code committed, peer-reviewed, unit-tested (>80% coverage), integration-tested, documentation updated, deployed to staging, and accepted by Product Owner."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every item on the DoD is a **validation gate**. A user story is NOT validated until **all** DoD items pass.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Test Metrics)

| # | Metric | Formula | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | Lower is better; > 1.0/KLOC = poor quality |
| 2 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{\text{Items Tested}}{\text{Total Items}} \times 100\%$ | Statements, branches, paths covered |
| 3 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found Pre-Release}}{\text{Total Defects}} \times 100\%$ | Target: > **95%** for production readiness |
| 4 | **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Reliability metric for validation |
| 5 | **Code Churn Ratio** | $Churn = \dfrac{\text{Lines Added + Modified + Deleted}}{\text{Total LOC}}$ | High churn = unstable, needs re-validation |
| 6 | **Validation Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Accepted Stories}}{\text{Total Stories Completed}} \times 100\%$ | Sprint-level validation efficiency |
| 7 | **Escaped Defect Ratio** | $EDR = \dfrac{\text{Post-Release Defects}}{\text{Total Pre-Release Defects}}$ | Lower = better validation effectiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Reminder:** Always specify the **unit** (KLOC, FP, %, hours) when reporting any metric. Examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Validation Practice |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare (FDA)** | IEC 62304 software validation mandatory for medical devices |
| **Aviation (DO-178C)** | Level A software requires 100% statement + decision coverage |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | ASIL-D demands formal validation evidence |
| **Banking (PCI-DSS)** | Penetration validation every quarter |
| **E-Commerce (Agile)** | A/B validation, canary releases, real-user monitoring |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Defect Density (DD) — KTU 3-Mark Standard

> **Problem:** During a Sprint validation cycle, a team of 5 developers worked on a module of **8 KLOC**. During validation, they identified **12 defects** before release. After release, the customer reported **3 more defects** within one month. Calculate the **Defect Density** and the **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**.

### Step 1: Identify Given Quantities
- Module size: $S = 8 \text{ KLOC}$
- Pre-release defects: $D_{pre} = 12$
- Post-release defects: $D_{post} = 3$
- Total defects: $D_{total} = D_{pre} + D_{post} = 12 + 3 = 15$

### Step 2: Apply Defect Density Formula

$$
DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}
$$

$$
DD = \dfrac{15 \text{ defects}}{8 \text{ KLOC}}
$$

$$
DD = 1.875 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result
A DD of 1.875/KLOC exceeds the industry benchmark of **< 1.0/KLOC**, indicating **poor validation thoroughness**. The team should enhance their test coverage and peer reviews.

### Step 4: Apply DRE Formula

$$
DRE = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
DRE = \dfrac{12}{15} \times 100\%
$$

$$
DRE = 80\%
$$

### Step 5: KTU Interpretation
A DRE of 80% is **below the industry target of 95%**, signaling that 20% of defects leaked to the customer — a clear validation failure indicator.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write DRE = $D_{pre} / D_{pre}$ (forgetting post-release defects). Always use $D_{total}$ in the denominator.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Test Coverage Analysis

> **Problem:** A Java service class has **240 lines** of executable code. After Sprint validation, automated tests cover **186 lines**. Calculate (a) Statement Coverage, (b) If 45 out of 60 branches were validated, calculate Branch Coverage.

### Step 1: Statement Coverage

$$
SC = \dfrac{\text{Lines Executed by Tests}}{\text{Total Executable Lines}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SC = \dfrac{186}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SC = 77.5\%
$$

### Step 2: Branch Coverage

$$
BC = \dfrac{\text{Branches Tested}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
BC = \dfrac{45}{60} \times 100\%
$$

$$
BC = 75.0\%
$$

### Step 3: Engineering Decision
- Both metrics are **< 80%** → DoD is **NOT satisfied**
- Agile team must add **23 more unit tests** to reach 80% statement coverage gate
- Per IEEE 1012, branch coverage < 75% indicates inadequate validation for safety-critical systems

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Validation Yield Across Sprints

> **Problem:** A Scrum team completed the following in 4 Sprints: Sprint 1 — 8 stories (8 accepted), Sprint 2 — 10 stories (9 accepted), Sprint 3 — 7 stories (6 accepted), Sprint 4 — 12 stories (12 accepted). Calculate average Validation Yield.

### Step 1: Per-Sprint Yields

$$
Y_1 = \dfrac{8}{8} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

$$
Y_2 = \dfrac{9}{10} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

$$
Y_3 = \dfrac{6}{7} \times 100\% \approx 85.71\%
$$

$$
Y_4 = \dfrac{12}{12} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

### Step 2: Average Yield

$$
\bar{Y} = \dfrac{Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + Y_4}{4}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} = \dfrac{100 + 90 + 85.71 + 100}{4}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} = \dfrac{375.71}{4} = 93.93\%
$$

### Step 3: Validation Maturity Assessment
Yield > 90% indicates a **mature Agile team**. Sprint 3's drop (85.71%) warrants a **Sprint Retrospective** action item to identify the validation root cause (e.g., unclear Acceptance Criteria, missing DoD items).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing a Validation Technique

| Criterion | TDD | BDD | ATDD | Exploratory | Manual UAT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automation Level | High | High | High | Low | None |
| Stakeholder Involvement | Low | High | High | Medium | Highest |
| Documentation Output | Test code | Living spec | Acceptance contract | Test session notes | Sign-off report |
| Defect Detection Phase | Coding | Requirements | Requirements | Post-build | Pre-release |
| KTU Recommended Use | Unit logic | Business rules | Sprint contracts | Edge cases | Final gate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: The Agile Validation Lifecycle (Continuous Loop)

This Mermaid diagram shows how validation loops continuously in Agile, contrasting sharply with the Waterfall's terminal validation approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Product Backlog] --> A2[Sprint Planning]
    A2 --> A3[Define Acceptance Criteria]
    A3 --> A4[Write Failing Tests - TDD/ATDD]
    A4 --> A5[Develop Code - Red Green Refactor]
    A5 --> A6[Continuous Integration Build]
    A6 --> A7[Automated Regression Suite]
    A7 --> A8{All DoD Items Pass?}
    A8 -- No --> A4
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Deploy to Staging]
    A9 --> A10[User Acceptance Testing]
    A10 --> A11{Stakeholder Accepts?}
    A11 -- No --> A12[Backlog Refinement]
    A12 --> A1
    A11 -- Yes --> A13[Sprint Review Demo]
    A13 --> A14[Increment Validated - Working Software]
    A14 --> A15[Next Sprint]
    A15 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Figure 2: Verification & Validation Block Architecture

This diagram maps how V&V activities map to Agile artifacts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VERIFICATION_BLOCK
        B1[Requirements Review] --> B2[Design Inspection]
        B2 --> B3[Code Walkthrough]
        B3 --> B4[Static Analysis - SonarQube]
        B4 --> B5[Peer Code Review]
    end
    subgraph VALIDATION_BLOCK
        C1[Unit Test Execution] --> C2[Integration Test]
        C2 --> C3[System Test]
        C3 --> C4[UAT with Customer]
        C4 --> C5[Production Smoke Test]
    end
    B5 --> C1
    C5 --> D1[Certified Working Software Increment]
```

## 4.3 Figure 3: V-Model Mapping to Agile Sprint Structure

The V-Model is mapped to a single Sprint cycle to show the **parallelism** of Agile validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT_LEG[Development Side - Verification]
        L1[User Story] --> L2[Acceptance Criteria]
        L2 --> L3[Test Cases - TDD]
        L3 --> L4[Source Code]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT_LEG[Testing Side - Validation]
        R1[Acceptance Tests] --> R2[Integration Tests]
        R2 --> R3[System Tests]
        R3 --> R4[Unit Tests]
    end
    L1 -. mapped to .-> R1
    L2 -. mapped to .-> R2
    L3 -. mapped to .-> R3
    L4 -. mapped to .-> R4
```

## 4.4 Figure 4: Definition of Done Validation Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[User Story Completed] --> G2{Code Committed}
    G2 --> G3{Unit Tests Pass}
    G3 --> G4{Code Reviewed}
    G4 --> G5{Integration Tests Pass}
    G5 --> G6{Documentation Updated}
    G6 --> G7{Deployed to Staging}
    G7 --> G8{Po Sign-off}
    G8 -- Yes --> G9[VALIDATED - Done]
    G8 -- No --> H1[Returned to Backlog]
    H1 --> G1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU:** In board answers, always caption diagrams with *"Figure X: [Diagram Name] — illustrating [concept]"* to score full marks for visual presentation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Software Validation. How does it differ from Software Verification?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Software Validation** is the process of evaluating software to ensure it meets the **intended user needs and business requirements** in the operational environment. It answers the question *"Are we building the RIGHT product?"*

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Question Asked | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| Approach | Static (reviews, inspections) | Dynamic (execution, testing) |
| Focus | Design, code, internal consistency | User needs, business value |
| Timing | Throughout development | Each Sprint + Final UAT |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of the Definition of Done (DoD) in Agile validation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Definition of Done (DoD)** is a *shared, agreed-upon checklist* that every Product Backlog Item must satisfy before it is declared complete and validated.

A typical DoD includes:
1. Code written, peer-reviewed, and committed
2. Unit tests written and passing (>80% coverage)
3. Integration tests executed
4. Documentation updated
5. Deployed to a staging environment
6. **Product Owner acceptance** obtained

The DoD acts as a **validation gate** — no story is accepted into the Increment unless all DoD items are fulfilled. It is owned by the **entire Scrum Team** and is continuously refined during Sprint Retrospectives.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for DoD items and gate function)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Note:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, answer **any ONE** of the following two questions.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **levels of software validation** with a neat diagram of the V-Model. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** and **Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)** as Agile validation techniques. Provide a BDD scenario example using Gherkin syntax. (7 Marks)

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Levels of Validation with V-Model (7 Marks)

Software validation is performed at **four progressive levels**, each corresponding to a development phase in the V-Model.

**Level 1: Unit Validation**
- Tests individual functions, methods, or classes in isolation
- Performed by developers using JUnit, NUnit, PyTest
- Fastest feedback — runs in milliseconds

**Level 2: Integration Validation**
- Validates interaction between modules/services
- Techniques: Top-down, Bottom-up, Sandwich testing
- Tools: JUnit + Mockito, Postman (API integration)

**Level 3: System Validation**
- End-to-end testing of the complete application
- Validates functional + non-functional requirements (performance, security, usability)
- Performed in a staging environment mirroring production

**Level 4: Acceptance Validation (UAT)**
- Conducted by **end users / customers** in a production-like environment
- Validates business requirements and user satisfaction
- Final gate before production release

**V-Model Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
Requirements       ←→       Acceptance Testing
   System Design    ←→       System Testing
   Module Design    ←→       Integration Testing
      Coding        ←→         Unit Testing
```

Each development activity on the **left leg** has a corresponding validation activity on the **right leg**. This enforces the principle of *early test planning* — test cases for unit validation are written *during* the coding phase itself.

*(1 mark per level + 2 marks for V-Model mapping + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — TDD and BDD with Gherkin Example (7 Marks)

**Test-Driven Development (TDD)** is an iterative Agile validation technique following the **Red-Green-Refactor** cycle:

1. **Red** — Write a failing test for a new feature
2. **Green** — Write the *minimum* code to make the test pass
3. **Refactor** — Improve code structure while keeping tests green

**Example (Java + JUnit):**

```java
@Test
public void testWithdrawFromAccount() {
    Account acc = new Account(1000);
    acc.withdraw(300);
    assertEquals(700, acc.getBalance());
}
```

The test is written **first**, then production code is added, then refactored. This guarantees >80% test coverage by design.

**Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)** extends TDD by writing tests in **natural language** that stakeholders can read. It uses the **Gherkin** `Given-When-Then` format.

**Example BDD Scenario (ATM Withdrawal):**

```
Feature: ATM Cash Withdrawal

  Scenario: Successful withdrawal with sufficient balance
    Given the account balance is 5000 INR
    And the ATM contains sufficient cash
    When the user requests to withdraw 2000 INR
    Then the account balance should be 3000 INR
    And the ATM should dispense 2000 INR
    And a transaction receipt should be printed
```

**Tools:** Cucumber, SpecFlow, JBehave execute these scenarios as automated tests.

**Key Difference:** TDD validates *technical* behavior; BDD validates *business* behavior with stakeholder-readable specs.

*(3 marks for TDD explanation + 3 marks for BDD + Gherkin example + 1 mark for comparison = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss **Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)** and the role of the **Three Amigos** in Agile validation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 10 KLOC module was validated in a Sprint. Pre-release testing identified 18 defects; post-release, the customer reported 2 additional defects. Calculate the **Defect Density** and **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**. Comment on the validation quality. (7 Marks)

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — ATDD and Three Amigos (7 Marks)

**Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)** is an Agile validation practice where the **customer, developer, and tester** collaborate *before* coding begins to write **automated acceptance tests**. These tests become the **validation contract** for the user story.

**Process Flow:**
1. User Story selected in Sprint Planning
2. Three Amigos workshop: customer states *what*, developer assesses *how*, tester identifies *edge cases*
3. Automated acceptance tests are written and **executed against non-existent code** (they fail)
4. Development proceeds; tests turn green as code is written
5. All tests green = story validated and accepted

**The Three Amigos:**
- 👤 **Business** (Product Owner, Customer) — Defines the *what* and *why*
- 👨‍💻 **Development** (Programmer) — Defines the *how*
- 🧪 **Testing** (QA Engineer) — Defines the *what could go wrong*

**Benefits:**
- Reduces ambiguity in Acceptance Criteria
- Shifts validation left — defects caught at requirements stage
- Living documentation that never goes stale
- Shared ownership of quality across the team

**Tools:** FitNesse, Cucumber, Concordion, Robot Framework.

*(3 marks for ATDD process + 3 marks for Three Amigos + 1 mark for benefits/tools = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Defect Density & DRE Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Module size: $S = 10 \text{ KLOC}$
- Pre-release defects: $D_{pre} = 18$
- Post-release defects: $D_{post} = 2$
- Total defects: $D_{total} = 18 + 2 = 20$

**Step 1: Calculate Defect Density**

$$
DD = \dfrac{D_{total}}{S} = \dfrac{20 \text{ defects}}{10 \text{ KLOC}}
$$

$$
DD = 2.0 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Defect Removal Efficiency**

$$
DRE = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
DRE = \dfrac{18}{20} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Step 3: Validation Quality Commentary**

| Metric | Industry Benchmark | Our Result | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| DD | < 1.0/KLOC | 2.0/KLOC | ❌ Poor (2× above benchmark) |
| DRE | > 95% | 90% | ⚠ Below benchmark |

**Conclusion:** The 90% DRE indicates that 10% of defects escaped to production. Combined with the high defect density of 2.0/KLOC, the validation process is **suboptimal**.

**Recommended Agile Actions:**
- Increase unit test coverage to >85%
- Conduct **peer code review** for every commit
- Implement **mutation testing** to validate test effectiveness
- Strengthen the DoD to include "zero high-severity defects"
- Schedule a **Sprint Retrospective** action item to review escape root causes

*(2 marks for DD calc + 2 marks for DRE calc + 3 marks for commentary + recommendations = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing V&V** — Writing validation for verification definition (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting units** in metric answers (–1 mark)
> 3. **No diagram** in V-Model question — minimum 2 marks deducted
> 4. **Skipping the "Comment" step** in numerical questions — partial marks only
> 5. **Not mentioning the Agile context** — Always link back to Sprint, DoD, or Increment
> 6. **Mixing up DRE denominator** — Use $D_{total}$, not $D_{pre}$ (–1 to –2 marks)

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ✅ **Validation = Right Product** | **Verification = Right Method**
- ✅ Validation is **dynamic + late-stage**, but Agile makes it **continuous + per-Sprint**
- ✅ **Four Levels:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (UAT)
- ✅ **Three Amigos:** Business + Developer + Tester (collaborative validation)
- ✅ **DoD** is the **validation contract** — no story is Done until all DoD items pass
- ✅ **TDD** = Red → Green → Refactor | **BDD** = Given-When-Then | **ATDD** = Customer-Developer-Tester written tests
- ✅ **V-Model** maps development (left) to validation (right) — 1:1 correspondence
- ✅ **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC → target < 1.0/KLOC
- ✅ **DRE** = Pre-release / Total × 100% → target > 95%
- ✅ **Test Coverage** (Statement, Branch, Path) → target > 80% for DoD
- ✅ **Continuous Validation** in DevOps = CI/CD + Smoke + Canary + A/B testing
- ✅ **Tools to mention in answers:** JUnit, Cucumber, Selenium, SonarQube, Postman
- ✅ **Standards to cite (if asked):** IEEE 1012-2016, ISO 25010, IEC 62304, DO-178C
- ✅ **Always end answers with the "Agile benefit"** — e.g., *"This enables faster feedback, lower defect cost, and continuous customer collaboration."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Evolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Evolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: SOFTWARE EVOLUTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Software Evolution* is the process of developing and maintaining software systems, in which the software is continually modified throughout its life cycle to accommodate changes in the user requirements, the operating environment, and to correct errors, improve performance, or adapt to new hardware. It encompasses all activities from initial development through successive releases and long-term maintenance.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Organism" View

Imagine a **city** that was built 50 years ago. The city is never "finished." New flyovers are added, old buildings are demolished and rebuilt, roads are widened, water pipelines are upgraded, and new Wi-Fi zones appear. The city's **blueprint keeps evolving** while people continue to live and work in it daily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software is exactly like that city** — once deployed, it is never truly "done." It must continuously **adapt, repair, enhance, and re-engineer** itself to remain useful. This continuous, post-delivery transformation of software is called **Software Evolution**.

The discipline that studies this phenomenon is called **Software Evolution** — a sub-field of Software Engineering formally established by **Meir "Manny" Lehman** in the late 1960s after his landmark studies on large-scale operating systems (OS/360, MULTICS).

### 🎯 Why Software Evolution Matters in Agile

In **Agile Methodologies**, software evolution is not an afterthought — it is the **central heartbeat** of the entire development process. Agile embraces evolution by:
- Delivering software in **small, frequent increments** (iterations/sprints)
- Welcoming **changing requirements**, even late in development
- Maintaining a **sustainable development pace** for indefinite lifetime
- Continuously **refactoring** code to keep it clean and adaptive

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 1 focus is on understanding *what* drives software to change, *how* we model that change, and *what laws* govern large-scale evolutionary behavior.

---

## 2. Terminology Quick-Glance Table

| Term | Meaning in One Line |
|---|---|
| **Software Maintenance** | Post-delivery modification of software to correct faults, improve attributes, or adapt to changes |
| **Software Evolution** | The broader, longer-term trend of progressive change in a software system |
| **Legacy System** | An old, often critical system that must be maintained but is hard to extend |
| **Refactoring** | Restructuring existing code without changing external behavior to improve non-functional attributes |
| **Re-engineering** | Rebuilding/reorganizing a legacy system to make it more maintainable, often with new functionality |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Analyzing existing software to extract design information, often from code back to design |
| **Forward Engineering** | Moving from high-level abstractions (design) down to implementation (code) |
| **Software Reuse** | Using existing software components in new systems or new contexts |
| **E-Type System** (Lehman) | A system whose operation embeds the real-world problem; thus it *must* evolve with the world |

---

## 3. The Three Categories of Lehman (E-Type Evolution)

> [!NOTE]
> Lehman classified all software into three categories based on how they are specified and used:

### 🔹 S-Type (Specified) Programs
- **Definition:** Programs whose behavior is fully and precisely specifiable.
- **Example:** A sorting algorithm, a mathematical library, a compiler's lexical analyzer.
- **Evolution Behavior:** Essentially static once built. They can be proven correct, and changes only occur if the spec changes.
- **Key Point:** They do *not* evolve in the Lehman sense — once correct, always correct (in theory).

### 🔹 P-Type (Practical) Programs
- **Definition:** Programs whose behavior depends on the human operator's interpretation. They solve a real-world problem heuristically.
- **Example:** An operating system scheduler, a heuristic route-planner.
- **Evolution Behavior:** Changes as the problem environment changes and as operators learn better ways to use it.

### 🔹 E-Type (Embedded) Programs
- **Definition:** Programs that operate as part of a real-world process. The real-world process *is embedded* inside the program's behavior.
- **Example:** An ATM system, an air traffic control system, an e-commerce platform.
- **Evolution Behavior:** **Continuous and mandatory** — the system *must* evolve to remain useful, or it progressively loses value.
- **Key Insight:** Almost all large business systems are E-Type, and they are the primary subject of Lehman's Laws.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam:** Always remember — *most business software is E-Type*, and Lehman's Laws apply **only** to E-Type systems. This is a favorite 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution (HIGH-YIELD 🔥)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Meir Lehman and his colleagues at IBM studied the evolution of large OS systems (OS/360) over decades. Between **1969 and 1996**, Lehman (and later co-authors like Ramil, Wernick) formulated and refined a set of **8 laws** governing the evolution of E-Type systems.

The first **5 laws** were originally published in **1974** (the "original 5 laws"). Three more were added in **1996** to address the feedback-loop era of modern development.

### 📜 The Eight Laws — Complete Reference

| # | Law Name | Formal Statement (Simplified) | Implication for Agile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Continuing Change** | A software system must be continually adapted, or it becomes progressively less useful. | Continuous delivery is mandatory. |
| **L2** | **Increasing Complexity** | As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is actively done to reduce it. | Justifies the need for **refactoring** and **technical debt management**. |
| **L3** | **Self-Regulation** | Software evolution processes are self-regulating with distributions of system attributes close to normal. | Effort and rate of change are statistically predictable. |
| **L4** | **Conservation of Organizational Stability** | The average effective global activity rate on an evolving system is invariant over the product's lifetime. | Teams cannot work faster than a sustainable limit — supports *sustainable pace* (Agile principle). |
| **L5** | **Conservation of Familiarity** | During the active life of a system, the incremental growth in any release remains roughly constant. | Predictable release content; supports time-boxed sprints. |
| **L6** | **Continuing Growth** | The functional content of a system must be continually increased to maintain user satisfaction over its lifetime. | Backlogs never truly empty — new features are perpetual. |
| **L7** | **Declining Quality** | Unless rigorously adapted to changing operational environments, a system's quality will appear to decline. | Code quality degrades without active investment. |
| **L8** | **Feedback System** | Successful evolution of E-type systems requires a *multi-level, multi-loop* feedback system to achieve convergence. | Closes the loop between users, customers, and developers — Agile's heart. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Papers:** Students often state laws like "L3: Self-Regulation" and claim the system is *stable*. The correct interpretation is that the **process** is self-regulating, not the system itself.

### 🔍 Deep Dive: Why Do These Laws Exist?

The underlying reason for all these laws lies in a single observation: **E-Type systems are dual-natured objects**.

1. **As an abstract program** → governed by mathematical rules (deterministic, provable).
2. **As a real-world artifact** → embedded in a continuously changing human/organizational environment.

This **duality** is the engine of evolution. Because the world changes, the system *must* change. Because the system changes, complexity creeps. Because complexity grows, the team must work harder just to *hold* the same value. This is the **entropy trap** that Agile methodologies try to escape.

---

## 2. Software Maintenance: The Day-to-Day Reality

Maintenance begins **after** delivery and consumes the majority of a software system's lifecycle cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic (Lehman & Belady, 1985):** Maintenance consumes **60%–80%** of total software lifetime cost. This is a frequently tested fact in KTU.

### 🔹 ISO/IEC 14764 Classification of Maintenance

The international standard categorizes maintenance into **4 primary types**:

| Type | Purpose | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|
| **Corrective** | Fix defects/bugs | Reported errors, fault reports |
| **Adaptive** | Adapt to environment changes | New OS, new hardware, new regulations, new dependencies |
| **Perfective** | Add new features or improve performance | New user requirements, market demands |
| **Preventive** | Improve maintainability, fix latent faults *before* they cause failures | Code analysis, refactoring, technical debt reduction |

### 🔹 Maintenance Process Flow (KTU Expected Diagram)

```
Maintenance Request → Classification → Impact Analysis → Approval
     → Implementation → Testing → Release → Documentation Update → Closure
```

> [!TIP]
> **Remember "CAPP"** — **C**orrective, **A**daptive, **P**erfective, **P**reventive. This mnemonic appears in nearly every KTU 2-mark question.

### 🔹 Maintenance Cost Drivers

Maintenance effort is not random. It is influenced by:

- **System stability** — older, stable systems are easier to maintain
- **Programming language** — high-level languages = lower maintenance cost
- **Personnel experience** — domain knowledge retention is critical
- **Code quality and documentation** — directly impacts defect density
- **External dependencies** — APIs, third-party libraries, regulatory changes

---

## 3. Legacy Systems and Re-Engineering

### 🔹 What is a Legacy System?

> [!NOTE]
> A *legacy system* is a software system that is **old, large, and critical** to business operations but is built on outdated technology and is difficult/expensive to extend or maintain.

**Examples:** COBOL-based banking mainframes, old ERP systems, proprietary telco billing systems.

### 🔹 Re-Engineering Decision Model

The decision to re-engineer a legacy system follows a structured process:

1. **Inventory Analysis** — catalog all legacy systems
2. **Business Value Assessment** — high, medium, low
3. **System Quality Assessment** — condition, maintainability
4. **Maintenance Cost Analysis** — current and projected
