# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(3 Marks)** *Explain the Cynefin Framework. How does it justify the use of Agile methods?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Cynefin Framework**, developed by Dave Snowden in 1999, is a sense-making model with **five domains**: Clear, Complicated, Complex, Chaotic, and Confusion (Disorder). Each domain represents a different cause-effect relationship.

In the **Complex domain**, cause-and-effect relationships can only be understood in retrospect — they cannot be predicted in advance. Most modern software development falls into this domain because requirements, technology, and user behaviour evolve together.

The framework justifies Agile as follows: the appropriate response in the Complex domain is **"Probe → Sense → Respond"** — run small experiments, gather feedback, and adapt. This is exactly what Agile Sprints, Retrospectives, and customer demos do. Therefore, **Agile is theoretically optimal for complex software work**, while Waterfall is suited to Clear/Complicated domains.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**
> Many students **just list the five domains** and lose 1.5 marks. You must (1) name the framework and author, (2) define each domain briefly, and (3) *explicitly link* Complex domain to Agile. All three are required for full marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(3 Marks)** *List the five focusing steps of the Theory of Constraints and state the principle behind it.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Theory of Constraints** (Goldratt, 1984) is based on the principle that *"a system is only as fast as its slowest constraint"*. The five focusing steps are:

1. **Identify** the system's constraint (bottleneck).
2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its output without major change).
3. **Subordinate** all other activities to the constraint.
4. **Elevate** the constraint (add capacity permanently).
5. **Repeat** — once the constraint is broken, find the next one.

In Agile, this is visible in **WIP limits** (exploit), **Sprint Goals** (subordinate), and **Dedicated Review Capacity** (elevate).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 out of 2, 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(14 Marks)** *With suitable examples, explain the major theories that form the theoretical foundation of Agile management. Discuss how each theory justifies an Agile practice.*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain Cynefin Framework, Theory of Constraints, and Systems Theory in detail. How do they justify Agile practices?*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cynefin Framework (3 marks)**
Dave Snowden's Cynefin Framework classifies problems into five domains based on the clarity of cause-effect relationships:
- **Clear**: Best practices, no need for Agile.
- **Complicated**: Good practices, expert analysis, Waterfall may apply.
- **Complex**: Emergent practices, **Agile applies here**.
- **Chaotic**: Novel practices, crisis management.
- **Confusion**: Need to re-classify.

Most software development is **Complex** — hence Agile's iterative "Probe-Sense-Respond" cycles are theoretically optimal. The **Daily Standup** and **Sprint Review** are sense-making activities.

**(ii) Theory of Constraints (2 marks)**
Goldratt's ToC says system throughput = bottleneck throughput. The five steps (Identify, Exploit, Subordinate, Elevate, Repeat) are mirrored in:
- **WIP limits** in Kanban (exploitation).
- **Sprint Goal** as a focus point (subordination).
- **Dedicated review/testing capacity** (elevation).

**(iii) Systems Theory (2 marks)**
Von Bertalanffy's Systems Theory says the **whole is greater than the sum of its parts** due to emergence, feedback loops, and synergy. Agile teams are designed for **synergy** (cross-functional), **emergence** (self-organization), and **feedback loops** (Retrospectives, Sprint Reviews). This justifies the **whole-team approach** of Agile over the siloed approach of Waterfall.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain Complexity Theory, Lean Thinking, and Tuckman's Model. Discuss their relevance to Agile teams.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Complexity Theory (2.5 marks)**
A **Complex Adaptive System (CAS)** has many independent agents that interact, learn, and adapt. **Order emerges** from local interactions; it cannot be imposed top-down. Properties: self-organization, emergence, non-linearity, path dependency. Agile teams are CAS — they are self-organizing, cross-functional, and produce emergent solutions. Short Sprints manage the chaos by creating frequent feedback.

**(ii) Lean Thinking (2.5 marks)**
Lean (Womack & Jones) focuses on:
- **Value** (customer-defined)
- **Value Stream** (end-to-end)
- **Flow** (smooth, uninterrupted)
- **Pull** (demand-driven)
- **Perfection** (Kaizen)

The **seven wastes** (transport, inventory, motion, waiting, overproduction, over-processing, defects + unused talent) are actively eliminated in Agile via WIP limits, continuous integration, Definition of Done, and Retrospectives.

**(iii) Tuckman's Model (2 marks)**
Tuckman's stages: **Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning**. Scrum Masters use this to:
- Allow **Storming** (it is natural and necessary).
- Facilitate **Norming** through team working agreements.
- Reach **Performing** (self-organization).
- Manage **Adjourning** in final Retrospectives.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**
> For 14-mark answers, do NOT write a *general* essay. **Allocate marks to sub-parts** explicitly. In part (a), three theories should be discussed, and in part (b), three more. Students who write only 4 theories total lose 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(14 Marks)** *Compare Waterfall and Agile management in terms of their theoretical underpinnings. Justify why Agile is theoretically superior for modern software development.*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Using Cynefin Framework and Theory of Constraints, justify why Agile is preferred over Waterfall in complex software projects.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cynefin-Based Justification (4 marks)**
Modern software development involves **uncertain requirements, evolving technology, and shifting user expectations**. This places it firmly in the **Cynefin "Complex" domain** where cause-effect is only retrospectively visible. In Complex domains, the correct management response is **"Probe → Sense → Respond"** — exactly what Agile Sprints do. Waterfall's "Sense → Analyze → Respond" is suited to Complicated domains but fails in Complex ones because the analysis is based on **assumed (not real)** cause-effect. The famous *"Conway's Law meets Moore's Law"* problem means requirements shift during development, breaking the Waterfall plan. Therefore, **Agile is theoretically justified** for most software work.

**(ii) ToC-Based Justification (3 marks)**
Waterfall optimizes **local efficiency** (each phase is locally efficient). However, Goldratt's Theory of Constraints says system throughput depends on the **bottleneck**, not on local efficiency. In Waterfall, the bottleneck often shifts to late phases (Testing, UAT) because defects propagate. **Agile's iterative approach** surfaces the bottleneck early (in the first Sprint) and applies the 5-step ToC continuously — every Sprint is a "focus on the current constraint" cycle. This is why Agile teams deliver faster overall, even though each individual activity may not be the fastest.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Using Complexity Theory, Lean Thinking, and Servant Leadership, discuss why Agile teams outperform traditional command-and-control teams in software projects.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Complexity Theory (2.5 marks)**
Software teams are **Complex Adaptive Systems (CAS)**. Waterfall's command-and-control model assumes a **mechanical system** (predictable parts). But in CAS, attempts to control from the top **suppress emergence** and lead to brittleness. Agile's **self-organizing teams** allow emergent solutions to surface — exactly the right intervention for a CAS.

**(ii) Lean Thinking (2.5 marks)**
Lean says **eliminate waste, deliver value**. Waterfall generates massive waste: unused documentation, late-found defects, handoffs, and rework. Agile's **iterative delivery** and **Definition of Done** eliminate these wastes. Specifically, the **seven lean wastes** are addressed by:
- Daily Standups (waiting/transport)
- CI/CD (defects, motion)
- Backlog grooming (overproduction)
- Retrospectives (over-processing, unused talent)

**(iii) Servant Leadership (2 marks)**
Robert Greenleaf's **Servant Leadership** philosophy says the leader's role is to **serve the team** — removing impediments, coaching, and empowering. This is fundamentally different from Waterfall's **command-and-control** project manager. Modern knowledge workers (software engineers) are **Theory Y** people (McGregor) — they are self-motivated, seek responsibility, and thrive under servant leadership. Command-and-control demotivates them, causing attrition and low quality. Agile's Scrum Master is the textbook servant-leader.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**
> A common mistake is to write a *marketing-style* essay ("Agile is faster, better, cheaper!") without **naming and applying** the theories. Each sub-part of part (a) and part (b) demands a **named theory with application**. Failing to name the theory explicitly = **loss of 1.5–2 marks per sub-part**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Recap — Read Before Exam**

- [x] **Cynefin Framework** has **5 domains**: Clear, Complicated, Complex, Chaotic, Confusion. Agile applies to **Complex**.
- [x] **Probe → Sense → Respond** is the Complex domain response.
- [x] **Theory of Constraints (ToC)** — 5 steps: **Identify, Exploit, Subordinate, Elevate, Repeat**.
- [x] ToC Principle: *System throughput = bottleneck throughput.*
- [x] **Systems Theory** — Emergence, synergy, feedback loops, boundaries. Whole > sum of parts.
- [x] **Complexity Theory** — Complex Adaptive Systems: self-organization, emergence, non-linearity, path dependency.
- [x] **Lean** — 5 principles: Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection. **7 + 1 wastes** (Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, + Unused Talent).
- [x] **Tuckman** — Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.
- [x] **Servant Leadership** — Greenleaf. **10 characteristics** (Listening, Empathy, Healing, Awareness, Persuasion, Conceptualization, Foresight, Stewardship, Growth, Community).
- [x] **Contingency Theory** — Fiedler. **No one best way** — adapt to context.
- [x] **Chaos Theory** — Lorenz. **Butterfly effect** — sensitive dependence on initial conditions. Justifies **short iterations**.
- [x] **Conway's Law** + **Cynefin Complex** = why Waterfall fails in modern software.
- [x] **Scrum Master = Servant Leader**; **PO = Customer servant**; **Team = Self-organizing CAS**.
- [x] **Retrospective** addresses Lean (Kaizen) + Systems Theory (feedback) + Tuckman (stage-appropriate coaching).
- [x] **Daily Standup** addresses ToC (identify constraint) + Lean (eliminate waiting) + Cynefin (sense-making).
- [x] **Sprint Review** addresses Cynefin (probe-sense-respond) + Lean (customer-defined value).
- [x] **WIP Limits** address ToC (exploit) + Lean (flow).
- [x] **Timeboxed Sprints** address Chaos Theory (limited prediction horizon) + Complexity Theory (iteration).
- [x] **Cross-functional teams** address Systems Theory (synergy).
- [x] The **Cynefin "Cliff"** — moving from Complex to Complicated prematurely (over-planning) destroys value.
- [x] **Hybrid approaches** (SAFe, Spotify) are born from **Contingency Theory** — context matters.
- [x] **McGregor's Theory X/Y** is the *human-side* underpinning of Agile (Theory Y) vs. Waterfall (Theory X).

> [!TIP] **Final KTU Exam Mantra**
> *"Never write Agile is good because it is Agile. Always write Agile is theoretically justified by [Named Theory] because [Reasoning], therefore [Specific Practice] is optimal for [Specific Context]."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Software Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Agile Software Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agile Software Development** is an umbrella term for a set of frameworks and practices based on the values and principles expressed in the *Agile Manifesto* (2001). It is an iterative and incremental (IID) approach to software engineering that emphasizes **adaptive planning, evolutionary delivery, and continuous improvement**, while encouraging rapid and flexible response to change.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Agile Software Development is defined as a **lightweight, people-centric, and value-driven software development methodology** that:

- Decomposes the project lifecycle into small, time-boxed iterations called **sprints** (typically **2 to 4 weeks** in duration).
- Produces working software increments at the end of every iteration rather than exhaustive documentation.
- Embraces changing requirements, even late in development, as a competitive advantage.
- Maintains a close, daily collaboration between **cross-functional teams** and the **customer/product owner**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)**
>
> Imagine you are very hungry and walk into a restaurant.
>
> - **Traditional (Waterfall) Model**: The waiter hands you a 50-page menu. You must order *everything* — starters, main course, dessert — at once. You cannot change your mind once the kitchen starts cooking. The food arrives only after 2 hours — all at the same time.
>
> - **Agile Model**: The waiter brings out one dish at a time, every 15 minutes. You taste it, give feedback ("less spicy, please"), and the next dish arrives adjusted to your taste. You stop when you are full.
>
> **In software terms**: The customer tastes a working feature every sprint, gives feedback, and the next sprint delivers an improved version. This is **iterative development with customer collaboration**.

## 1.3 The Genesis: The Agile Manifesto (2001)

In **February 2001**, **17 software practitioners** met at **Snowbird, Utah, USA**, and authored the *Agile Manifesto*. The manifesto is built on **4 core values** and **12 supporting principles**.

### The 4 Core Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We are uncovering better ways of developing software by doing it and helping others do it. Through this work we have come to value:
>
> 1. **Individuals and Interactions** over *processes and tools*
> 2. **Working Software** over *comprehensive documentation*
> 3. **Customer Collaboration** over *contract negotiation*
> 4. **Responding to Change** over *following a plan*
>
> *That is, while there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more.*

## 1.4 The 12 Principles of Agile Software Development (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite — Memorize these principles; they appear in nearly every exam.**

| # | Principle (Short Form) |
|---|------------------------|
| 1 | **Customer satisfaction** through early and continuous delivery of valuable software |
| 2 | **Welcome changing requirements**, even late in development |
| 3 | **Deliver working software frequently** (weeks rather than months) |
| 4 | **Business people and developers** must work together daily |
| 5 | Build projects around **motivated individuals**; give them the environment and support they need |
| 6 | **Face-to-face conversation** is the most effective method of communication (co-location) |
| 7 | **Working software** is the primary measure of progress |
| 8 | Agile processes promote **sustainable development** — maintain a constant pace indefinitely |
| 9 | Continuous attention to **technical excellence and good design** |
| 10 | **Simplicity** — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential |
| 11 | The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from **self-organizing teams** |
| 12 | The team **reflects** on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior |

## 1.5 Why Agile? The Limitations of Traditional Models

Traditional models like the **Waterfall Model** suffer from:

- **Rigidity**: Sequential phases make mid-course corrections extremely costly.
- **Late Delivery Risk**: Testing happens at the end, so defects are discovered too late.
- **Customer Disconnection**: Customer sees the product only after long development cycles.
- **High Failure Rate**: Reports suggest **>60%** of traditional software projects fail to meet original scope, time, or cost goals (Standish Group CHAOS Report).

Agile solves this by being **adaptive**, **iterative**, and **collaborative**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of Agile Software Development

Agile methodologies share a common set of characteristics that distinguish them from plan-driven approaches:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Characteristics of Agile (Must-Know for KTU Module 2)**
>
> 1. **Iterative and Incremental Development** — The product is built in small, repeated cycles.
> 2. **Time-Boxing** — Each iteration has a fixed duration (**2–4 weeks**), known as a *sprint* or *time-box*.
> 3. **Customer Collaboration** — Active, ongoing involvement of the customer/product owner.
> 4. **Adaptive Planning** — Planning is done at the start of every iteration, not once at project initiation.
> 5. **Self-Organizing Teams** — Teams decide *how* to accomplish the work, not the manager.
> 6. **Continuous Integration & Testing** — Code is integrated and tested daily to detect issues early.
> 7. **Working Software as Primary Metric** — Progress is measured by shippable, functional software increments.
> 8. **Minimal Documentation** — Lightweight artifacts (user stories, burn-down charts) replace exhaustive specs.
> 9. **Sustainable Pace** — Teams work at a pace that can be maintained indefinitely (avoiding burnout).
> 10. **Face-to-Face Communication** — Direct, rich communication is preferred over written documents.

## 2.2 The Agile Software Development Lifecycle (Iterative Loop)

The Agile lifecycle is a continuous, repeating cycle. The standard flow is:

1. **Concept/Iteration Planning** — Define the goal and select user stories for the sprint.
2. **Iteration (Sprint) Execution** — Build, code, test, integrate the selected features.
3. **Daily Standup (Scrum Meeting)** — 15-minute sync on *what was done, what is next, blockers*.
4. **Sprint Review/Demo** — Demonstrate working software to the product owner/stakeholders.
5. **Sprint Retrospective** — Team reflects on what went well, what to improve next iteration.
6. **Backlog Grooming/Refinement** — Re-prioritize product backlog based on feedback.
7. **Repeat** — The cycle restarts with the next iteration.

## 2.3 The Iterative-Incremental Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Agile is not "no planning" — it is *rolling-wave planning*. Each iteration re-plans the immediate future in detail, while leaving the distant future coarse.

In a project with total scope $S$ delivered across $N$ iterations, each iteration ideally delivers an increment of value. Let:

- $S$ = total product scope (story points or function points)
- $V_i$ = value delivered at the end of iteration $i$
- $C_i$ = cumulative value delivered after $i$ iterations

The cumulative value delivered is:

$$C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} V_k$$

The percentage of completion at iteration $i$ is:

$$P_i = \frac{C_i}{S} \times 100\%$$

The team's **velocity** (a KTU high-yield term) is the average value delivered per iteration:

$$V_{\text{team}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity** is the cornerstone metric in Agile. It is used to forecast when the project will complete. If a team has a stable velocity of $V_{\text{team}}$ story points per sprint, the **estimated number of remaining sprints** is:
>
> $$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{S - C_{\text{done}}}{V_{\text{team}}} \right\rceil$$

## 2.4 Agile vs. Traditional (Plan-Driven) Development — Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the highest-yield content in KTU Module 2. It is a guaranteed question in either Part A or Part B.**

| Parameter | Traditional (Plan-Driven / Waterfall) | Agile |
|-----------|---------------------------------------|-------|
| **Approach** | Sequential, linear | Iterative, incremental |
| **Requirements** | Fixed at start, frozen early | Evolving, welcomed even late |
| **Delivery** | Single delivery at end | Frequent small deliveries |
| **Customer Involvement** | At milestones (beginning and end) | Continuous, daily collaboration |
| **Documentation** | Extensive, comprehensive | Minimal, just-enough |
| **Team Structure** | Specialized, siloed roles | Cross-functional, self-organizing |
| **Planning** | Detailed upfront plan | Adaptive, rolling-wave |
| **Change Management** | Formal change control board | Changes embraced each iteration |
| **Testing** | At the end of the cycle | Continuous, test-driven |
| **Risk Profile** | High risk discovered late | Low risk, discovered early |
| **Success Metric** | Adherence to plan | Working software delivered |
| **Feedback Cycle** | Long (months) | Short (days to weeks) |
| **Suitable For** | Well-understood, stable requirements | Dynamic, uncertain requirements |
| **Examples** | Waterfall, V-Model, Spiral (plan-driven) | Scrum, XP, Kanban, FDD |

## 2.5 When to Use Agile (and When Not To)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agile is NOT a silver bullet. KTU expects you to know when to apply it.**

**Agile is BEST suited for:**

- Projects with **uncertain, evolving, or rapidly changing requirements** (e.g., startups, R&D).
- Small to medium **co-located, cross-functional teams** (**typically 5–9 members**).
- Projects where **customer involvement is feasible** and active.
- Domains with high innovation needs: web apps, mobile apps, AI/ML products.

**Agile is NOT ideal for:**

- **Safety-critical / mission-critical systems** (aerospace, medical devices) where extensive documentation and certification are mandated.
- Projects with **strict regulatory compliance** (FDA, DO-178C) requiring exhaustive traceability.
- **Very large, distributed teams** (>50 members, multiple time zones).
- Projects with **frozen, well-defined requirements** (e.g., government tenders with fixed specs).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Unit / Format |
|---------------|------------|---------------|
| $S$ | Total product scope (story points) | Story points |
| $V_i$ | Value delivered in iteration $i$ | Story points |
| $C_i$ | Cumulative value after $i$ iterations | Story points |
| $V_{\text{team}}$ | Team velocity | Story points / sprint |
| $N$ | Total number of iterations (planned) | Integer |
| $T_{\text{sprint}}$ | Sprint duration | **2 to 4 weeks** |
| $P_i$ | Percentage of completion at iteration $i$ | Percentage (%) |
| $N_{\text{remaining}}$ | Sprints remaining to complete the project | Integer (ceiling) |

**Key formulas:**

$$C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} V_k \qquad \qquad P_i = \frac{C_i}{S} \times 100\% \qquad \qquad V_{\text{team}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \qquad \qquad N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{S - C_{\text{done}}}{V_{\text{team}}} \right\rceil$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Agile is not just a software process — it is an **engineering philosophy** that powers modern product development across industries:
>
> - **Software**: Spotify, Amazon, Microsoft Azure, Google, Meta all run on Agile/Scrum.
> - **Hardware**: Tesla uses Agile for firmware; SpaceX uses iterative design for rockets.
> - **Non-Engineering**: Marketing teams use Kanban boards; HR uses sprints for hiring.
>
> The core engineering value of Agile is **risk reduction through early and continuous feedback loops**, which is a universal principle in any complex system design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sprint Velocity & Project Forecasting

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Style):**
>
> A software team is using Agile Scrum. The product backlog contains a total of **300 story points**. The team has completed **5 sprints** and delivered the following values:
>
> Sprint 1: **40** story points
> Sprint 2: **45** story points
> Sprint 3: **50** story points
> Sprint 4: **48** story points
> Sprint 5: **42** story points
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The team's average velocity.
> (b) The cumulative value delivered so far.
> (c) The number of sprints remaining to complete the project.
> (d) The percentage of completion after Sprint 5.

### Step 1 — Compute Cumulative Value Delivered ($C_5$)

We use the cumulative sum formula:

$$C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} V_k$$

Substituting $i = 5$:

$$C_5 = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4 + V_5$$

$$C_5 = 40 + 45 + 50 + 48 + 42$$

$$\boxed{C_5 = 225 \text{ story points}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Team Velocity ($V_{\text{team}}$)

We use the velocity formula:

$$V_{\text{team}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$$

With $N = 5$:

$$V_{\text{team}} = \frac{1}{5} \times (40 + 45 + 50 + 48 + 42)$$

$$V_{\text{team}} = \frac{225}{5}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{team}} = 45 \text{ story points / sprint}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Sprints Remaining ($N_{\text{remaining}}$)

Using the forecasting formula:

$$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{S - C_{\text{done}}}{V_{\text{team}}} \right\rceil$$

With $S = 300$, $C_{\text{done}} = 225$, $V_{\text{team}} = 45$:

$$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{300 - 225}{45} \right\rceil$$

$$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{75}{45} \right\rceil$$

$$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil 1.667 \right\rceil$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{remaining}} = 2 \text{ sprints}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Percentage of Completion ($P_5$)

Using the percentage formula:

$$P_5 = \frac{C_5}{S} \times 100\%$$

$$P_5 = \frac{225}{300} \times 100\%$$

$$P_5 = 0.75 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{P_5 = 75\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Note that $N_{\text{remaining}}$ uses the **ceiling function** because you cannot deliver a *fraction* of a sprint — the team needs a full sprint to deliver the remaining 75 story points (45 in Sprint 6, 30 in Sprint 7).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Burn-Down Chart Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
>
> A team starts a sprint with **120 story points** of work in the sprint backlog. The ideal burn-down for a **10-day sprint** removes **12 points per day**. After 4 days, the team has **80 points** remaining.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) The actual average daily burn rate so far.
> (b) The projected completion date if this rate continues.
> (c) Is the team ahead, on-track, or behind schedule?

### Step 1 — Compute Actual Burn Rate

The actual work done in 4 days is:

$$W_{\text{done}} = 120 - 80 = 40 \text{ story points}$$

The actual daily burn rate is:

$$R_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{done}}}{\text{Days elapsed}} = \frac{40}{4} = \boxed{10 \text{ points / day}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Projected Completion

If the team continues at 10 points/day, the remaining work is **80 points**, which needs:

$$D_{\text{remaining}} = \frac{80}{10} = 8 \text{ days}$$

Total project time = 4 (elapsed) + 8 (remaining) = **12 days**.

### Step 3 — Compare with Ideal

Ideal rate = **12 points/day**, actual = **10 points/day**.

$$\Delta = R_{\text{ideal}} - R_{\text{actual}} = 12 - 10 = 2 \text{ points/day (lag)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Team is BEHIND schedule by } 2 \text{ points/day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A burn-down chart that **flattens** (no reduction) for a day is called a *flat-line day* — it indicates an **impediment** (blocker). A burn-up chart (the dual visualization) tracks total scope added vs. total work done.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Agile Practices by Category

| Category | Practice | Purpose | KTU Exam Weight |
|----------|----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Planning** | User Stories, Story Mapping, Release Planning | Capture requirements in customer language | High |
| **Execution** | Pair Programming, Mob Programming, Continuous Integration | Code quality + team knowledge sharing | High |
| **Quality** | Test-Driven Development (TDD), Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD), Refactoring | Defect prevention over detection | High |
| **Collaboration** | Daily Stand-up, Sprint Review, Retrospective | Information radiators, feedback loops | High |
| **Visibility** | Burn-down Chart, Burn-up Chart, Velocity Chart, Cumulative Flow Diagram | Track progress and forecast | Medium |
| **Architecture** | Evolutionary Architecture, Emergent Design, Last Responsible Moment | Avoid big-design-upfront (BDUF) | Medium |
| **Sustainability** | Sustainable Pace, Team Norms, Definition of Done | Long-term productivity | Low |

---

## 3.4 The Agile Triangle (Iron Triangle vs. Agile Triangle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note — Often Tested in KTU Module 2:**
>
> - **Traditional Iron Triangle (Project Management):** Scope, Time, Cost — *quality* is the variable that suffers.
> - **Agile Triangle:** *Value*, *Quality* are FIXED (non-negotiable). Time and Cost are *bounded*. Scope is the *flexible* variable — the customer can drop features to maintain quality and deadline.

This is a fundamental shift in mindset and is a common KTU question.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Key Numerical Results (Worked Example 1)

| Metric | Computed Value | Interpretation |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| $C_5$ (Cumulative value) | **225 story points** | 225 of 300 points delivered |
| $V_{\text{team}}$ (Velocity) | **45 story points/sprint** | Stable, predictable team |
| $N_{\text{remaining}}$ (Sprints left) | **2 sprints** | Project finishes in Sprint 7 |
| $P_5$ (Percentage complete) | **75%** | Three-quarters done |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agile Development Lifecycle Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram — The repeating sprint cycle of Agile Software Development.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Backlog Created] --> B[Sprint Planning Meeting]
    B --> C[Sprint Backlog Defined]
    C --> D[Sprint Execution 2 to 4 Weeks]
    D --> E[Daily Standup 15 Minutes]
    E --> F{Daily Work Loop}
    F --> G[Develop Code]
    G --> H[Continuous Integration]
    H --> I[Run Tests]
    I --> J[Code Review]
    J --> K{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    K -- No --> F
    K -- Yes --> L[Update Done Increment]
    L --> M{Sprint Duration Over}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N[Sprint Review with Stakeholders]
    N --> O[Sprint Retrospective Team Reflection]
    O --> P[Backlog Refinement for Next Sprint]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Agile vs. Waterfall: Architectural Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Waterfall Model]
        direction TB
        T1[Requirements Gathering] --> T2[System Design]
        T2 --> T3[Implementation]
        T3 --> T4[Integration and Testing]
        T4 --> T5[Deployment]
        T5 --> T6[Maintenance]
    end
    subgraph AGILE[Agile Iterative Model]
        direction TB
        A1[Iteration 1 Plan] --> A2[Iteration 1 Build Test]
        A2 --> A3[Iteration 1 Deliver Increment]
        A3 --> A4[Iteration 2 Plan]
        A4 --> A5[Iteration 2 Build Test]
        A5 --> A6[Iteration 2 Deliver Increment]
        A6 --> A7[Iteration N Deliver Final]
    end
```

## 4.3 The 4 Values and 12 Principles — Conceptual Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AM[Agile Manifesto 2001]
    AM --> V1[Value 1 Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools]
    AM --> V2[Value 2 Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation]
    AM --> V3[Value 3 Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation]
    AM --> V4[Value 4 Responding to Change over Following a Plan]
    V1 --> P1[Principle Motivated Individuals]
    V1 --> P2[Principle Face to Face Communication]
    V1 --> P3[Principle Self Organizing Teams]
    V2 --> P4[Principle Working Software as Measure of Progress]
    V2 --> P5[Principle Continuous Delivery]
    V2 --> P6[Principle Technical Excellence]
    V3 --> P7[Principle Customer Collaboration Daily]
    V3 --> P8[Principle Customer Satisfaction Early and Continuous]
    V4 --> P9[Principle Welcome Changing Requirements]
    V4 --> P10[Principle Sustainable Pace]
    V4 --> P11[Principle Simplicity Maximize Work Not Done]
    V4 --> P12[Principle Regular Reflection and Adjustment]
```

## 4.4 Agile Roles and Ceremonies — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROLES[Core Agile Roles]
        PO[Product Owner]
        SM[Scrum Master]
        DT[Development Team 5 to 9 Members]
    end
    subgraph ARTIFACTS[Core Artifacts]
        PB[Product Backlog]
        SB[Sprint Backlog]
        IN[Increment Working Software]
    end
    subgraph CEREMONIES[Core Ceremonies]

        RET[Sprint Retrospective]

    end
    PO --> PB
    PO --> SP
    SM --> DS
    SM --> RET
    DT --> SB
    DT --> IN
    SP --> SB
    SB --> IN
    IN --> SR
    DS --> SB
    BG --> PB
    RET --> BG
```

## 4.5 Sprint Velocity Trend — Visual Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sprint 1 Velocity 40] --> S2[Sprint 2 Velocity 45]
    S2 --> S3[Sprint 3 Velocity 50]
    S3 --> S4[Sprint 4 Velocity 48]
    S4 --> S5[Sprint 5 Velocity 42]
    S5 --> AVG[Average Velocity 45 Points per Sprint]
    AVG --> FORECAST[Forecasting Remaining Sprints]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Agile Team

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CUST[Customer and Stakeholders] <--> PO[Product Owner]
    PO <--> PB[(Product Backlog Prioritized)]
    PB --> SPMT[Sprint Planning Meeting]
    SPMT --> SB[(Sprint Backlog)]
    SB --> DEV[Cross Functional Development Team]
    DEV --> CI[Continuous Integration Server]
    CI --> TEST[Automated Test Suite]
    TEST --> INCR[Shippable Product Increment]

    DEMO --> CUST
    DEV --> RET[Sprint Retrospective]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams and Not Physical Drawings?**
>
> Agile Software Development is a *process and workflow* concept, not a physical hardware object. Mermaid flowcharts precisely model the *flow of information, artifacts, and decisions* — which is the engineering essence of the topic. This aligns with the KTU syllabus focus on process understanding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern (Module 2 Internal Choice):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (2 to be answered out of 3).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with internal choice (full sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks).
> - All questions map to Course Outcomes (CO2 / CO3) and Bloom's Levels (L1–L4).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Agile Software Development. List the four values of the Agile Manifesto.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Agile Software Development** is an iterative and incremental software development methodology based on the *Agile Manifesto* (2001). It emphasizes adaptive planning, customer collaboration, and rapid delivery of working software in short iterations called *sprints* (typically **2–4 weeks**).
>
> **The Four Values of the Agile Manifesto:**
>
> 1. **Individuals and Interactions** over *processes and tools*
> 2. **Working Software** over *comprehensive documentation*
> 3. **Customer Collaboration** over *contract negotiation*
> 4. **Responding to Change** over *following a plan*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Model]**
*List any six characteristics of Agile Software Development.*

**Model Answer:**

> Six key characteristics of Agile Software Development are:
>
> 1. **Iterative and incremental** delivery of software in small cycles.
> 2. **Time-boxed** sprints (typically **2–4 weeks**).
> 3. **Customer collaboration** throughout the project lifecycle.
> 4. **Self-organizing, cross-functional teams** of **5–9 members**.
> 5. **Working software** as the primary measure of progress.
> 6. **Adaptive planning** with willingness to embrace change even late in development.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **twelve principles of the Agile Manifesto** with one-line descriptions of each. *(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Agile Software Development** with the **Traditional (Waterfall) Model** across at least **eight parameters**. *(**7 Marks**)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The 12 principles of the Agile Manifesto are:

1. **Customer satisfaction through early and continuous delivery** — The highest priority is to satisfy the customer by delivering valuable software early and continuously.
2. **Welcome changing requirements, even late in development** — Agile processes harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.
3. **Deliver working software frequently** — Prefer the shorter timescale, ranging from a couple of weeks to a couple of months.
4. **Business people and developers must work together daily** — Close, daily cooperation between business people and developers.
5. **Build projects around motivated individuals** — Give them the environment and support they need, and trust them to get the job done.
6. **Face-to-face conversation is the most effective** — The most efficient and effective method of conveying information is direct, in-person conversation.
7. **Working software is the primary measure of progress** — A working increment is the true indicator of real progress.
8. **Agile processes promote sustainable development** — Sponsors, developers, and users should maintain a constant pace indefinitely.
9. **Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design** — Enhances agility by improving the speed and quality of work.
10. **Simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done** — Focus on what is essential and eliminate waste.
11. **The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from self-organizing teams** — Great solutions come from empowered teams.
12. **The team regularly reflects on how to become more effective** — Then tunes and adjusts its behavior accordingly.

*(For 7 marks, write each principle with a 1–2 line explanation as above.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Parameter | Traditional (Waterfall) | Agile |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Approach** | Sequential, linear phases | Iterative, incremental cycles |
| 2 | **Requirements** | Frozen early via sign-off | Evolving, refined continuously |
| 3 | **Delivery** | Single delivery at project end | Frequent small deliveries per sprint |
| 4 | **Customer Involvement** | At start and at delivery | Continuous, daily collaboration |
| 5 | **Documentation** | Heavy, comprehensive specs | Light, just-enough documentation |
| 6 | **Team Structure** | Specialized, role-segregated | Cross-functional, self-organizing |
| 7 | **Risk Profile** | High — late discovery | Low — early and continuous feedback |
| 8 | **Change Management** | Formal CCB, costly | Changes embraced in next sprint |
| 9 | **Testing** | End-of-cycle phase | Continuous / test-driven |
| 10 | **Progress Metric** | % milestones complete | Working software delivered |

**Conclusion:** Agile outperforms traditional models in dynamic, uncertain environments by embracing change and delivering value incrementally, while traditional models are better suited for stable, well-understood, safety-critical domains.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A software product has a **total backlog of 400 story points**. A Scrum team has completed **6 sprints** with the following velocity per sprint:
Sprint 1: 50, Sprint 2: 55, Sprint 3: 60, Sprint 4: 58, Sprint 5: 62, Sprint 6: 55.

Calculate:
(i) Average velocity of the team.
(ii) Cumulative story points delivered so far.
(iii) Number of sprints remaining to complete the project.
(iv) Percentage of completion after Sprint 6.
*(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of traditional software development models** and explain how Agile Software Development addresses them. *(**7 Marks**)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Cumulative Story Points Delivered**

$$C_6 = 50 + 55 + 60 + 58 + 62 + 55 = \mathbf{340 \text{ story points}}$$

**Step 2: Average Team Velocity**

$$V_{\text{team}} = \frac{340}{6} = \mathbf{56.67 \text{ story points / sprint}}$$

**Step 3: Sprints Remaining**

$$N_{\text{remaining}} = \left\lceil \frac{400 - 340}{56.67} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.058 \right\rceil = \mathbf{2 \text{ sprints}}$$

**Step 4: Percentage of Completion**

$$P_6 = \frac{340}{400} \times 100\% = \mathbf{85\%}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Limitations of Traditional Models:**

1. **Late Defect Detection** — Testing happens at the end; defects are costly to fix.
2. **Inflexibility to Change** — Once requirements are baselined, changes require formal change control.
3. **Customer Disengagement** — The customer sees the product only at delivery, leading to mismatched expectations.
4. **High Project Failure Rate** — Standish Group reports show >50% of waterfall projects exceed budget/time.
5. **Risk Concentration** — All technical risk surfaces at the integration/testing stage.
6. **Poor Feedback Loop** — Feedback cycles are months long, stifling course correction.

**How Agile Addresses These:**

| Limitation | Agile Solution |
|------------|----------------|
| Late defect detection | Continuous integration + automated testing per iteration |
| Inflexibility to change | Sprint-level replanning, welcoming changing requirements |
| Customer disengagement | Daily collaboration with Product Owner + Sprint Reviews |
| High failure rate | Iterative delivery reduces scope/time risk exposure |
| Risk concentration | Risk identified in every iteration through retrospectives |
| Poor feedback loop | 2–4 week feedback cycles with working software demos |

**Conclusion:** Agile's iterative nature, customer-centricity, and adaptive planning make it a robust solution to the chronic problems of traditional software development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> - **Do NOT confuse the 4 *values* with the 12 *principles*.** Values are *prioritizations*; principles are *practical guidelines*. Mixing them up costs easy marks.
> - **Avoid stating "Agile has no documentation."** This is the *biggest misconception*. Agile prefers *just-enough* documentation, not *zero* documentation.
> - **In numerical problems, always show the formula before substitution.** Examiners award 1 mark for the formula itself.
> - **The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is mandatory** when computing remaining sprints — never round down or truncate.
> - **Burn-down vs. Burn-up:** Burn-down tracks *remaining* work; Burn-up tracks *completed* work. Don't interchange.
> - **Agile is a *mindset*, not a framework.** Scrum, XP, Kanban are *implementations* of Agile. The question may ask for the difference.
> - **Story points ≠ person-hours.** Story points are a *relative* measure of effort, complexity, and risk, not absolute time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Use this in the last 15 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Agile** = iterative + incremental, customer-collaborative, change-embracing.
- [x] **Manifesto** signed by **17 practitioners** at **Snowbird, Utah (2001)**.
- [x] **4 Values** = Individuals & Interactions, Working Software, Customer Collaboration, Responding to Change.
- [x] **12 Principles** must be memorized (see table in Section 1.4).
- [x] **Sprint duration = 2 to 4 weeks** (a *must-know* KTU fact).
- [x] **Team size = 5 to 9 members** (cross-functional, self-organizing).
- [x] **Velocity = average story points delivered per sprint** (used for forecasting).
- [x] **Cumulative value** $C_i = \sum V_k$ from $k=1$ to $i$.
- [x] **Sprints remaining** $N_{\text{remaining}} = \lceil (S - C_{\text{done}}) / V_{\text{team}} \rceil$.
- [x] **Iron Triangle (Traditional)** = Scope/Time/Cost fixed; Quality flexes.
- [x] **Agile Triangle** = Value/Quality fixed; Scope flexes; Time/Cost bounded.
- [x] **Working software > documentation**, but documentation is *not zero* — it is *just-enough*.
- [x] **Agile fits dynamic/uncertain requirements**; **not** for safety-critical or fixed-spec projects.
- [x] **Daily standup = 15 minutes**, three questions (done / next / blockers).
- [x] **Burn-down** = remaining work; **Burn-up** = completed work; **Velocity chart** = per-sprint throughput.
- [x] **Ceremonies**: Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective, Backlog Grooming.
- [x] **Artifacts**: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment.
- [x] **Roles**: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team.
- [x] **Story points are relative**, not absolute time estimates.
- [x] **TDD, Pair Programming, CI/CD, Refactoring** = key engineering practices in Agile.
- [x] **Agile is a mindset**; Scrum, XP, Kanban, FDD, DSDM are concrete *frameworks*.

---

topics.title: Traditional Model vs. Agile Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Traditional Model vs. Agile Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Traditional Model (Plan-Driven / Waterfall Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Traditional Model** (also called the *Plan-Driven Model* or *Waterfall Model*) is a **sequential, linear software development life cycle (SDLC)** approach in which each phase — *Requirements, Design, Implementation, Verification, and Maintenance* — must be completed before the next begins. Progress flows **downwards** through phases, like a waterfall, with **minimal iteration** and **fixed scope** established at the start.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect-Built House" 🏠

Imagine you commission an architect to build a house. The architect first draws the **complete blueprint** (Requirements + Design), then the foundation is laid, then the walls, then the roof, then the interiors, and finally the painting. You cannot move in until the **entire structure is complete**, and changing the floor plan midway is extremely expensive. This is the **Traditional Model** — everything is planned *upfront*, and changes are costly.

---

## 1.2 Agile Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Agile Model** is an **iterative and incremental** software development methodology that emphasizes **flexibility, customer collaboration, and rapid delivery** of working software. It is grounded in the **Agile Manifesto (2001)** and operates in short, time-boxed iterations (called *sprints*, typically **1–4 weeks**), delivering a potentially shippable product increment after every cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tasting-and-Adjusting Chef" 👨‍🍳

Now imagine you ask a chef to prepare a grand feast. The chef doesn't cook everything at once. Instead, after every **30 minutes**, they bring out a small tasting plate — you taste it, say *"less salt, more spice"*, and they adjust the recipe **in real time**. The final dish is delivered in **small, delicious batches**, each informed by your previous feedback. This is the **Agile Model** — *iterative delivery + continuous customer feedback*.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly asks students to *contrast* the two models across **at least 8 dimensions** (approach, requirements, customer, testing, risk, documentation, delivery, change handling). Memorizing this comparison is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in university exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Each Model

### Traditional Model Philosophy
1. **Predictability First** — Projects with *well-understood* requirements must be planned exhaustively before coding starts.
2. **Documentation is King** — Every decision, design, and requirement is captured in formal documents to enable future audits and handovers.
3. **Customer is the "Spec Giver"** — The customer defines requirements once; the development team executes them.
4. **Change is the Enemy** — Late-stage changes are discouraged because they invalidate prior work and increase cost.

### Agile Model Philosophy
1. **Adaptability First** — Requirements *will* evolve; embracing change is cheaper than resisting it.
2. **Working Software is King** — A working increment delivered today is **more valuable** than a perfect spec delivered tomorrow.
3. **Customer is the "Team Member"** — Continuous collaboration replaces contract-bound handoffs.
4. **Change is the Fuel** — Welcoming late changes allows the product to stay aligned with real market needs.

---

## 2.2 The Agile Manifesto (2001) — Foundation Stone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Agile Manifesto** was authored by **17 software practitioners** at Snowbird, Utah, in **February 2001**. It defines **4 core values** and **12 principles** that differentiate Agile from traditional approaches.

### Four Core Values
1. **Individuals & Interactions** over *processes and tools*
2. **Working Software** over *comprehensive documentation*
3. **Customer Collaboration** over *contract negotiation*
4. **Responding to Change** over *following a plan*

> *The items on the right have value, but the items on the left are valued more.*

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** for Module 2. Reproduce it from memory in the exam hall.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Traditional Model** | **Agile Model** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Development Approach** | Sequential, linear (Waterfall) | Iterative + Incremental (Sprints) |
| 2 | **Requirements** | Fixed, frozen at project start | Evolving, refined each iteration |
| 3 | **Customer Involvement** | At milestone reviews only | Continuous, daily/weekly |
| 4 | **Documentation** | Extensive (SRS, SDD, STD, etc.) | Lightweight, just-enough |
| 5 | **Delivery Style** | Single delivery at project end | Frequent small releases (every sprint) |
| 6 | **Testing** | Performed after implementation | Continuous (Test-Driven, every sprint) |
| 7 | **Risk Exposure** | Discovered late (often at UAT) | Mitigated early (visible every sprint) |
| 8 | **Change Management** | Discouraged; costly to implement | Welcomed; built into the process |
| 9 | **Team Structure** | Hierarchical, role-specialized | Cross-functional, self-organizing |
| 10 | **Feedback Loop** | Long (months between demos) | Short (end of every sprint, 1–4 weeks) |
| 11 | **Project Size Fits** | Large, well-defined projects | Small-to-medium, dynamic-scope projects |
| 12 | **Success Metric** | On-time, on-budget, on-spec | Working software + customer satisfaction |
| 13 | **Planning Horizon** | Long-term, fixed plan | Rolling-wave, adaptive plan |
| 14 | **Communication** | Formal (written reports) | Informal (daily stand-ups, face-to-face) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Traditional** is dominant in **safety-critical engineering** — avionics, medical devices, nuclear systems — where **regulatory documentation** (e.g., DO-178C, IEC 62304) is mandatory and *cannot* be replaced by working code alone.
* **Agile** dominates **consumer-facing software** — mobile apps, e-commerce, SaaS platforms — where market feedback drives monthly evolution and **time-to-market** dictates business survival.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Comparison

## 3.1 Phase-by-Phase Operational Walkthrough

### Traditional Model — 5 Sequential Phases

1. **Requirement Analysis & Planning** — Stakeholders and Business Analysts gather *all* requirements. Output: **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** document.
2. **System Design** — Architects produce **High-Level Design (HLD)** and **Low-Level Design (LLD)** documents.
3. **Implementation (Coding)** — Developers write code strictly per design. Unit tests are written alongside.
4. **Integration & Testing** — All modules are integrated, then **System Testing**, **Acceptance Testing** are performed. Bugs are fixed in a separate maintenance cycle.
5. **Deployment & Maintenance** — The complete product is released to production. Patches and updates follow a formal change-request process.

> *There is **no going back** to Phase 1 without expensive re-work. Each phase has a **gate review** (sign-off required) before progressing.*

### Agile Model — Iterative Sprint Cycle (Scrum example)

1. **Product Backlog Creation** — Product Owner maintains a *prioritized* list of features (user stories).
2. **Sprint Planning** — Team selects a subset of high-priority stories for the upcoming sprint (1–4 weeks).
3. **Daily Stand-up (15 min)** — Each member answers: *What did I do? What will I do? Any blockers?*
4. **Sprint Review** — At the end of the sprint, the team demos the *working increment* to the customer.
5. **Sprint Retrospective** — Team reflects on *what went well, what to improve* — process is refined.
6. **Next Sprint Begins** — Cycle repeats until backlog is exhausted or product is retired.

> *Each sprint delivers a **potentially shippable product increment**, and the backlog is **continuously re-prioritized** based on customer feedback.*

---

## 3.2 Quantitative Cost-of-Change Comparison

The **Cost of Change Curve** is a fundamental analytical concept in Software Engineering Economics.

$$
C_{traditional}(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}
$$

$$
C_{agile}(t) \approx C_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot t)
$$

Where:
* $C_0$ = **baseline cost of a change** at the start of the project
* $k$ = **exponential growth constant** for traditional model (typically $k \in [0.05,\, 0.15]$ per month)
* $\alpha$ = **linear growth rate** for agile model (typically $\alpha \in [0.01,\, 0.05]$ per sprint)
* $t$ = **time elapsed** (months for traditional, sprints for agile)

### Step-by-Step Numerical Comparison

> **Problem:** Compare the cost of making a change at month **$t = 6$** for both models, given $C_0 = \text{₹}10{,}000$, $k = 0.10$, $\alpha = 0.03$.

**Step 1 — Traditional Model Cost**

$$
C_{traditional}(6) = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{0.10 \cdot 6}
$$

$$
C_{traditional}(6) = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{0.60}
$$

$$
C_{traditional}(6) = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.8221
$$

$$
C_{traditional}(6) \approx \text{₹}18{,}221
$$

**Step 2 — Agile Model Cost** *(assuming 6 months ≈ 12 two-week sprints, so $t = 12$)*

$$
C_{agile}(12) = 10{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.03 \cdot 12)
$$

$$
C_{agile}(12) = 10{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.36)
$$

$$
C_{agile}(12) = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.36
$$

$$
C_{agile}(12) = \text{₹}13{,}600
$$

**Step 3 — Difference**

$$
\Delta C = C_{traditional} - C_{agile} = 18{,}221 - 13{,}600 = \text{₹}4{,}621
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The traditional model exhibits **exponential cost growth** because every change may invalidate completed phases (design, code, tests), forcing cascade re-work. Agile's **linear growth** is possible because each sprint produces an isolated, working increment, so changes are localized to the current iteration's backlog.

---

## 3.3 Decision Framework — When to Choose Which Model

| **Choose Traditional** if… | **Choose Agile** if… |
| :--- | :--- |
| Requirements are **stable, well-documented** upfront | Requirements are **vague, evolving**, or unclear |
| Project is **safety/life-critical** (medical, aviation) | Project is **market-driven** (apps, SaaS) |
| **Regulatory compliance** demands exhaustive documentation | Rapid **time-to-market** is the priority |
| Team is **distributed, large, and specialized** | Team is **co-located, small, cross-functional** |
| Customer cannot commit to **continuous involvement** | Customer is **available and engaged** throughout |
| Technology is **well-understood and mature** | Technology is **novel, exploratory**, or high-risk |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write *"Agile is always better than Traditional."* This is **wrong** and loses marks. The correct answer is: **"The choice depends on project characteristics, team structure, and customer availability — neither is universally superior."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Traditional (Waterfall) Model — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS"] --> B1["SYSTEM DESIGN"]
    B1 --> C1["IMPLEMENTATION CODING"]
    C1 --> D1["INTEGRATION AND TESTING"]
    D1 --> E1["DEPLOYMENT"]
    E1 --> F1["MAINTENANCE"]
    F1 --> G1["PROJECT ENDS"]
    F1 -. "Change Request" .-> H1["EXPENSIVE REWORK"]
    H1 -. "Loops Back" .-> B1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows show the **forward sequential flow**. The dashed arrow shows the *only* way to revisit an earlier phase — via an expensive, formal **change request**. There is no internal iteration within phases.

---

## 4.2 Agile Model — Iterative Sprint Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PRODUCT BACKLOG"] --> P2["SPRINT PLANNING"]
    P2 --> P3["SPRINT BACKLOG"]
    P3 --> P4["DAILY STANDUPS"]
    P4 --> P5["WORKING INCREMENT"]
    P5 --> P6["SPRINT REVIEW"]
    P6 --> P7["SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE"]
    P7 --> P8["BACKLOG REFINEMENT"]
    P8 --> P1
    P6 -. "Customer Feedback" .-> P1
    P5 -. "Continuous Testing" .-> P4
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The model is **cyclical**, not linear. After every sprint, customer feedback re-shapes the product backlog, and the next sprint begins. Continuous testing happens *during* the sprint, not after.

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Decision Framework — When to Pick Which

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["START NEW PROJECT"] --> Q2{"Are requirements stable and well-known?"}
    Q2 -- "YES" --> Q3{"Is regulatory documentation mandatory?"}
    Q2 -- "NO" --> Q4{"Is customer available for continuous collaboration?"}
    Q3 -- "YES" --> R1["USE TRADITIONAL MODEL"]
    Q3 -- "NO" --> R2["USE TRADITIONAL OR SCRUMFALL HYBRID"]
    Q4 -- "YES" --> R3["USE AGILE MODEL SCRUM OR XP"]
    Q4 -- "NO" --> R4["USE AGILE WITH PROXY PRODUCT OWNER"]
    R1 --> Z1["DELIVER SINGLE RELEASE"]
    R2 --> Z2["DELIVER PHASED RELEASES"]
    R3 --> Z3["DELIVER INCREMENTAL RELEASES"]
    R4 --> Z4["DELIVER INCREMENTAL RELEASES WITH CAUTION"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the **Traditional Software Development Model**. List **any three** characteristics of this model.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The Traditional Model (also called the *Plan-Driven* or *Waterfall* model) is a **sequential, linear SDLC approach** where each phase (Requirement, Design, Implementation, Testing, Maintenance) is completed before the next begins.
>
> **Three characteristics:**
> 1. **Sequential phases** with strict gate reviews between them.
> 2. **Frozen requirements** at the start of the project — changes are costly.
> 3. **Extensive documentation** (SRS, SDD, STD) is the primary deliverable alongside code.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the **Agile Manifesto**? List its **four core values**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Agile Manifesto** is a foundational document drafted in **February 2001** by 17 software practitioners at Snowbird, Utah. It articulates the values and principles that distinguish Agile software development from traditional plan-driven approaches.
>
> **Four Core Values:**
> 1. **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools
> 2. **Working software** over comprehensive documentation
> 3. **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation
> 4. **Responding to change** over following a plan

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question*

---

> ### 🅰️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*
>
> **(a)** Compare the **Traditional Model** and the **Agile Model** across **any seven** parameters. For each parameter, write **one line** contrasting the two approaches. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **suitable diagram**, explain the **phases of the Waterfall (Traditional) Model**. Briefly discuss **two situations** in which the Waterfall model is preferred over Agile. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Seven-Parameter Comparison *(7 marks — 1 mark per parameter)*

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Traditional Model** | **Agile Model** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Approach** | Sequential, one-shot | Iterative, sprint-based |
| 2 | **Requirements** | Frozen at the start | Evolving throughout |
| 3 | **Customer Role** | Passive, reviews at milestones | Active, collaborates daily |
| 4 | **Testing** | After coding phase | Continuous, every sprint |
| 5 | **Delivery** | One final release | Incremental releases |
| 6 | **Documentation** | Heavy (SRS, SDD, STD) | Light, just-enough |
| 7 | **Risk Discovery** | Late (at UAT) | Early (every sprint review) |

#### Part (b) — Waterfall Phases + When to Use *(7 marks)*

**Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1["REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS - SRS DOCUMENT"] --> W2["SYSTEM DESIGN - HLD AND LLD"]
    W2 --> W3["IMPLEMENTATION - CODING AND UNIT TEST"]
    W3 --> W4["INTEGRATION AND SYSTEM TESTING"]
    W4 --> W5["DEPLOYMENT TO PRODUCTION"]
    W5 --> W6["MAINTENANCE AND SUPPORT"]
```

**Two situations preferring Waterfall over Agile (3 marks):**

1. **Safety-Critical Systems (e.g., Avionics, Medical Devices):** Regulatory bodies such as **FDA** and **FAA** require exhaustive, traceable documentation for every requirement, design decision, and test case. The Waterfall model naturally produces this audit trail, whereas Agile's *minimal-documentation* philosophy conflicts with these mandates.

2. **Projects with Fixed, Contract-Bound Scope:** When a client pays a **fixed price** for a **fixed deliverable** (e.g., government tenders, defence contracts), the scope cannot evolve. The Waterfall model allows the team to define a complete schedule, cost, and scope upfront, making contractual compliance straightforward.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **draw the Waterfall diagram incorrectly** by adding backward arrows between phases. In the *pure* Waterfall model, **no backward iteration is permitted** — only formal change requests can re-open a phase. Drawing arrows backwards will cost you **2 marks**.

---

> ### 🅱️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*
>
> **(a)** Explain the **four core values** of the Agile Manifesto with a **one-line justification** for each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Scrum framework** in detail. Draw the **Scrum process flow** and explain the roles of the **Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team**. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Core Values with Justification *(7 marks — 1.75 marks per value)*

1. **Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools**
   *Justification:* The most effective communication is *face-to-face* conversation. A skilled, motivated team using simple tools outperforms a mediocre team using best-in-class tools. People drive success, not processes.

2. **Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation**
   *Justification:* A working increment delivered today provides immediate *business value* and *user feedback*. Lengthy documentation is often outdated, unread, and does not generate revenue.

3. **Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation**
   *Justification:* Requirements evolve throughout the project. Continuous customer engagement ensures the final product matches *real* needs, rather than the *assumed* needs captured in a signed contract.

4. **Responding to Change over Following a Plan**
   *Justification:* Markets shift, competitors launch, technologies evolve. A team that *adapts quickly* delivers more business value than one rigidly following a stale plan created months earlier.

#### Part (b) — Scrum Framework *(7 marks)*

**Roles (3 marks):**
* **Product Owner (PO):** Represents the *customer* and *business*. Owns the *Product Backlog* — a prioritized list of features (user stories). Decides *what* gets built and in *what order*.
* **Scrum Master (SM):** A *servant-leader* for the team. Removes *impediments*, facilitates Scrum ceremonies, and ensures the team follows Agile practices. **Not** a traditional project manager.
* **Development Team:** Typically **3–9 cross-functional members** (developers, testers, designers). They *self-organize* to deliver the sprint increment — no sub-teams or hierarchies.

**Scrum Process Flow Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["PRODUCT BACKLOG"] --> S2["SPRINT PLANNING"]
    S2 --> S3["SPRINT BACKLOG"]
    S3 --> S4["SPRINT 1 TO 4 WEEKS"]
    S4 --> S5["DAILY STANDUP 15 MIN"]
    S5 --> S6["SPRINT REVIEW DEMO"]
    S6 --> S7["SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE"]
    S7 --> S1
```

**Five Ceremonies (2 marks):**
1. **Sprint Planning** — Team selects backlog items for the sprint.
2. **Daily Stand-up** — 15-minute sync meeting, held at the *same time and place* daily.
3. **Sprint Review** — Demo of the working increment to stakeholders.
4. **Sprint Retrospective** — Team reflects on process improvements.
5. **Backlog Refinement** — Continuous grooming of the Product Backlog.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake is calling the **Scrum Master** a *"project manager"* or *"team leader"*. In Scrum, the Scrum Master is a **servant-leader** who *facilitates* and *removes impediments* — they do **not** assign tasks, manage budgets, or control the team. Writing "Scrum Master = Project Manager" will cost you **2–3 marks**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Traditional vs. Agile**

- **Traditional Model** = *Sequential*, **Waterfall**, **plan-driven**, *fixed scope*, *heavy docs*, *late testing*, *single delivery*.
- **Agile Model** = *Iterative + incremental*, *sprint-based*, *adaptive scope*, *light docs*, *continuous testing*, *frequent releases*.
- **Agile Manifesto (Feb 2001, 17 authors, Snowbird Utah)** declares **4 values** and **12 principles** — memorize the 4 values verbatim.
- The **Agile Manifesto values the LEFT side more** than the right — both have value, but the left is prioritized.
- **Cost of Change** grows **exponentially** in Traditional, **linearly** in Agile — this is the economic justification for Agile.
- **Choose Traditional** for: *safety-critical systems, regulatory projects, fixed-price government contracts, well-known stable requirements*.
- **Choose Agile** for: *market-driven products, evolving requirements, co-located small teams, rapid time-to-market needs*.
- **Scrum roles** = *Product Owner + Scrum Master + Development Team* (3–9 members, cross-functional, self-organizing).
- **Scrum ceremonies** = *Sprint Planning + Daily Stand-up + Sprint Review + Sprint Retrospective + Backlog Refinement* (5 events).
- **Sprint length** = typically **1–4 weeks** (most common = **2 weeks**).
- **Common exam trap:** Never claim one model is *universally* superior — justify your choice with **project characteristics**.
- **Memorize the 14-row comparison table** — it is the single most exam-relevant artefact in Module 2.
- The Waterfall model has **no backward iteration** in its *pure* form — only formal change requests re-open phases.
- **Customer collaboration** is the *single biggest behavioural difference* between the two models — Agile requires the customer to be a *team member*, not a *sign-off authority*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Agile Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGLE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Agile Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Classification of Agile Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> *Classification of Agile Methods* refers to the systematic categorization of various Agile software development methodologies based on shared characteristics such as **iteration philosophy**, **team structure**, **delivery cadence**, **quality discipline**, **customer collaboration model**, and **planning horizon**. This taxonomy enables practitioners to select, adapt, and combine methods suitable for a given project context, team size, and criticality level.

In essence, the Agile "family" is not a single uniform process — it is a **constellation** of related yet distinct methods (Scrum, XP, FDD, DSDM, Crystal, ASD, Lean, Kanban, Scrumban, etc.). Because each method was born from a different problem context, a classification framework is required to compare them on common axes.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Showroom" View

Imagine walking into a vehicle showroom. Cars are not stored randomly — they are grouped logically:

| Showroom Section | What It Groups | Agile Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| By **Fuel Type** (Petrol, Diesel, EV) | Engineering principle | Iterative vs. Iterative + Code-centric vs. Lean |
| By **Body Type** (Sedan, SUV, Hatchback) | Physical purpose | Team size (Crystal Clear, Orange, Red) |
| By **Use Case** (Family, Off-road, Racing) | User intent | Project criticality / discipline |
| By **Brand** (Toyota, BMW, Tata) | Origin / philosophy | Scrum, XP, Lean, FDD lineage |

Just as a customer chooses a car by intersecting these dimensions, an Agile coach chooses a method by intersecting **team size**, **criticality**, **discipline focus**, and **iteration style**. The classification frameworks formalize this "decision filter".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 specifically expects students to articulate the **Fowler Classification**, the **Crystal Family (Cockburn)**, the **Boehm–Turner Agile–Disciplined Spectrum**, and the **Process-Discipline Matrix** — all four are high-yield areas for the ESE.

### Why Classification Matters in Practice

- **Method selection:** Maps project constraints to the right method.
- **Method tailoring:** Allows hybrid creation (e.g., Scrumban).
- **Process improvement:** Identifies gaps when a method is mismatched to context.
- **Academic comparison:** Provides a common vocabulary for evaluation.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love a **diagram + 2-line caption** in 3-mark questions on this topic. Memorize the four primary classification schemes and the author(s) associated with each.

---

### Standard Metrics & Constants to Memorize (in **bold**)

- **Crystal Clear** = teams of **3–8 members**, low criticality (**C** for **C**lear = **C**omfortable).
- **Crystal Orange** = teams of **10–40 members**, high criticality (life-critical or mission-critical systems).
- **Crystal Red** = teams of **40–100+ members**, very high criticality (e.g., air traffic control software).
- **Sutherland's Scrum cadence:** Sprint length **2–4 weeks**.
- **XP planning game cadence:** Iteration = **1–2 weeks**; release cycle ≈ **quarterly**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Principal Classification Frameworks

The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to be conversant with **four orthogonal lenses** through which Agile methods are classified. Each lens answers a different question:

### Lens A — *Fowler's Iterative Depth Classification* (Martin Fowler, 2001)
Groups methods by **how deep the iteration goes** inside the engineering cycle.

### Lens B — *Cockburn's Crystal Family Classification* (Alistair Cockburn)
Groups methods by **team size + criticality** — the "**size-and-risk**" matrix.

### Lens C — *Boehm–Turner Agile vs. Disciplined Spectrum* (Boehm & Turner, 2003)
Places methods on a **continuum** from purely Agile to heavily plan-driven.

### Lens D — *Process-Discipline Matrix* (Combining Agile + CMMI thinking)
Classifies methods by their emphasis on **people, process, product, or project** discipline.

---

## 2.2 Fowler's Classification — The Iterative Depth Model

Fowler argued that all Agile methods iterate, but **the depth of the iteration varies**. Some iterate only on the **planning and delivery** layer, while others iterate **all the way down to the code**.

| # | Category | What Iterates | Core Philosophy | Representative Methods |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Iterative-only** (Delivery-layer iteration) | Planning, review, retrospective | Light engineering, framework on top of existing engineering practices | **Scrum** |
| 2 | **Iterative + Code-centric** | Planning + coding practices (TDD, pair prog, refactoring) | Engineering discipline is the heart of agility | **Extreme Programming (XP)** |
| 3 | **Iterative + Architecture/Design-centric** | Planning + feature modeling, domain walkthroughs | Features drive the model; design emerges feature-by-feature | **Feature-Driven Development (FDD)** |
| 4 | **Iterative + Adaptive/Learning** | Planning + speculation–collaboration–learning cycles | Embrace change as a learning loop | **Adaptive Software Development (ASD)**, **Crystal** |
| 5 | **Lean / Flow-based** | Planning + value-stream flow, WIP limits | Eliminate waste, optimize flow | **Lean Software Development**, **Kanban** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fowler's Key Insight:** Methods like Scrum *do not prescribe* engineering practices — they can be combined *with* XP. This is the philosophical basis of hybrid methods (e.g., **Scrum + XP engineering practices**).

### Why this matters
- Choosing a method whose iteration depth matches the **technical risk** of the project is critical.
- A **research/exploration project** → ASD or Crystal (learning-oriented).
- A **safety-critical avionics project** → XP + FDD (engineering rigor + architectural clarity).
- A **marketing microsite** → Scrum or Kanban (delivery-layer agility is sufficient).

---

## 2.3 Cockburn's Crystal Family — The Size-and-Criticality Grid

Alistair Cockburn (one of the Agile Manifesto signatories) proposed that **no single method fits all project sizes**. He created a **family** of methods, color-coded by team size and system criticality, sharing a **common core** (incremental delivery, communication, reflection) but differing in **ceremony weight**.

$$C_{project} = f(\text{team size}, \text{criticality}, \text{personal safety impact})$$

| Crystal Color | Team Size (people) | Criticality Level | Communication Style | Heaviness of Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Clear** | 2 – 8 | Low (Comfort / C6–C8) | Co-located, whiteboard, OSBU sessions | **Lightest** |
| **Crystal Yellow** | 10 – 20 | Medium-low (C5–C6) | Co-located + occasional workshops | Light |
| **Crystal Orange** | 20 – 40 | High (C4 – Essential money at stake) | Co-located + dedicated communication infrastructure | Medium |
| **Crystal Red** | 40 – 100+ | Very high (C3 – Life critical) | Distributed + formal communication channels | **Heaviest** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cockburn's "C" number** denotes the *consequence of failure*. C3 = loss of life, C4 = essential money, C5–C6 = comfort/discretionary money, C7–C8 = comfort. The **higher the C-number (more discomfort)**, the **lighter** the method; the **lower the C-number (life-critical)**, the **heavier** the method.

### Common Core of the Crystal Family
- **Incremental delivery** in short cycles.
- **Frequent user interaction** with on-site customer access.
- **Reflection workshops** (akin to retrospectives).
- **Personal safety** — work only the number of hours one can sustain productively.
- **Communication-rich environment** — co-location preferred, but progressively formalized as team grows.

---

## 2.4 Boehm–Turner Agile vs. Disciplined Spectrum

Barry Boehm and Richard Turner proposed that Agile is **not a binary state** — it lies on a **continuum** with disciplined (plan-driven) methods. The right choice is the **least-Agile method that still meets the project's agility needs**.

```
  ◄──────────── Plan-Driven ──────────┼────────── Agile ──────────►
  CMMI Level 5, ISO 9001, V-Model      │  Scrum, XP, FDD, DSDM
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              "Disciplined Agility"
                            (Agile with governance)
```

### Key Tenets of Boehm–Turner

1. **Agility has limits** — it suits projects with **rapidly changing requirements**, **small co-located teams**, and **developer-driven cultures**.
2. **Disciplined Agility** — Agile methods can be *augmented* with planning, governance, and documentation where the project demands it.
3. **Plan-driven suits** — large, distributed teams, hardware-software co-design, regulatory compliance, fixed-price contracts.

> [!TIP]
> The phrase "**Disciplined Agility**" is a guaranteed 2-mark KTU question. Memorize: *Agile methods retain their core, but add **discipline** through **enterprise-level governance**.*

### Where Popular Methods Sit on the Spectrum

| Method | Position on Spectrum | Lean Toward |
|---|---|---|
| **XP** | Far-Agile | Code, pair programming, TDD |
| **Scrum** | Mid-Agile | Process cadence, ceremonies |
| **FDD** | Mid-Agile | Design and modeling |
| **DSDM Atern** | Disciplined-Agile hybrid | Formal governance + iterative delivery |
| **RUP (well-trimmed)** | Near plan-driven | Architecture, use cases, milestones |
| **Waterfall / ISO** | Far plan-driven | Sequential, document-heavy |

---

## 2.5 Process-Discipline Matrix — People / Process / Product / Project

A fourth classification lens, popular in KTU viva, asks: **What is the method optimizing for?**

| Discipline Focus | What Is Optimized | Methods in this class |
|---|---|---|
| **People Discipline** | Team culture, pair work, ownership | XP, Crystal |
| **Process Discipline** | Cadence, ceremonies, transparency | Scrum, DSDM |
| **Product Discipline** | Feature quality, domain modeling, design | FDD, DSDM |
| **Project Discipline** | Value delivery, cost, schedule, contracts | DSDM, Lean, Disciplined Agile (DA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **DSDM Atern** is unique because it spans **all four** disciplines — that is why it is often called the "**most complete**" Agile method. KTU examiners may ask: *"Which Agile method is considered the most comprehensive disciplined framework?"* — Answer: **DSDM Atern** (or equivalently, **Disciplined Agile Delivery — DAD** by Scott Ambler).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> The following table is your **revision compact** for the ESE. No `|` characters inside cells.
