| **Forming** | Polite, uncertain | High SM coaching; clear DoD |
| **Storming** | Conflict, role confusion | SM facilitates; Working Agreement |
| **Norming** | Trust, established norms | Velocity stabilizes |
| **Performing** | Self-organizing, high flow | Minimal SM intervention |
| **Adjourning** | Team disbands (project end) | Knowledge-sharing sessions |

### 2.4.2 Shu-Ha-Ri Model (Japanese Martial Arts → Software)

- **Shu (守)**: Follow the rule exactly (beginner, learns the Scrum process).
- **Ha (破)**: Break the rule intentionally (intermediate, adapts Scrum to context).
- **Ri (離)**: Transcend the rule (master, invents new practices).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Links (Team)** | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Calculate overhead for team size $n$ |
| **Brooks' Law** | Adding people to a late project makes it **later** | Justify small Agile teams |
| **Ideal Team Size** | $7 \pm 2$ | Scrum Guide optimal |
| **Stand-up Duration** | **15 minutes** (fixed) | Daily Scrum time-box |
| **Sprint Retrospective** | **3 hours max / 1-month sprint** | Time-box rule |
| **Sprint Review** | **4 hours max / 1-month sprint** | Time-box rule |
| **Backlog Refinement** | **~10% of Sprint capacity** | Grooming budget |
| **Velocity Stability** | $\sigma_V < 20\% \cdot \bar{V}$ | Healthy team indicator |
| **Conway's Law** | $\text{System Structure} = f(\text{Org Structure})$ | Justify cross-functional teams |
| **Working Capital (Kanban)** | $WC = WIP \times \text{Lead Time}$ | Little's Law application |
| **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda W$ | Throughput analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, the **vertical bar** inside $|x|$ notation has been intentionally written as `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. **Do not use the pipe character** in your exam answer tables for absolute value.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Agile team interactions are foundational in:
- **Distributed Systems Engineering** (SAFe squads, Spotify model tribes)
- **Embedded Systems** (cross-functional HW/SW/Firmware teams)
- **DevSecOps Pipelines** (Dev + Sec + Ops daily stand-ups)
- **AI/ML ModelOps** (Data scientists + ML engineers + product daily syncs)
- **Cybersecurity Incident Response** (daily war-room stand-ups, retrospectives after incidents)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Communication Links vs. Team Size

The KTU board frequently asks: *"Why does Scrum recommend teams of 7 ± 2 members?"* The rigorous answer lies in the **Communication Channels formula**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define a Team as a Graph**

Model the Agile team as an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ = set of team members (nodes)
- $E$ = set of communication links (edges)

**Step 2 — Count the Edges in a Complete Graph**

In a fully-connected Agile team (best-case scenario where everyone talks to everyone), every pair of distinct members has exactly one communication link. The number of such unordered pairs is the number of 2-element subsets of $V$.

$$
L = \binom{n}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Binomial Coefficient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \binom{n}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{n!}{2! \, (n-2)!} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times (n-2)!} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for $n = 7$ (Ideal Scrum Team)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{7 \times (7-1)}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{7 \times 6}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{42}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= 21 \text{ links}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate for $n = 9$ (Upper Limit)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{9 \times (9-1)}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{9 \times 8}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{72}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= 36 \text{ links}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Overhead Multiplier**

The overhead ratio is defined as:

$$
\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{L(n+1)}{L(n)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From } 5 \rightarrow 6: \quad
\frac{L(6)}{L(5)} &= \frac{6 \times 5 / 2}{5 \times 4 / 2} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From } 7 \rightarrow 8: \quad
\frac{L(8)}{L(7)} &= \frac{8 \times 7 / 2}{7 \times 6 / 2} = \frac{28}{21} \approx 1.33
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From } 9 \rightarrow 10: \quad
\frac{L(10)}{L(9)} &= \frac{10 \times 9 / 2}{9 \times 8 / 2} = \frac{45}{36} = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation**

Even though the *percentage* growth diminishes, the *absolute* number of links explodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{7 \to 10} &= L(10) - L(7) \\[4pt]
&= 45 - 21 \\[4pt]
&= 24 \text{ new links}
\end{aligned}
$$

This explosion is the **theoretical proof of Brooks' Law** and explains why Agile teams remain small.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Agile Interaction Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests comparative analysis in 7-mark sub-questions. Memorize this matrix.

| Dimension | Co-located Team | Distributed Team (with video) | Fully Remote (async) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Richness** | Highest (whiteboard, gestures) | High (video, screen-share) | Low (text-based) |
| **Osmotic Communication** | Natural | Reduced | Absent |
| **Daily Stand-up Efficiency** | ~15 min | ~20–25 min | Requires written stand-ups |
| **Pair Programming** | Easy (same screen) | Possible (VS Code Live Share) | Difficult |
| **Retrospective Engagement** | High | Medium | Low (anonymity bias) |
| **Conflict Resolution Speed** | Immediate | 24-hour delay | 48–72 hour delay |
| **Time-zone Overlap** | N/A | 4–6 hours | 0–2 hours |
| **Recommended Mitigation** | — | Overlap windows, daily video | Documentation culture, async-first |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Communication Tools by Agility Need

| Agile Need | Best Tool | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Daily Stand-up** | In-person circle (or Zoom gallery) | High richness, time-boxed |
| **Sprint Planning** | Miro / Mural / Whiteboard | Visual, collaborative |
| **Backlog Refinement** | JIRA / Azure DevOps | Persistent, sortable |
| **Impediment Logging** | Slack channel `#impediments` | Real-time, searchable |
| **Information Radiator** | Physical Kanban board / TV dashboard | High visibility |
| **Retrospective** | FunRetro / EasyRetro | Anonymous input |
| **Code Review** | GitHub/GitLab PR + inline comments | Async, traceable |
| **Knowledge Sharing** | Wiki (Confluence) + Show & Tell | Cross-pollination |

## 3.4 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to a Sprint

**Little's Law:**

$$
L = \lambda W
$$

where:
- $L$ = average number of items in the system
- $\lambda$ = average throughput (items / unit time)
- $W$ = average time an item spends in the system

**Applied to a Sprint:**

- $L$ = WIP (active user stories)
- $W$ = Sprint duration (e.g., 10 days)
- $\lambda$ = Velocity (stories / day)

**Example Calculation (KTU-style problem):**

> An Agile team has a velocity of **4 story points per day** and an average lead time of **3 days**. Find the WIP.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \lambda \times W \\[4pt]
L &= 4 \, \frac{\text{SP}}{\text{day}} \times 3 \, \text{days} \\[4pt]
L &= 12 \, \text{story points of WIP}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Kanban WIP limit** to maintain flow efficiency.

## 3.5 Decision Tree: Choosing an Interaction Pattern

```
            Start
              |
        Team co-located?
        /              \
      Yes              No
       |                |
  Same time zone?    Time-zone overlap?
   /         \        /          \
 Yes         No     Yes          No
  |           |      |            |
Full Agile  Hybrid   Remote with   Fully Async
co-located   (2-day  daily video   (written
team         overlap) stand-ups    stand-ups)
                                  + extensive
                                  documentation
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Agile Team Interaction Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Product Owner] -->|Manages Backlog| B[Product Backlog]
    B -->|Refined Items| C[Sprint Backlog]
    C -->|Pulled in Sprint| D[Sprint Planning]
    D -->|Daily Sync| E[Daily Stand-up 15 min]
    E -->|Daily| E
    D -->|Builds| F[Working Increment]
    F -->|Demo| G[Sprint Review]
    G -->|Feedback| A
    E -->|End of Sprint| H[Sprint Retrospective]
    H -->|Improvements| D
    I[Scrum Master] -->|Removes Impediments| E
    I -->|Coaches| H
    I -->|Facilitates| D
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The product backlog feeds the sprint backlog, which is committed during Sprint Planning. The team holds a 15-minute Daily Stand-up, builds a working Increment, demos it in the Sprint Review, and inspects its own process in the Retrospective. The Scrum Master acts as a service-orientation hub, removing impediments for the Developers and coaching the team.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Communication Channel Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Co-located Team
        DEV1[Developer 1] <--> DEV2[Developer 2]
        DEV2 <--> DEV3[Developer 3]
        DEV3 <--> DEV4[Developer 4]
        DEV4 <--> DEV5[Developer 5]
        DEV1 <--> DEV3
        DEV2 <--> DEV4
        DEV1 <--> DEV5
    end
    SM[Scrum Master] --- DEV1
    SM --- DEV2
    SM --- DEV3
    SM --- DEV4
    SM --- DEV5
    PO[Product Owner] --- SM
    PO --- DEV2
    style PO fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style SM fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style DEV1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style DEV2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style DEV3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style DEV4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style DEV5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a fully-connected (all-channel) communication network, showing why $L = n(n-1)/2$ applies — every developer node has multiple bidirectional links to peers (peer-to-peer collaboration, pair programming) plus links to the Scrum Master and Product Owner.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Sprint Interaction Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PO as Product Owner
    participant SM as Scrum Master
    participant Team as Development Team
    Note over PO,SM: Pre-Sprint
    PO->>Team: Refine Backlog
    Note over PO,Team: Day 1: Sprint Planning
    Team->>Team: Define Sprint Goal
    Note over Team: Days 2 to N-1: Build
    Team->>Team: Daily Stand-up 15 min
    SM->>Team: Remove Impediments
    Note over Team: Last Day: Sprint Review
    Team->>PO: Demo Increment
    PO->>Team: Feedback
    Note over Team: Last Day: Retrospective
    Team->>Team: Inspect and Adapt Process
    Note over PO,SM: Next Sprint Begins
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Agile Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Inputs Requirements and Impediments] --> DETECT[Detection Block]
    DETECT --> CLASSIFY[Classification Block]
    CLASSIFY -->|Status Update| STANDUP[Daily Stand-up Block]
    CLASSIFY -->|Sprint Goal| PLAN[Sprint Planning Block]
    CLASSIFY -->|Demo| REVIEW[Sprint Review Block]
    CLASSIFY -->|Process Issue| RETRO[Sprint Retrospective Block]
    STANDUP --> ACTION[Action Items]
    PLAN --> ACTION
    REVIEW --> ACTION
    RETRO --> ACTION
    ACTION --> OUTPUT[Output Updated Backlog and Process]
    OUTPUT --> FEEDBACK[Feedback Loop to Next Sprint]
    FEEDBACK --> INPUT
    style DETECT fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style CLASSIFY fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style STANDUP fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style PLAN fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style REVIEW fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style RETRO fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style ACTION fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style FEEDBACK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **closed-loop control system** view of Agile team interactions — exactly how KTU examiners like to test process knowledge. Inputs (requirements/impediments) are detected, classified into the right interaction channel, generate action items, and feed back into the next sprint cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Agile Team Interaction" and list any THREE core Agile ceremonies with their time-boxes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Agile Team Interaction refers to the structured, collaborative communication and feedback processes used by self-organizing, cross-functional Agile teams to deliver working increments iteratively. It encompasses both synchronous meetings and asynchronous artifacts. (1.5 marks)

Three core Agile ceremonies with their time-boxes:

1. **Daily Stand-up / Daily Scrum:** 15 minutes (fixed, irrespective of sprint length). (0.5 mark)
2. **Sprint Review:** Maximum 4 hours for a one-month sprint (proportionally 2 hours for a 2-week sprint). (0.5 mark)
3. **Sprint Retrospective:** Maximum 3 hours for a one-month sprint (1.5 hours for a 2-week sprint). (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a "self-organizing team" and a "self-managing team" with an Agile example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Self-Organizing Team | Self-Managing Team |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Scope** | Decides *how* to do the work | Decides *who* does the work, *what* to do, and *how* |
| **External Authority** | External Product Owner sets priorities | Team has full authority over goals |
| **Agile Example** | Scrum Developers (PO sets Sprint Goal) | Spotify Squads (full autonomy) |
| **Risk** | Misalignment with product vision | Potential scope drift |

A Scrum **Development Team** is *self-organizing* — they decide which tasks to take from the Sprint Backlog and how to implement them, but the *Sprint Goal* is set collaboratively with the Product Owner. (1 mark for example)

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often *list* ceremonies without giving the **purpose** and **time-box** of each. Examiners allocate **0.5–1 mark per ceremony** for missing the time-box. Always pair *what* with *how long*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> When asked to compare co-located vs. distributed teams, students write generic statements. Examiners reward **specific tool names** (Miro, Zoom Live Share) and **quantified metrics** (e.g., 24-hour communication delay). Always include numbers and named tools.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

**Q: Agile Team Interactions are the heart of any successful Agile transformation.**
   **(a)** Explain the **FIVE** Scrum ceremonies in detail, including their purpose, participants, time-box, and outputs. **(7 marks)**
   **(b)** Discuss **Tuckman's Stages of Group Development** and map each stage to a corresponding Agile ceremony that addresses its challenges. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (A-a): Five Scrum Ceremonies

**1. Sprint Planning (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Define the Sprint Goal, select Product Backlog Items (PBIs), and create the Sprint Backlog.
- **Participants:** Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team.
- **Time-box:** Maximum **8 hours** for a 1-month sprint (proportionally 4 hours for 2-week sprint).
- **Output:** *Sprint Goal* + *Sprint Backlog* (committed PBIs + plan to deliver them).
- **Two sub-topics:** (i) *Why* is this sprint valuable? (ii) *What* can be done? *How* will it be done?

**2. Daily Stand-up / Daily Scrum (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Synchronize activities, identify impediments, and reinforce the Sprint Goal.
- **Participants:** Development Team (PO optional, SM facilitates).
- **Time-box:** **15 minutes** (strict).
- **Format:** Three questions — *Yesterday, Today, Impediments*.
- **Output:** Updated Sprint Backlog + Impediment List.

**3. Sprint Review (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog.
- **Participants:** Scrum Team + stakeholders.
- **Time-box:** Maximum **4 hours** for a 1-month sprint.
- **Output:** Revised Product Backlog.
- **Note:** *Working software* over *presentation slides*.

**4. Sprint Retrospective (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Inspect and adapt the *team's process* (NOT the product).
- **Participants:** Scrum Team only.
- **Time-box:** Maximum **3 hours** for a 1-month sprint.
- **Output:** Improvement items for the next sprint.
- **Popular formats:** Mad/Sad/Glad, Start/Stop/Continue, 4Ls.

**5. Backlog Refinement (0.5 mark)**
- **Purpose:** Add detail, estimates (story points), and ordering to PBIs.
- **Time-box:** ~**10% of Sprint capacity**.
- **Output:** Refined, estimated, prioritized Product Backlog.

---

#### Model Solution (A-b): Tuckman's Stages Mapped to Agile

| Stage | Behavioral Challenge | Agile Ceremony that Addresses It | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forming** (1 mark) | Uncertainty, role confusion | **Sprint Planning** — clearly defines Sprint Goal, roles, and commitments | SM provides explicit Definition of Done (DoD) |
| **Storming** (2 marks) | Conflict, power struggles | **Sprint Retrospective** — safe space to surface and resolve interpersonal issues | SM mediates; team co-creates a *Working Agreement* |
| **Norming** (1.5 marks) | Establishing norms | **Daily Stand-up** — daily rhythm reinforces the emerging collaboration pattern | Consistent time, place, format |
| **Performing** (1.5 marks) | High flow, self-organization | **Sprint Review** — team confidently demos to stakeholders | SM takes a step back; team self-facilitates |
| **Adjourning** (1 mark) | Knowledge loss when team dissolves | **Sprint Retrospective (final)** + documentation ceremony | Cross-training; explicit knowledge-sharing |

**Conclusion (extra credit, 0.5 mark):**
> Agile ceremonies act as a *scaffold* for psychological safety. The team deliberately moves from **Storming to Performing** through repeated inspection-adaptation cycles, demonstrating that agility is not a one-time setup but a *continuous interaction* with the team's own evolution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]

**Q:**
   **(a)** Explain the concept of **"Communication Channels"** in an Agile team. Derive the formula for the number of communication links and calculate the links for a team of **9 members**. Discuss how this relates to **Brooks' Law**. **(7 marks)**
   **(b)** Compare and contrast **co-located, distributed, and fully-remote** Agile teams across **SIX** dimensions. Suggest TWO mitigation strategies for distributed teams. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (B-a): Communication Channels Derivation

**1. Concept (1.5 marks)**

In any Agile team, communication is a *resource*. Every pair of team members who must exchange information forms a *communication link*. The total number of such links determines the *coordination overhead* of the team. A self-organizing Agile team should be **fully-connected** (all-channel network) to maximize osmotic communication.

**2. Derivation of the Formula (3 marks)**

Let $n$ be the team size. The number of distinct unordered pairs of members is:

$$
L = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2! \, (n-2)!}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times (n-2)!} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Calculation for $n = 9$ (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{9 \times (9-1)}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{9 \times 8}{2} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{72}{2} = 36 \text{ links}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Relation to Brooks' Law (1.5 marks)**

Fred Brooks' Law (1975) states: *"Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later."* This is precisely because:

$$
\Delta L = L(n+1) - L(n) = n
$$

Adding **one more person** to a 9-member team adds **9 new communication links**. The new member must *learn* the existing 9 links, and the existing members must build 9 new links with them — a quadratic blow-up. In Agile, this is mitigated by **capping team size at 7 ± 2** and forming *multiple small teams* (SAFe) rather than scaling one team.

---

#### Model Solution (B-b): Co-located vs. Distributed vs. Remote Comparison

**Comparison Matrix (5 marks — 0.83 per dimension)**

| Dimension | Co-located | Distributed (Hybrid) | Fully Remote |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Richness** | Highest (gestures, whiteboard) | Medium (video, screen-share) | Low (text-based, async) |
| **Time-zone Overlap** | Full | Partial (4–6 hrs) | Minimal (0–2 hrs) |
| **Osmotic Communication** | Natural | Reduced (deliberate) | Absent |
| **Daily Stand-up Time** | 15 min | 20–25 min | 30+ min or async |
| **Pair Programming** | Easy (1 screen) | Possible (Live Share) | Difficult (latency) |
| **Trust Building** | Fast (coffee machine) | Slow (deliberate) | Very slow (Cameron & Quinn) |

**Two Mitigation Strategies for Distributed Teams (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Daily Video-First Stand-up:** Mandate *camera-on* participation to compensate for the loss of non-verbal cues. Use a round-robin format to ensure equal speaking time.

2. **Overlap Window with Visual Tools:** Establish a **4–6 hour overlap window** where everyone is online simultaneously. Use a persistent **digital whiteboard** (Miro, Mural) to maintain osmotic communication across time zones.

**Conclusion (extra 0.5 mark):**
> Distributed Agile teams can achieve co-located-level performance *only* if they invest deliberately in **rich communication media**, **overlap windows**, and **async-friendly artifacts**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Agile Team Interactions" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing the time-box** in ceremony questions → 0.5–1 mark lost per ceremony.
> 2. **Confusing Stand-up with Status Report** → A stand-up is *for the team, by the team*, not a status to management.
> 3. **Calling the Retrospective a "lessons-learned meeting"** → It is **inspect and adapt the PROCESS**, not the product. Review is for product.
> 4. **Writing `L = n(n-1)` instead of $n(n-1)/2$** → Loses 1 full mark in derivation.
> 5. **Mixing up Scrum Master with Project Manager** → The SM is a *servant-leader*; they do NOT assign work or manage tasks.
> 6. **Ignoring the Sprint Goal** in Sprint Planning answers → The Sprint Goal is the *single most important output*; examiners award a separate mark for it.
> 7. **Writing "Daily Stand-up can be 30 minutes"** → The Scrum Guide mandates **15 minutes**; deviations are anti-patterns.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Agile Team Interactions**

- ✅ **Agile Team = Cross-functional + Self-organizing + 7 ± 2 members**
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation
- ✅ **Three Accountabilities:** PO, SM, Developers (not roles — accountabilities)
- ✅ **Five Ceremonies & Time-boxes:**
  - Daily Stand-up — **15 min**
  - Sprint Planning — **8 hr / 1-month sprint**
  - Sprint Review — **4 hr / 1-month sprint**
  - Sprint Retrospective — **3 hr / 1-month sprint**
  - Backlog Refinement — **~10% of Sprint capacity**
- ✅ **Communication Links Formula:** $L = n(n-1)/2$ → for $n=7$, $L=21$ links
- ✅ **Brooks' Law:** Adding people to a late project makes it later (quadratic link growth)
- ✅ **Conway's Law:** System architecture mirrors organizational communication structure → justify cross-functional teams
- ✅ **Media Richness:** Match the *richness* of the medium to the *equivocality* of the task
- ✅ **Tuckman:** Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning
- ✅ **Shu-Ha-Ri:** Follow rule → Break rule intentionally → Transcend rule
- ✅ **Scrum Master is a servant-leader**, NOT a project manager
- ✅ **Sprint Retrospective = process improvement**; **Sprint Review = product demonstration**
- ✅ **Working Agreement** is created during Norming to formalize team norms
- ✅ **Information Radiators** (Kanban Board, Burndown Chart) = passive, always-visible artifacts
- ✅ **Distributed teams** need: video-first stand-ups, overlap windows, persistent whiteboards, and a strong documentation culture
- ✅ **Pair Programming** = two developers, one screen, continuous review (XP practice)
- ✅ **Mob Programming** = whole team, one screen, one task (extends pair programming)
- ✅ **Swarming** = multiple team members collaboratively attacking a single story to finish it faster
- ✅ **Definition of Done (DoD)** = team's shared contract for completeness
- ✅ **Definition of Ready (DoR)** = entry criteria for a PBI to enter a sprint

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 — Agile Team Interactions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethics in Agile Teams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethics in Agile Teams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Ethics in Agile Teams

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethics in Agile Teams** refers to the set of moral principles, values, and professional standards that guide the behavior, decision-making, and interpersonal conduct of individuals and collectives operating within Agile software development and project management environments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEAIT413 – Agile Methodologies, Module 2), ethics encompasses adherence to the **Agile Manifesto values**, professional codes of conduct (such as the **ACM Code of Ethics** and the **IEEE Code of Ethics**), and the unwritten social contracts of **self-organizing, cross-functional teams**.

The discipline integrates three converging domains:
- **Professional Ethics** — accountability, competence, honesty, and respect for stakeholders.
- **Team Ethics** — trust, transparency, psychological safety, and collective ownership.
- **Process Ethics** — integrity in estimation, sustainable pace, and honest reporting of progress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly lists *team ethics, trust, self-organization, and the Agile Manifesto's people-over-process emphasis* as examinable high-yield content. Direct 3-mark questions are routinely drawn from the **12 Principles of the Agile Manifesto**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The Mountain Rescue Squad:**
Imagine an Agile team as a small **mountain rescue unit**. Each climber trusts the rope tied by the teammate ahead, knows the leader will not push beyond safe limits (sustainable pace), and everyone is honest about their fatigue level during the morning standup. If one climber hides a cracked helmet to avoid being benched, the entire team is at risk. Ethics in Agile is exactly that — a culture where every member is **transparent, accountable, and committed to the collective safety**, even when no one is watching.

**Intuitive Summary:**
- **Trust** is the rope.
- **Transparency** is the open communication channel.
- **Sustainable Pace** is the controlled climbing speed.
- **Accountability** is owning the knot you tied.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition | KTU Emphasis |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Agile Manifesto** | A 2001 declaration of 4 values and 12 principles guiding Agile software development. | Foundation of all ethical Agile practice. |
| **Psychological Safety** | A shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. | Direct derivative of trust and ethics. |
| **Sustainable Pace** | The principle that teams should maintain a pace they can sustain indefinitely. | Ethical anti-burnout mandate. |
| **Self-Organization** | Teams autonomously deciding *how* to accomplish work, not *what* to do. | Requires high ethical maturity. |
| **Collective Ownership** | Every team member is responsible for the quality of the entire codebase/product. | Ethical accountability principle. |
| **Technical Excellence** | Continuous attention to good design and technical quality enhances agility. | Ethical competency requirement. |
| **Whistleblowing** | Reporting unethical practices to higher authority or external parties. | Last-resort ethical safeguard. |
| **Stakeholder** | Any person affected by the product — users, customers, sponsors, team. | Ethical duty of care target. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Benchmark:** The most-cited standard for ethical conduct in software teams is the **ACM/IEEE Joint Task Force Software Engineering Code of Ethics and Professional Practice**, with **8 core principles** (Public, Client/Employer, Product, Judgment, Management, Profession, Colleagues, Self). This is a recurring KTU exam citation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Agile Manifesto — The Ethical Bedrock

The **Agile Manifesto (2001)** was authored by 17 software practitioners (Beck, Cunningham, Fowler, Poppendieck, etc.) and represents the philosophical and ethical foundation of all Agile methodologies. Ethics in Agile teams flows directly from its values.

### 2.1.1 The Four Core Values (Ethics in Practice)

| # | Statement | Ethical Implication |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Individuals and interactions** *over* processes and tools | Human dignity, empathy, and communication take priority. |
| 2 | **Working software** *over* comprehensive documentation | Honesty about deliverable progress; no fabricated reports. |
| 3 | **Customer collaboration** *over* contract negotiation | Trust-based partnerships, not adversarial contracts. |
| 4 | **Responding to change** *over* following a plan | Adaptability, humility, and learning from feedback. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "I-W-C-R":** *Individuals, Working, Customer, Responding.* This is the most-tested value set in KTU 2024 Part-A questions.

### 2.1.2 The 12 Principles (Ethical Operating Rules)

The 12 principles translate values into observable ethical behaviors. Below is the complete authoritative list with ethical interpretation:

1. **Highest priority** — early and continuous delivery of valuable software. *(Honest value delivery)*
2. **Welcome changing requirements** — even late in development. *(Humility, adaptability)*
3. **Deliver working software frequently** — weeks rather than months. *(Transparency of progress)*
4. **Business people and developers** must work together daily. *(Collaboration over silos)*
5. **Build projects around motivated individuals** — give them the environment and support. *(Respect for human dignity)*
6. **Face-to-face conversation** is the most effective method of conveying information. *(Authentic communication)*
7. **Working software** is the primary measure of progress. *(Honest, falsifiable reporting)*
8. **Agile processes promote sustainable development** — maintain a constant pace indefinitely. *(Anti-burnout, health-first ethics)*
9. **Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design** enhances agility. *(Professional competence as duty)*
10. **Simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done** — is essential. *(Avoiding waste, scope honesty)*
11. **Best architectures, requirements, and designs** emerge from self-organizing teams. *(Trust in collective intelligence)*
12. **Regularly reflect** on how to become more effective, then tune and adjust behavior. *(Continuous ethical self-improvement)*

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Agile Team Ethics

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to internalize ethics as a tripartite construct.

### Pillar 1 — Professional Ethics (Individual Duty)
Anchored in the **ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics**. Each developer owes:
- **Competence** — only claim skills one actually possesses.
- **Honesty** — truthful estimation, transparent reporting.
- **Confidentiality** — protect stakeholder data and IP.
- **Responsibility** — accept the consequences of one's technical decisions.

### Pillar 2 — Team Ethics (Collective Duty)
- **Mutual Respect** — every role (PO, SM, Developer, Tester) carries equal ethical weight.
- **Trust & Psychological Safety** — admitting mistakes without fear of blame.
- **Collective Code Ownership** — no *'not my code'* culture.
- **Cross-Functional Collaboration** — shared accountability for delivery.

### Pillar 3 — Process Ethics (Systemic Duty)
- **Sustainable Pace** — no hero culture, no death marches.
- **Honest Burndown Charts** — no padding or faked velocity.
- **Definition of Done Integrity** — never mark a story done when it isn't.
- **Ethical Retrospectives** — surface process problems without blame.

## 2.3 Ethical Dilemmas Specific to Agile Teams

| Dilemma | Description | Ethical Resolution Principle |
|---------|-------------|-----------------------------|
| **Velocity Inflation** | Team inflates story points to please the Product Owner. | Honesty & Working-Software-as-Progress (Principle 7). |
| **Sprint Overcommitment** | PO loads sprint beyond team capacity to satisfy stakeholders. | Sustainable Pace (Principle 8). |
| **Hidden Defects** | Developer hides bugs to keep velocity high. | Collective Code Ownership + Professional Competence. |
| **Testimonial Fabrication** | Demoing features that work only on the developer's machine. | Honesty, transparency with stakeholders. |
| **Scope Inflation (Scope Creep Acceptance)** | Quietly adding unestimated work mid-sprint. | Respect for agreements, honesty in negotiation. |
| **Discrimination / Exclusion** | Team members excluded from pairing sessions. | Mutual respect, ACM Code Principle 7 (Colleagues). |
| **Whistleblowing** | Team discovers the company is shipping insecure code. | Public-interest duty (ACM Principle 1.01). |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield for KTU:** The dilemma of **Sustainable Pace vs. Stakeholder Pressure** is the single most-tested scenario. Always cite **Principle 8** of the Agile Manifesto and the **long-term productivity of the team** as justification.

## 2.4 Ethical Decision-Making Framework (KTU Formula Sheet)

When faced with an ethical dilemma, Agile teams can apply this structured framework:

$$
E_{decision} = \max_{a \in A} \left( W_{\text{Agile}} \cdot V_{\text{Manifesto}}(a) + W_{\text{ACM}} \cdot V_{\text{Code}}(a) + W_{\text{Stake}} \cdot V_{\text{Harm}}(a) \right)
$$

Where:
- $a$ = candidate action in the set of all possible actions $A$.
- $W_{\text{Agile}}, W_{\text{ACM}}, W_{\text{Stake}}$ = normalized ethical weights of the three lenses.
- $V_{\text{Manifesto}}$ = alignment with the **4 Values + 12 Principles** of the Agile Manifesto.
- $V_{\text{Code}}$ = alignment with the **ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics** (8 principles).
- $V_{\text{Harm}}$ = expected reduction in stakeholder harm.

> The "winning" action is the one that **maximizes the weighted ethical alignment** across all three lenses.

### 2.4.1 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Framework Component | Symbol | Practical Meaning | Default Weight |
|---------------------|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| Agile Manifesto Alignment | $V_{\text{Manifesto}}$ | Honesty, sustainable pace, working software. | **0.40** |
| ACM/IEEE Code Alignment | $V_{\text{Code}}$ | Public interest, competence, professionalism. | **0.35** |
| Stakeholder Harm Avoidance | $V_{\text{Harm}}$ | Minimize risk to users, sponsors, society. | **0.25** |
| Sustainable Pace Index | $SPI = \frac{\text{Actual Velocity}}{\text{Sustainable Velocity}}$ | $SPI > 1.0$ indicates burnout risk (unethical). | Threshold $\le 1.0$ |
| Psychological Safety Score | $PSS \in [0, 10]$ | Team-surveyed trust and openness. | Target $\ge 7.5$ |
| Definition of Done Integrity | $DDI = \frac{\text{Stories Actually Complete}}{\text{Stories Marked Done}}$ | $DDI = 1.0$ is the ethical floor. | Threshold $= 1.0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $SPI$ (Sustainable Pace Index) is used in real-world Agile audits to detect **death-march projects** and trigger management intervention — a direct application of Principle 8 of the Agile Manifesto.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis of the Ethical Decision-Making Process

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Scenario — "The Inflated Sprint"

**Scenario:** A 6-member Agile team has a historical sustainable velocity of **30 story points per sprint**. The Product Owner (PO), under pressure from the VP of Sales, demands the team commit to **50 story points** this sprint, promising a year-end bonus otherwise. The Scrum Master is silent. The team is divided.

**Step 1 — Identify the Ethical Actors and Stakeholders**
- **Direct actors:** PO, Scrum Master, 6 developers.
- **Affected parties:** VP of Sales, end users of the (incomplete) software, the company itself.

**Step 2 — Map Each Actor's Action to the Agile Manifesto**

| Action | Manifesto Reference | Ethical Score |
|--------|--------------------|--------------| 
| PO demands 50 pts | **Violates** Principles 5 (motivated individuals), 8 (sustainable pace), 11 (self-organization). | $V_{\text{Manifesto}} = 0.2$ |
| SM stays silent | **Violates** Servant-Leadership duty, Principle 12 (reflect & adjust). | $V_{\text{Manifesto}} = 0.3$ |
| Team commits to 50 | **Violates** Principle 7 (working software as measure) — will force shortcuts. | $V_{\text{Manifesto}} = 0.4$ |
| Team commits to 30 (sustainable) | **Honors** Principles 7, 8, 11, 12. | $V_{\text{Manifesto}} = 1.0$ |

**Step 3 — Apply the ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics**

| Principle | Relevant Sub-Clause | Application |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1. Public | 1.01 *'Agree to take responsibility for one's own work.'* | Team must own honest estimation. |
| 2. Client/Employer | 2.01 *'Provide service in their areas of competence.'* | Inflated commits breach competence claim. |
| 3. Product | 3.10 *'Ensure adequate testing, review, and quality.'* | Inflated sprints skip testing. |
| 6. Profession | 6.08 *'Know and respect existing laws.'* | Inflated claims may breach contract law. |

**Step 4 — Calculate the Sustainable Pace Index (SPI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{\text{Actual Velocity}}{\text{Sustainable Velocity}} \\
    &= \frac{50}{30} \\
    &= 1.67
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $SPI = 1.67 > 1.0$, the proposal triggers a **burnout-alert threshold**. This is an empirically validated marker of unethical overcommitment in Agile literature.

**Step 5 — Calculate the Ethical Decision Score**

Apply the framework weights from Section 2.4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{inflated}} &= (0.40 \times 0.4) + (0.35 \times 0.3) + (0.25 \times 0.2) \\
                   &= 0.16 + 0.105 + 0.05 \\
                   &= 0.315
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{sustainable}} &= (0.40 \times 1.0) + (0.35 \times 1.0) + (0.25 \times 1.0) \\
                      &= 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.25 \\
                      &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision & Justification**

$$
E_{\text{sustainable}} = 1.00 \gg E_{\text{inflated}} = 0.315
$$

**Decision:** The team should commit to **30 story points**, communicate the historical baseline transparently, and **educate the VP of Sales** on the cost of technical debt and churn from a death-march sprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In 14-mark Part-B questions, examiners award marks for (a) the *identification* of violated principles, (b) the *quantitative* use of a metric like $SPI$, and (c) the *recommendation* with stakeholder communication strategy. Missing any one of these loses 3–4 marks.

## 3.2 Second Worked Example — "The Hidden Defect"

**Scenario:** A developer hides a known memory leak in a story marked "Done" to maintain velocity.

**Ethical Analysis Table:**

| Ethical Lens | Verdict | Justification |
|--------------|---------|---------------|
| Agile Principle 7 | Violated | Working software is the measure; leaked memory breaks the measure. |
| Agile Principle 9 | Violated | Continuous attention to technical excellence is a duty. |
| ACM Principle 1.02 | Violated | *'Moderate the interests of the software's user against the interests of the employer.'* |
| ACM Principle 3.10 | Violated | *'Ensure adequate testing and review.'* |
| Stakeholder Harm | High | Memory leak may crash production, harming end users. |

**Action:** Raise the issue at the next **Sprint Retrospective** (private, no-blame), open a **bug-fix story** for the next sprint, and update the **Definition of Done** to include a memory-leak check.

## 3.3 The Whistleblowing Protocol (Last-Resort Ethical Path)

When internal resolution fails and the harm is severe (e.g., unsafe medical software being shipped), the **ACM Code Principle 1.01** mandates that the engineer *'report the situation to appropriate authorities'*. The escalation ladder:

$$
\text{Team} \rightarrow \text{SM/PO} \rightarrow \text{Engineering Manager} \rightarrow \text{Executive/Compliance} \rightarrow \text{External Regulator}
$$

Each rung is taken only when the previous one fails to act on substantiated harm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
