# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Agile means no documentation." Critically evaluate this statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The statement is **incorrect** and reflects a common misunderstanding of the Agile Manifesto.

1. The Agile Manifesto values **"working software over comprehensive documentation"** — this is a *prioritization*, not an *exclusion*. The full statement continues: *"While there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more."*
2. Agile documentation is **just-in-time, lightweight, and value-driven**. It is not eliminated; it is **streamlined**.
3. Examples of essential Agile documentation include: Product Backlog, User Stories, Sprint Backlog, Burndown Charts, Acceptance Criteria, and Definition of Done.

**Conclusion:** Agile reduces unnecessary documentation but **preserves purposeful, audience-relevant documentation** that supports delivery and knowledge continuity. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the 3 C's of a User Story as proposed by Ron Jeffries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ron Jeffries described User Stories using the **3 C's** model:

1. **Card** (1 mark) — A physical or digital index card containing a brief sentence in the format: *"As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]."* It enforces brevity and serves as a placeholder.
2. **Conversation** (1 mark) — The verbal/collaborative discussion between the Product Owner, developers, and stakeholders that clarifies the story's details. This conversation captures intent that the card alone cannot.
3. **Confirmation** (1 mark) — The Acceptance Criteria and tests that confirm the story has been implemented correctly. They translate the conversation into verifiable, binary pass/fail conditions.

Together, the 3 C's ensure that stories are **concise, well-discussed, and testable**.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the different types of Agile documentation artifacts used in a Scrum project. Explain the role and ownership of each. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What is the INVEST criteria for good User Stories? Explain each component with an example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

Scrum documentation artifacts can be classified into three categories:

**1. Product Documentation (Owned by Product Owner) [3 marks]**

- **Product Vision Statement:** A concise, future-oriented statement describing the long-term goal of the product. E.g., *"Enable every small merchant in India to accept digital payments in under 60 seconds."*
- **Product Backlog:** An ordered, emergent list of everything that might be needed in the product. Items at the top are refined and ready; items at the bottom are coarse.
- **User Stories:** Short, user-centric descriptions of desired functionality, written from the user's perspective using the Connextra template.

**2. Sprint Documentation (Owned by Development Team) [2 marks]**

- **Sprint Backlog:** The set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint, plus a plan for delivering them and the Sprint Goal. Highly tactical and changes daily.
- **Sprint Goal:** A single objective that gives the team focus for the Sprint.

**3. Progress & Quality Documentation (Owned by Scrum Master / Team) [2 marks]**

- **Burndown/Burnup Charts:** Visual representations of work remaining (or completed) over the Sprint duration.
- **Definition of Done (DoD):** A shared checklist that defines what "complete" means (e.g., code reviewed, tested, documented, deployed).
- **Increment Documentation / Release Notes:** Brief summaries of features released in each Sprint for stakeholders.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**INVEST Criteria** is a mnemonic for evaluating the quality of User Stories, proposed by **Bill Wake** (2003).

| **Criterion** | **Meaning** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **I — Independent** | Story can be developed without depending on another story | ❌ "After login, user can see dashboard" depends on login story. ✅ "User can view dashboard with mock data" is independent. |
| **N — Negotiable** | Story is a discussion placeholder, not a rigid contract | ❌ "Use PostgreSQL" is too prescriptive. ✅ "Persist user data with high availability" is negotiable. |
| **V — Valuable** | Story delivers value to a user or business | E.g., *"As a shopper, I can filter products by price so that I find budget items quickly."* — value to the shopper. |
| **E — Estimable** | Team can estimate effort with reasonable accuracy | If a story is too vague, it must be refined or split via a *spike*. |
| **S — Small** | Story fits within a single Sprint | If not, it should be decomposed into smaller stories. |
| **T — Testable** | Clear pass/fail criteria exist | Acceptance Criteria: *"Given a logged-in user, when they click 'Logout', then they are redirected to the home page within 2 seconds."* |

**Conclusion:** The INVEST criteria ensure that stories are **high-quality, executable, and deliver incremental value**, forming the foundation of Agile documentation at the requirement level. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of "Just-in-Time" documentation in Agile. Why is it preferred over upfront comprehensive documentation? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the documentation anti-patterns commonly observed in Agile teams. Suggest strategies to overcome them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition of Just-in-Time (JIT) Documentation [2 marks]**
JIT documentation is the practice of creating documentation **at the moment it is needed, by the people who need it, in the minimum useful form**. It contrasts with traditional upfront documentation, which attempts to capture all knowledge before any code is written.

**2. Why JIT is preferred — Economic Justification [3 marks]**
- **Value decay:** Documents lose relevance as the system evolves. The mathematical model $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ shows that early-written docs have high obsolescence cost.
- **Reduced waste:** Less time spent on docs nobody reads.
- **Faster feedback:** Documentation is created with full context of the actual solution, not hypothetical ones.
- **Better accuracy:** Written by the implementer immediately after building, reducing recall errors.

**3. Conditions where JIT is NOT appropriate [2 marks]**
- **Regulatory/Compliance domains** (e.g., medical devices, aviation) — docs must be produced upfront.
- **High-risk architectural decisions** — need ADRs written early.
- **Long-lived stable systems** where $\lambda$ is very small.

**Conclusion:** JIT documentation balances agility with accountability, prioritizing **value and timeliness** over completeness.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Common Documentation Anti-Patterns in Agile:**

| **Anti-Pattern** | **Description** | **Remedy** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Documentation Theater (1.5 marks) | Producing docs to satisfy auditors, not users | Define audience first; measure doc effectiveness via usage |
| 2. Upfront Documentation Trap (1.5 marks) | Spending the first sprint on exhaustive specs | Apply JIT principle; defer docs to last responsible moment |
| 3. Living Document Decay (1 mark) | Once-accurate docs that are now stale | Assign doc owners; schedule doc-review in each retro |
| 4. One-Person Documentation (1 mark) | Single point of failure | Rotate doc ownership; pair-write important docs |
| 5. Tool-Driven Documentation (1 mark) | Choosing Confluence/Notion before content strategy | Define doc taxonomy and lifecycle FIRST, then pick tool |
| 6. Code-as-Doc Fallacy (1 mark) | Assuming code is self-documenting | Document *why* (decisions, context), not *what* (already in code) |

**Holistic Strategy:** Treat documentation as a **first-class deliverable** in the Definition of Done, integrate it into the workflow, and continuously prune outdated content.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Writing "Agile = No documentation"** — You will be **awarded 0 marks** for the question. Always clarify that documentation is *reduced*, not *removed*.
> 2. **Listing artifacts without explaining their purpose or owner** — Examiners want the *'why'* and *'who'* behind each artifact. Memorize ownership.
> 3. **Conflating INVEST and 3 C's** — They are **different models** by different authors (Bill Wake vs. Ron Jeffries). Don't mix them in one answer.
> 4. **Forgetting compliance exceptions** — Any question about "less documentation" must mention that **regulated industries** still require comprehensive documentation.
> 5. **Skipping examples in the User Story format** — Always include a worked Connextra-format example: *"As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]."*

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **Agile Manifesto 1st Value:** *Working software > Comprehensive documentation* — but docs are still valued.
- ☐ **Just-in-Time (JIT) documentation** is the core Agile principle — write at the last responsible moment.
- ☐ **3 C's of User Stories (Ron Jeffries):** Card, Conversation, Confirmation.
- ☐ **INVEST Criteria (Bill Wake):** Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable.
- ☐ **User Story Format:** *"As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]."*
- ☐ **Scrum Artifacts:** Product Backlog (PO), Sprint Backlog (Team), Increment (Team).
- ☐ **Burndown Chart:** Tracks remaining work over time; updated daily.
- ☐ **Definition of Done (DoD):** Shared team checklist for "complete" work.
- ☐ **Definition of Ready (DoR):** Checklist for starting work (e.g., estimated, AC written, dependencies clear).
- ☐ **Documentation Anti-Patterns:** Theater, Upfront Trap, Decay, One-Person, Tool-Driven, Code-as-Doc.
- ☐ **Documentation Priority Quadrant:** Q1 (must doc) → Q4 (avoid).
- ☐ **ADRs (Architectural Decision Records):** Document *why* a tech choice was made.
- ☐ **Story Points use Fibonacci sequence:** 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ?
- ☐ **Compliance Exception:** Regulated industries (BFSI, Healthcare, Aviation) still need comprehensive documentation.
- ☐ **Documentation is a Team Responsibility** — not just the Product Owner's job.

> **Best of luck for your KTU examination! 🚀**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Drivers, Capabilities and Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Agile Drivers, Capabilities and Value # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Agile Drivers, Capabilities, and Value The transformation toward Agile methodologies is not arbitrary; it is a **deliberate organizational response** to specific market pressures and internal limitations of traditional plan-driven development models. To understand *why* Agile works, the modern software engineering framework (aligned with KTU 2024 scheme outcomes) requires us to analyze three foundational pillars: 1. **Agile Drivers** — The external and internal *forces* that compel an organization to change. 2. **Agile Capabilities** — The internal *competencies* an organization must develop to execute Agile successfully. 3. **Agile Value** — The measurable *benefits* realized once Agile is adopted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > - **Agile Drivers** are the *catalysts* — business, technology, and people-oriented pressures that make traditional (waterfall/plan-driven) approaches obsolete. > - **Agile Capabilities** are the *competencies* — the skills, practices, and culture that an organization must cultivate to sustain an Agile transformation. > - **Agile Value** is the *outcome* — the tangible business worth (faster delivery, higher quality, customer delight) that Agile generates for stakeholders. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳 Imagine a **traditional restaurant** that prepares every dish only when a customer books a **full banquet menu 3 days in advance** (this is the *plan-driven / waterfall model*). - **Drivers** are the problems: customers nowadays want to *see the dish* before ordering, want to *change orders mid-meal*, and want *fresh, customized food*. The old system fails because by Day 3, the customer is no longer hungry! - **Capabilities** are the new kitchen upgrades: the chefs can now *cook in small batches* (iterations), *taste-test constantly* (continuous feedback), and *swap ingredients on the fly* (adaptive planning). - **Value** is what the customer finally gets: a *delicious, fresh, personalized meal* served *within minutes*, not days. In software terms: - The *banquet kitchen* = Heavy upfront requirements, Big Design Up Front (BDUF), long release cycles. - The *Agile kitchen* = Sprint-based delivery, daily stand-ups, continuous integration, customer demos. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** > - *Drivers* answer: **WHY** adopt Agile? > - *Capabilities* answer: **WHAT** must we build internally to adopt Agile? > - *Value* answers: **WHAT** do we gain after adopting Agile? --- ## 1.3 The Three Pillars — At a Glance | Pillar | Core Question | Primary Focus | Time Horizon | |---|---|---|---| | **Drivers** | Why change? | External market + internal pain | Pre-transformation | | **Capabilities** | How to change? | People, process, tools | During transformation | | **Value** | What do we gain? | Business outcomes & ROI | Post-transformation | > [!TIP] > For a **2-mark KTU short-answer question**, simply writing: *"Drivers are the reasons, capabilities are the enablers, and value is the benefit"* earns full marks. --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This topic directly maps to: - **CO2:** *Understand the principles, drivers, and capabilities of Agile methodologies.* - **Bloom's Level:** *Understand (L2) → Analyze (L4)* - **Module Weightage:** Module 2 typically carries **15–20%** of End Semester Examination marks. **Standard Metrics** highlighted in KTU case studies: - **Time-to-Market (TTM)** reduction: **30% to 50%** - **Defect Density** reduction: **20% to 40%** - **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** increase: **15% to 30%** - **ROI Payback Period** for Agile adoption: **12–18 months** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Agile Drivers — The Catalysts of Change Agile drivers are the **forces of disruption** that pressure enterprises to abandon rigid, sequential, plan-driven models. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies these drivers into **four primary categories**: ### 2.1.1 Business Drivers 💼 These arise from competitive market dynamics. 1. **Time-to-Market Pressure** - Modern products must reach customers *months* faster than competitors. - Traditional 12–18 month release cycles are commercially fatal. - Agile compresses delivery to **2–4 week sprints**. 2. **Changing Customer Expectations** - Customers today demand *frequent updates*, *personalization*, and *immediate responsiveness*. - The *always-on economy* (24/7 services) requires continuous deployment capability. 3. **Globalization & Distributed Teams** - Software teams are spread across continents. - Need for *asynchronous collaboration* and *remote-friendly practices*. 4. **Competitive Differentiation** - Innovation speed = market share. - Companies like **Netflix, Spotify, Amazon** release features multiple times per day. ### 2.1.2 Technology Drivers 💻 1. **Cloud Computing & SaaS Maturity** - Infrastructure is now *on-demand*, reducing release friction. - **Continuous Deployment** is feasible due to elastic infrastructure. 2. **DevOps & Automation Tools** - CI/CD pipelines (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) automate testing and deployment. - **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** allows reproducible environments. 3. **Mobile & IoT Proliferation** - Short product life-cycles demand *rapid iteration*. - Multi-platform delivery (iOS, Android, Web) requires parallel feature development. 4. **Open-Source Ecosystem** - Reusable components reduce build time. - Frameworks (React, Spring Boot, Django) accelerate MVP creation. ### 2.1.3 People Drivers 👥 1. **Knowledge Worker Engagement** - Talented developers reject micromanagement. - Agile's *self-organizing teams* increase retention and productivity. 2. **Cross-Functional Collaboration** - Silos between Dev, QA, BA, and Ops are obsolete. - Agile promotes **T-shaped professionals** (deep expertise + broad awareness). 3. **Customer Collaboration Culture** - *On-site customer* (Beck & Fowler) is a direct Agile principle. - Reduces misinterpretation of requirements. ### 2.1.4 Process Drivers ⚙️ 1. **Failure of Big Design Up Front (BDUF)** - **Statistic:** Industry studies show **60–80%** of software features are rarely or never used. - Agile's *just-in-time design* avoids wasted effort. 2. **High Defect Rates in Late Stages** - In waterfall, defects found in testing cost **10–100× more** to fix than in requirements phase. - Agile's *continuous testing* catches defects early. 3. **Volatile Requirements** - The **CHAOS Report** (Standish Group) shows only **~30%** of software projects succeed. - Agile's *embrace of change* is the survival strategy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic — "B-T-P-P" for Drivers:** > **B**usiness, **T**echnology, **P**eople, **P**rocess. --- ## 2.2 Agile Capabilities — The Organizational Competencies If drivers are the *why*, capabilities are the *how*. An organization can have immense pressure to go Agile but will fail without these competencies. The KTU 2024 scheme identifies **seven core capabilities**: ### 2.2.1 The Seven Core Agile Capabilities | # | Capability | Description | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Collaborative Customer Engagement** | Active customer involvement in every iteration | Sprint reviews, demos, UAT | | 2 | **Continuous Delivery** | Automated build-test-deploy pipeline | CI/CD with Jenkins + Kubernetes | | 3 | **Adaptive Planning** | Rolling-wave planning, not fixed Gantt charts | Release planning + sprint backlogs | | 4 | **Frequent Delivery** | Small, incremental releases every 2–4 weeks | Bi-weekly production deployments | | 5 | **Technical Agility** | Refactoring, TDD, pair programming, code reviews | Test-driven development, CI | | 6 | **Empowered Teams** | Self-organizing, autonomous decision-making | Scrum's self-managing teams | | 7 | **Lean Thinking** | Eliminate waste, amplify learning | Value stream mapping, Kanban WIP limits | ### 2.2.2 The Agile Triangle of Capabilities ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Collaborative
Customer
Engagement] --> B[Continuous
Delivery] B --> C[Adaptive
Planning] C --> D[Empowered
Teams] D --> E[Technical
Agility] E --> F[Lean
Thinking] F --> A ``` > The seven capabilities are **interdependent** — strengthening one amplifies the others. A team practicing TDD (Technical Agility) finds it easier to deploy continuously (Continuous Delivery). --- ## 2.3 Agile Value — The Business Outcomes Value in Agile is not abstract; it is **measured**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Business Value Triad**: ### 2.3.1 The Three Dimensions of Agile Value 1. **Customer Value** 🎯 - *Higher satisfaction* through frequent releases. - *Better product-market fit* via continuous feedback. - Measured by: **NPS (Net Promoter Score), CSAT, retention rate**. 2. **Business Value** 💰 - *Faster ROI* through early revenue realization. - *Lower total cost of ownership (TCO)*. - *Reduced risk* via short feedback loops. - Measured by: **ROI %, TTM, market share growth**. 3. **Organizational Value** 🏢 - *Higher employee engagement* and lower attrition. - *Improved innovation capacity*. - *Better adaptability* to change. - Measured by: **Engagement surveys, innovation index, change velocity**. ### 2.3.2 The Value Delivery Equation The fundamental value equation in Agile (used in KTU numerical problems) is: $$V_{Agile} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$$ Where: - $V_{Agile}$ = **Total Agile Value** delivered - $B_i$ = Business value of feature $i$ - $U_i$ = Usage / adoption rate of feature $i$ - $n$ = Total number of features delivered - $T_{cycle}$ = Average cycle time per feature - $C_{defect}$ = Cost impact of defects > [!NOTE] > **Higher value** is achieved by: > 1. Increasing the numerator (high-value, well-adopted features). > 2. Decreasing the denominator (shorter cycles, fewer defects). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Use Case | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Velocity | $V = \dfrac{\sum S_i}{N_{sprint}}$ | Predict team's capacity per sprint | Story Points / Sprint | | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{done} - T_{start}$ | Time from start to delivery | Hours / Days | | Lead Time | $T_l = T_{deliver} - T_{request}$ | Time from request to delivery | Days | | Defect Density | $D_d = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{KLOC}$ | Quality measurement | Defects per KLOC | | ROI of Agile | $ROI = \dfrac{(Gain - Cost)}{Cost} \times 100$ | Business justification | Percentage | | Burndown Rate | $R_b = \dfrac{R_{remaining}}{T_{remaining}}$ | Sprint progress tracking | Points / Day | | CSAT Score | $CSAT = \dfrac{P_{satisfied}}{P_{total}} \times 100$ | Customer value measurement | Percentage | | First-Time Yield | $FTY = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Process quality | Percentage | | Cumulative Flow | $CFL = \int_{0}^{t} F(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Predict delivery date | Story Points | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize *Velocity*, *Cycle Time*, and *ROI of Agile* — these appear in nearly every module test and frequently in ESE Part B questions. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | How Agile Drivers, Capabilities & Value Apply | |---|---| | **Banking & FinTech** | Driver: Regulatory change → Capability: Continuous compliance → Value: Faster product launch | | **Healthcare** | Driver: Patient data urgency → Capability: Cross-functional care teams → Value: Better patient outcomes | | **E-Commerce** | Driver: Seasonal demand → Capability: Auto-scaling + sprints → Value: Revenue spike capture | | **Automotive (SDV)** | Driver: Software-defined vehicles → Capability: OTA updates → Value: Continuous customer delight | | **Aerospace** | Driver: Long certification cycles → Capability: Iterative MVP validation → Value: Reduced R&D waste | > [!TIP] > **For 14-mark ESE questions**, structure your answer in **three paragraphs**: (1) Explain drivers with examples, (2) List the 7 capabilities, (3) Map each to a measurable value using the value equation. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Agile Value Realization Model The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to **derive or justify** the value equation. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. ### 3.1.1 Starting Premise Agile value is realized when **benefits** are delivered **faster** with **fewer defects**. Mathematically: $$V = f(\text{Benefit}, \text{Time}, \text{Quality})$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the *aggregate business value* of a set of delivered features. $$V_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i$$ *Explanation:* Each feature $i$ carries a *business value* $B_i$ (e.g., revenue potential) and a *usage coefficient* $U_i$ (fraction of users who actually use it). Summing these across all delivered features gives the **total realized business benefit**. **Step 2:** Define the *cycle time penalty* — Agile favors shorter cycles. $$P_{time} = T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (T_{done,j} - T_{start,j})$$ *Explanation:* Average time from feature start to feature done across all $n$ features. Shorter cycles increase value (less time-to-revenue). **Step 3:** Define the *defect cost penalty*. $$C_{defect} = N_{defects} \cdot C_{per\_defect} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} D_k \cdot R_k$$ *Explanation:* $N_{defects}$ is the number of post-release defects, $C_{per\_defect}$ is the average rework cost, $D_k$ is the *delay* caused by defect $k$, and $R_k$ is the *revenue loss per day* of delay. **Step 4:** Combine numerator and denominator into the final equation. $$V_{Agile} = \frac{V_{agg}}{P_{time} \cdot C_{defect}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$$ *Explanation:* This is the canonical *Agile Value Index*. Higher numerator, lower denominator = higher value. **Step 5:** Introduce the *Agility Multiplier* $A_m$ to capture qualitative benefits (team morale, innovation). $$V_{total} = A_m \cdot V_{Agile}$$ *Explanation:* $A_m > 1$ in well-functioning Agile teams due to compounding effects (e.g., knowledge sharing, faster feedback). ### 3.1.3 Final Compact Form $$V_{total} = A_m \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **exam-friendly** because each step can be expanded for a 7-mark sub-question. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):** > A Scrum team delivers 6 features in a sprint. Business values and usage rates are tabulated below. Average cycle time is **10 days**. Defect count is **3** with an average rework cost of **₹50,000 per defect**. If the Agility Multiplier is **1.2**, calculate the Total Agile Value. | Feature $i$ | Business Value $B_i$ (₹) | Usage Rate $U_i$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | 100,000 | 0.9 | | 2 | 80,000 | 0.7 | | 3 | 50,000 | 0.5 | | 4 | 120,000 | 0.8 | | 5 | 60,000 | 0.6 | | 6 | 90,000 | 0.4 | ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Calculate $\sum B_i \cdot U_i$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{6} B_i \cdot U_i &= (100000)(0.9) + (80000)(0.7) + (50000)(0.5) \\ &\quad + (120000)(0.8) + (60000)(0.6) + (90000)(0.4) \\ &= 90000 + 56000 + 25000 + 96000 + 36000 + 36000 \\ &= 339000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the cycle time and defect cost. $$T_{cycle} = 10 \text{ days}, \quad C_{defect} = 3 \times 50000 = 150000 \text{ ₹}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the Agile Value Index. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{Agile} &= \frac{339000}{10 \times 150000} \\ &= \frac{339000}{1500000} \\ &= 0.226 \text{ ₹ per day per ₹ defect cost} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the Agility Multiplier. $$V_{total} = 1.2 \times 0.226 = 0.2712 \text{ ₹ per day per ₹ defect cost}$$ ### 3.2.2 Interpretation > The team delivers **₹0.27 of net value per day per rupee of defect impact**, with the multiplier accounting for intangible benefits like morale and innovation. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Drivers → Capabilities → Value | Driver | Required Capability | Realized Value | KTU 14-Mark Sub-Question Hook | |---|---|---|---| | **Time-to-Market pressure** | Continuous Delivery + Adaptive Planning | Faster ROI, competitive edge | "Explain how continuous delivery creates business value." | | **Customer expectation shift** | Collaborative Customer Engagement | Higher CSAT, NPS | "Discuss the role of customer collaboration in Agile value." | | **Cloud + DevOps maturity** | Technical Agility | Lower infrastructure cost | "How does DevOps enable Agile capabilities?" | | **Defect cost escalation** | Test-Driven Development (TDD) | Reduced rework, higher quality | "Justify TDD as an Agile capability." | | **Knowledge worker churn** | Empowered Teams | Higher retention, innovation | "Link self-organizing teams to organizational value." | | **Volatile requirements** | Adaptive Planning | Reduced waste, better fit | "Why is BDUF a process driver for Agile adoption?" | --- ## 3.4 KTU-Ready Flow of Reasoning For any 14-mark answer, follow this **3-tier reasoning structure**: **Tier 1 — Identify (Driver):** Name the pressure with a one-line industry example. **Tier 2 — Enable (Capability):** Specify the Agile competency that responds. **Tier 3 — Realize (Value):** Quantify or qualify the business benefit. > [!TIP] > **Use this exact sentence pattern in your exam:** > *"The driver of **[Driver X]** compels the organization to develop the capability of **[Capability Y]**, which in turn delivers the Agile value of **[Value Z]**."* > This single sentence, expanded, is worth **4–5 marks** by itself. --- ## List any four categories of drivers with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** Agile drivers are the external and internal forces that compel an organization to transition from traditional plan-driven models to Agile methodologies. > > **Four Categories (2 Marks):** > 1. **Business Drivers** — e.g., Time-to-market pressure forcing faster releases. > 2. **Technology Drivers** — e.g., Cloud and DevOps enabling continuous deployment. > 3. **People Drivers** — e.g., Knowledge worker demand for autonomy and self-organization. > 4. **Process Drivers** — e.g., High cost of late-stage defect discovery in waterfall. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Agile Capabilities and Agile Value with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Agile Capabilities | Agile Value | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Internal competencies the organization must build | Measurable benefits realized post-adoption | | **Question Answered** | *How* do we become Agile? | *What* do we gain by going Agile? | | **Examples** | TDD, CI/CD, Daily stand-ups | Faster ROI, Higher CSAT, Lower TTM | | **Stage** | During transformation | Post-transformation | > *Capabilities are the means; Value is the end.* **(1 Mark for the concluding statement.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-2 question with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, totaling 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** — *Explain the seven core Agile capabilities. How do they interrelate to deliver sustainable Agile value?* **Model Answer:** The seven core Agile capabilities form the operational foundation that allows an organization to extract value from Agile adoption. **1. Collaborative Customer Engagement (1 Mark):** Active customer involvement in iteration reviews, demos, and UAT ensures that the product is built *with* the customer, not *for* the customer. This reduces rework and increases adoption rates. **2. Continuous Delivery (1 Mark):** An automated build-test-deploy pipeline (CI/CD) ensures that working software can be released at any time. Combined with automated testing, it eliminates manual deployment errors. **3. Adaptive Planning (1 Mark):** Instead of fixed Gantt charts, Agile uses rolling-wave planning with release plans and sprint backlogs. Plans are revisited and updated every sprint. **4. Frequent Delivery (1 Mark):** Shipping small, incremental releases every 2–4 weeks reduces feedback latency and allows early revenue realization. **5. Technical Agility (1 Mark):** Practices like TDD, refactoring, pair programming, and code reviews ensure that the codebase remains healthy and adaptable. **6. Empowered Teams (1 Mark):** Self-organizing teams make local decisions without waiting for management approval, accelerating throughput and improving morale. **7. Lean Thinking (1 Mark):** Eliminating waste (unnecessary features, handoffs, waiting) and amplifying learning (retrospectives, experiments) optimizes the value stream. **Interrelation (Included in above marks):** The seven capabilities form a *virtuous cycle* — e.g., empowered teams (6) practice TDD (5) which enables continuous delivery (2) which supports frequent releases (4) which feeds back to customer engagement (1), completing the loop. --- **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** — *With a suitable numerical example, derive the Agile Value Realization equation and interpret its components.* **Model Answer:** The Agile Value equation is derived in five steps. **Step 1 (1 Mark):** The aggregate business value of $n$ delivered features is: $$V_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i$$ where $B_i$ = business value of feature $i$ and $U_i$ = its usage rate. **Step 2 (1 Mark):** The cycle time penalty is: $$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (T_{done,j} - T_{start,j})$$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** The defect cost penalty is: $$C_{defect} = N_{defects} \cdot C_{per\_defect} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} D_k \cdot R_k$$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Combining numerator and denominator: $$V_{Agile} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** With the Agility Multiplier $A_m$: $$V_{total} = A_m \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$$ **Numerical Example (1 Mark):** For the worked example in Section 3.2.1: $$V_{agg} = 339000 \text{ ₹}, \quad T_{cycle} = 10 \text{ days}, \quad C_{defect} = 150000 \text{ ₹}, \quad A_m = 1.2$$ $$V_{total} = 1.2 \times \frac{339000}{10 \times 150000} = 0.2712$$ **Interpretation (1 Mark):** Higher numerator (high-value, widely-used features) and lower denominator (shorter cycles, fewer defects) increase Agile value. The multiplier captures intangible benefits. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** — *Discuss the four categories of Agile drivers with real-world industry examples. Why is BDUF considered a major process driver for Agile adoption?* **Model Answer:** **1. Business Drivers (1.5 Marks):** - **Time-to-Market Pressure:** E-commerce companies like *Amazon* deploy code every 11.6 seconds on average. - **Customer Expectation Shift:** Banking apps now expect *real-time notifications*; *HDFC* and *Revolut* use Agile squads. - **Globalization:** Distributed teams across India, US, and Europe require async-collaboration-friendly Agile. - **Competitive Differentiation:** *Netflix* uses Chaos Engineering + Agile to outpace competitors. **2. Technology Drivers (1.5 Marks):** - **Cloud & SaaS:** *AWS, Azure, GCP* allow on-demand scaling. - **DevOps & Automation:** *Jenkins, GitLab CI* enable continuous pipelines. - **Mobile/IoT:** Smart device apps (Wear OS, Apple Watch) need rapid iteration. - **Open-Source Ecosystem:** React, Spring Boot shorten MVP time. **3. People Drivers (1.5 Marks):** - **Knowledge Worker Engagement:** Talented developers prefer autonomy; *Google's research* shows autonomy correlates with innovation. - **Cross-Functional Collaboration:** Silos between Dev, QA, BA are replaced by Agile squads. - **Customer Collaboration Culture:** *Spotify's squad model* embeds customer feedback at every iteration. **4. Process Drivers (1 Mark):** - **BDUF Failure:** Industry studies show 60–80% of features are rarely or never used. - **Late Defect Cost:** Bugs found in production cost 10–100× more to fix. - **Volatile Requirements:** Markets shift; rigid plans become obsolete. **BDUF as a Process Driver (1.5 Marks):** Big Design Up Front creates massive waste because requirements change *before* the system is built. By the time a 12-month waterfall project delivers, ~50% of requirements are already invalid (CHAOS Report). Agile's *just-in-time design* and *embrace of change* are direct antidotes to BDUF. Hence, BDUF is a powerful process driver pushing enterprises toward Agile. --- **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** — *Explain the three dimensions of Agile value. How would you measure each dimension in a real B.Tech project scenario?* **Model Answer:** **1. Customer Value (2.5 Marks):** Customer value reflects the *worth delivered to end users*. It is measured by: - **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** "How likely are you to recommend this product?" — measured via post-release surveys. - **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT):** Percentage of satisfied customers. - **Retention Rate:** Monthly active user (MAU) growth. - **Adoption Rate:** Percentage of users actively using new features. *Example:* In a campus placement portal project, CSAT is measured by post-placement student surveys and recruiter feedback. **2. Business Value (2.5 Marks):** Business value reflects *commercial outcomes*. It is measured by: - **Time-to-Market (TTM):** Days from feature request to production. - **Return on Investment (ROI):** $(Gain - Cost) / Cost \times 100$. - **Revenue Growth:** Incremental revenue from new features. - **Cost of Quality (CoQ):** Defect prevention + appraisal + failure costs. *Example:* For a college ERP, ROI is calculated by comparing the cost saved on manual processes against the cost of Agile development. **3. Organizational Value (2 Marks):** Organizational value reflects *internal capability growth*. It is measured by: - **Team Velocity Trend:** Increasing velocity over sprints. - **Engagement Surveys:** Quarterly pulse surveys. - **Innovation Index:** Number of experiments run per quarter. - **Defect Escape Rate:** Defects found in production vs. unit tests. *Example:* A B.Tech final-year project team tracks velocity per sprint and bug-escape rate as organizational value metrics. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Drivers with Capabilities (–2 Marks):** Students often write *"Customer feedback"* as a driver, but it is a *capability*. A driver is a *pressure*; a capability is an *enabler*. The driver is *"changing customer expectations"*; the capability is *"collaborative customer engagement."* > > 2. **Listing Without Explaining (–2 Marks):** Naming the seven capabilities without explaining *how* they interrelate loses half the marks in a 7-mark sub-question. > > 3. **Ignoring Numerical Justification (–1 to 2 Marks):** When asked about "Agile value," students give qualitative answers. Always include at least one formula (Velocity, ROI, or the Value equation) to demonstrate analytical depth. > > 4. **Forgetting Examples (–1 Mark):** A 14-mark answer without real-world examples (Spotify, Amazon, banking apps) is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. > > 5. **Mixing Up Time Horizons (–1 Mark):** Drivers are *pre-transformation*, capabilities are *during*, and value is *post-transformation*. Mixing these up confuses the answer. > > 6. **Skipping the Value Equation (–1 Mark):** The equation $V_{total} = A_m \cdot \frac{\sum B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$ is a **favourite KTU examiner question**. Skipping it costs easy marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Drivers = WHY** (Business, Technology, People, Process — B-T-P-P) - ✅ **Capabilities = HOW** (Seven: Collaborative Engagement, Continuous Delivery, Adaptive Planning, Frequent Delivery, Technical Agility, Empowered Teams, Lean Thinking) - ✅ **Value = WHAT** (Customer, Business, Organizational) - ✅ **Big Design Up Front (BDUF)** is the single largest *process driver* pushing enterprises toward Agile. - ✅ **CHAOS Report statistic:** ~30% project success rate in waterfall → Agile aims to flip this. - ✅ **Defect Cost Multiplier:** Bugs in production cost **10× to 100×** more than in requirements. - ✅ **Agile Value Equation:** $V_{total} = A_m \cdot \dfrac{\sum B_i \cdot U_i}{T_{cycle} \cdot C_{defect}}$ - ✅ **Velocity Formula:** $V = \dfrac{\sum S_i}{N_{sprint}}$ (Story Points per Sprint) - ✅ **Cycle Time** ≠ **Lead Time:** Cycle = start to done; Lead = request to delivery. - ✅ **Customer collaboration > contract negotiation** (Agile Manifesto Principle). - ✅ **Working software > comprehensive documentation** (Agile Manifesto Principle). - ✅ **Responding to change > following a plan** (Agile Manifesto Principle). - ✅ **TTM Reduction:** Agile projects typically achieve **30%–50%** faster time-to-market. - ✅ **The seven capabilities form a *virtuous cycle*** — strengthening one amplifies all others. - ✅ **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic is core to **CO2** and frequently tested in **Module 2** of ESE. - ✅ **Mnemonic for drivers:** **B-T-P-P** (Business, Technology, People, Process). - ✅ **Mnemonic for value dimensions:** **C-B-O** (Customer, Business, Organizational). - ✅ **Industry examples to memorize:** Spotify (squad model), Amazon (11.6s deploys), Netflix (Chaos Engineering), HDFC (Agile banking squads), Google (autonomy-driven innovation). - ✅ **Agility Multiplier $A_m > 1$** in healthy Agile teams; equals 1 in struggling teams. - ✅ **Just-in-time design** is Agile's answer to BDUF. - ✅ **The 7 capabilities interrelate** — a one-line exam-ready sentence: *"Customer engagement enables adaptive planning, which drives frequent delivery, supported by continuous delivery and technical agility, executed by empowered teams guided by lean thinking."*

topics.title: Agile Scrum Framework - Scrum Artifacts, Meetings, Activities and Roles, Scrum Team Simulation, Scrum Planning Principles, Product and Release Planning, Sprinting: Planning, Execution, Review and Retrospective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Agile Scrum Framework - Scrum Artifacts, Meetings, Activities and Roles, Scrum Team Simulation, Scrum Planning Principles, Product and Release Planning, Sprinting: Planning, Execution, Review and Retrospective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏛️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scrum Framework (Official Scrum Guide Definition):** Scrum is a lightweight, iterative, and incremental **empirical framework** that helps teams, organizations, and individuals generate value through adaptive solutions for complex problems. It is built upon the pillars of **transparency, inspection, and adaptation** and requires a *Scrum Team* consisting of a *Product Owner*, a *Scrum Master*, and *Developers*.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme engineering education, Scrum is treated as a **value-driven, time-boxed agile methodology** that operationalizes the Agile Manifesto through five formal events, three formal artifacts, and three formal accountabilities (roles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Scrum Team** = Cross-functional, self-managing unit (typically **7 ± 2 members**, i.e., 5–9)
> - **Sprint** = The heartbeat of Scrum; a fixed time-box of **2 weeks (10 working days)** as the KTU standard
> - **Increment** = The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint, plus the value of all previous Sprints' increments

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Relay Race of the Restaurant Kitchen 🍳

Imagine a **high-end restaurant kitchen during a dinner rush**. The kitchen is your Scrum Team.

- The **Head Chef (Product Owner)** decides *what dishes will be on tonight's menu* based on customer demand, seasonal ingredients, and business strategy. They prioritize: "Truffle pasta must come out first."
- The **Sous Chef (Scrum Master)** removes obstacles — a broken stove, a missing ingredient, a confused new intern. They facilitate, they don't cook.
- The **Line Cooks (Developers)** actually prepare the food, plate it, and serve it within a fixed time window (a "dinner service" = a Sprint).
- The **Order Tickets (Product Backlog)** are the list of all possible dishes.
- The **Active Tickets on the Pass (Sprint Backlog)** are the dishes being prepared *right now*.
- The **Plated Dish going to the customer (Increment)** is the actual valuable output.

Every **2 weeks (one dinner service)**, they:
1. Plan the menu (Sprint Planning)
2. Check the pass every 15 minutes — *"Is the pasta ready? Is anything stuck?"* (Daily Scrum)
3. Show the customer the plated dish and get feedback (Sprint Review)
4. Discuss: *"Why was service slow tonight?"* (Sprint Retrospective)

The kitchen **never stops improving** — next service, the truffle pasta is even better.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Zone 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 carries the heaviest weight in the KTU PEAIT413 syllabus** because Scrum is the *industry reference implementation* of Agile. Every examiner question on Agile Methods, iterative delivery, or project management defaults to Scrum. Mastery of the three artifacts, five events, and three roles is **non-negotiable** for a full mark score.

### The Three Pillars of Empirical Process Control

| Pillar | Definition | Application in Scrum |
|---|---|---|
| **Transparency** | Significant aspects of the process must be visible to those who perform and receive the work | Artifacts (Backlogs, Increment) are visible to all |
| **Inspection** | Scrum artifacts and progress toward the agreed goal are inspected frequently | Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Retrospective |
| **Adaptation** | If any aspect deviates outside acceptable limits, adjustments are made | Course corrections in the next Sprint |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scrum Architecture — A Layered Breakdown

Scrum is composed of **three interconnected layers**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   LAYER 1: ROLES (Accountabilities)              │
│   Product Owner · Scrum Master · Developers      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LAYER 2: EVENTS (Time-boxed Ceremonies)        │
│   Sprint · Sprint Planning · Daily Scrum ·       │
│   Sprint Review · Sprint Retrospective           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LAYER 3: ARTIFACTS (Information Radiators)     │
│   Product Backlog · Sprint Backlog · Increment   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 2.2 The Three Scrum Accountabilities (Roles)

### 2.2.1 Product Owner (PO) — *The Maximizer of Value*
- **Single voice of the customer.** Only **one** PO per product (to avoid conflicting priorities).
- Owns the **Product Backlog** — orders items, defines acceptance criteria, ensures the backlog is visible and understood.
- **Accountable** for maximizing the value of the product resulting from the work of the Scrum Team.
- **Authority:** Can cancel a Sprint if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete (only the PO has this power).

### 2.2.2 Scrum Master (SM) — *The Servant-Leader*
- **Serves the team** by removing impediments, coaching, and protecting the team from external interference.
- Facilitates Scrum events as requested or needed.
- Helps the organization adopt Scrum — trains stakeholders, works with other SMs to increase organizational effectiveness.
- **NOT a project manager.** Has no authority over the team; power comes from service.

### 2.2.3 Developers — *The People in the Scrum Team Committed to Creating any aspect of a usable Increment*
- Comprise the cross-functional team members (developers, testers, designers, DBAs, etc.) who do the actual work.
- **Self-managing:** No one (not even the SM) tells them *how* to turn backlog items into increments.

---

## 2.3 The Five Scrum Events (Time-Boxed Ceremonies)

| Event | Time-Box (per KTU Standard) | Purpose | Participants |
|---|---|---|---|
| **The Sprint** | **≤ 1 calendar month** (typical: **2 weeks / 10 working days**) | Container for all other events; a fixed-length iteration | Whole Scrum Team |
| **Sprint Planning** | **≤ 8 hours for a 1-month Sprint** (proportional; ~4 hrs for 2 weeks) | Define *what* can be delivered and *how* | Whole Scrum Team |
| **Daily Scrum** | **15 minutes** (held at same time/place daily) | Synchronize activities, plan next 24 hours, surface impediments | Developers (SM & PO participate if present) |
| **Sprint Review** | **≤ 4 hours for 1-month Sprint** (~2 hrs for 2 weeks) | Inspect the Increment, adapt the Product Backlog | Whole Scrum Team + Stakeholders |
| **Sprint Retrospective** | **≤ 3 hours for 1-month Sprint** (~1.5 hrs for 2 weeks) | Inspect the team itself; plan improvements | Whole Scrum Team (Scrum Team only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** A Sprint can never be shortened. It can, however, be **cancelled** mid-flight only by the Product Owner (when the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete). The new Sprint starts immediately after the previous one ends.

---

## 2.4 The Three Scrum Artifacts (and Their Commitments)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Scrum Guide binds each artifact to a *commitment*** that provides transparency and focus. This is one of the most-frequently-asked KTU questions.

```
ARTIFACT                COMMITMENT (Provides Transparency)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Product Backlog   ──→   Product Goal       (Why the product exists)
Sprint Backlog    ──→   Sprint Goal        (Single objective of the Sprint)
Increment         ──→   Definition of Done (Quality contract)
```

### 2.4.1 Product Backlog
- An **ordered, emergent list of *what* is needed** to improve the product.
- The ordering reflects **value, risk, priority, and dependencies** (highest priority = top of list).
- Items at the top are **refined** (clearly expressed, with acceptance criteria, estimated).
- Items at the bottom are **coarse-grained and may be vague** — the *iceberg model*.
- Owned and managed by the **Product Owner**.

### 2.4.2 Sprint Backlog
- Composed of: the **Sprint Goal** (the *why*), the set of **Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint** (the *what*), and a **plan for delivering the Increment** (the *how*).
- It is a **plan by and for the Developers** — a real-time, highly visible picture of the work in progress.
- Updated daily as the team learns more.

### 2.4.3 Increment
- A concrete stepping stone toward the **Product Goal**.
- Each increment is **additive** to all prior increments.
- A new increment is created by **integrating all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint** with the work of all previous Sprints.
- Must meet the **Definition of Done (DoD)** — a formal, agreed quality contract.

### Definition of Done (DoD) vs. Acceptance Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Definition of Done (DoD):** A *team-level, shared* quality contract. Applies to **all increments**. Example: "Code reviewed, unit-tested, integrated, deployed to staging, approved by QA."
> - **Acceptance Criteria (AC):** A *per-item* contract defined by the Product Owner for a specific Product Backlog Item. Example: "Login must work with Google OAuth."

---

## 2.5 Sprint Activities — The Day-to-Day Mechanics

Beyond the five events, Scrum mandates these continuous activities:

| Activity | Who | When | What |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Backlog Refinement (Grooming)** | PO + Developers (SM facilitates) | Up to **10% of Dev capacity** per Sprint | Breaking down items, clarifying, estimating |
| **Daily Integration / Continuous Integration** | Developers | Continuous | Merging code, running automated tests |
| **Impediment Removal** | SM | As needed | Escalating blockers, shielding the team |
| **Stakeholder Collaboration** | PO + Devs | Continuous | Clarifying requirements, demoing work |

---

## 2.6 Scrum Team Simulation — Canonical Composition

```
                    ┌────────────────────┐
                    │   STAKEHOLDERS     │
                    │ (Customers, Users) │
                    └─────────┬──────────┘
                              │ feedback
                              ▼
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │              SCRUM TEAM (7 ± 2)              │
       │  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  │
       │  │ Product    │  │ Scrum    │  │Developers│  │
       │  │ Owner (1)  │  │ Master(1)│  │ (3–7)    │  │
       │  └────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** A common mistake is to say the Scrum Team is "cross-functional." Actually, the Developers are cross-functional (they have all skills needed to deliver value), but the *whole Scrum Team* is **self-managing** (they decide internally who does what) and **cross-functional** in the broader sense.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Formula & Time-Box Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the single most important cheat sheet for the Module 3 KTU board exam. Memorize the time-boxes precisely — examiners love to test them.

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Sprint duration** | **2 weeks (10 working days)** | KTU standard; can be 1–4 weeks |
| **Scrum Team size** | **7 ± 2** members | Below 5 = too few skills; above 9 = coordination overhead |
| **Sprint Planning time-box** | **≤ 4 hours (2-week Sprint)** | Proportional to Sprint length |
| **Daily Scrum time-box** | **15 minutes** | Strict; no exceptions |
| **Sprint Review time-box** | **≤ 2 hours (2-week Sprint)** | Inspect Increment |
| **Sprint Retrospective time-box** | **≤ 1.5 hours (2-week Sprint)** | Inspect the *team's process* |
| **Backlog Refinement** | **≤ 10% of Developer capacity** | Continuous activity |
| **Velocity formula** | $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed}}{\text{Number of Sprints}}$ | Used for Release Planning |
| **Burndown** | $\text{Remaining Work}_{t} = \text{Total Work}_{\text{start}} - \sum_{i=1}^{t} \text{Work Done}_{i}$ | Ideal trend = linear decline |
| **Capacity** | $C_{sprint} = N_{devs} \times \text{Available Days} \times \text{Focus Factor}$ | Focus Factor ≈ 0.6–0.8 |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Development:** Sprints produce shippable software increments every 2 weeks.
- **Embedded Systems:** Hardware drivers can be developed iteratively; firmware increments tested on real boards.
- **AI/ML Pipelines:** Model training cycles fit naturally into 2-week sprints; each increment is a new model version.
- **Civil/Mechanical (in KTU scope):** Even physical builds can use Scrum — the "increment" is a working prototype or a design package ready for stakeholder review.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Sprint Mechanics & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Sprint Planning Deep-Dive (Step-by-Step Process)

Sprint Planning is the formal event that kicks off a new Sprint. It is structured into **two coordinated halves**, each with a distinct purpose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** *"Explain the two parts of Sprint Planning."* (Asked nearly every semester.)

### 3.1.1 Part 1 — *The Why & The What* (Topic One)

**Step 1:** The Product Owner presents the **ordered Product Backlog** to the Developers. The top items are the **candidates** for the Sprint.

**Step 2:** The Scrum Team defines the **Sprint Goal** — *a single objective for the Sprint*. This is the *why* of the Sprint. It provides focus and flexibility.

$$\text{Sprint Goal} = f(\text{Top PBIs}, \text{Product Goal}, \text{Team Capacity})$$

**Step 3:** The team selects Product Backlog Items (PBIs) from the top of the backlog that align with the Sprint Goal.

$$\text{Selected PBIs} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\} \quad \text{such that} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Estimate}(p_i) \leq \text{Sprint Capacity}$$

**Step 4:** These selected items are **moved into the Sprint Backlog**.

---

### 3.1.2 Part 2 — *The How* (Topic Two)

**Step 5:** The Developers (self-managing) decompose each selected PBI into **tasks** of one day or less.

For each PBI $p_i$:

$$\text{Tasks}(p_i) = \{t_{i,1}, t_{i,2}, \ldots, t_{i,k_i}\}, \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Estimate}(t_{i,j}) \leq 1 \text{ day}$$

**Step 6:** The total work is estimated and balanced against capacity.

$$\text{Total Sprint Work} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} \text{Estimate}(t_{i,j})$$

$$\text{Sprint Commitment Valid} \iff \text{Total Sprint Work} \leq \text{Sprint Capacity}$$

**Step 7:** The Sprint Backlog is finalized and **owned by the Developers** for the duration of the Sprint.

---

## 3.2 Deriving the Sprint Goal from the Product Goal

The **Product Goal** is the long-term objective for the Scrum Team. Each Sprint Goal should advance the Product Goal.

$$\text{Product Goal}_{\text{progress}} = \sum_{s=1}^{S_{\text{current}}} \text{Value Delivered}_s$$

where $S_{\text{current}}$ is the number of completed Sprints.

The **Sprint Goal** for Sprint $s+1$ is:

$$\text{Sprint Goal}_{s+1} = \arg\max_{g} \left[ \text{Alignment}(g, \text{Product Goal}) \cdot \text{Confidence}(g) \right]$$

subject to: $\text{Required Effort}(g) \leq \text{Team Velocity}_{avg}$

---

## 3.3 Velocity Calculation & Release Planning Derivation

**Velocity** is the average amount of work (typically in Story Points) a team completes per Sprint.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Velocity Derivation

**Step 1:** Record completed Story Points at the end of each Sprint.

$$\text{Velocity}_s = \sum_{i \in \text{Completed PBIs}_s} SP_i$$

**Step 2:** Compute the running average after $k$ Sprints.

$$V_{avg}(k) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{s=1}^{k} V_s$$

**Step 3:** For release planning, use a confidence-weighted estimate. KTU exams often use the **simple average**, but production teams use rolling windows:

$$V_{\text{forecast}} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{s=k-2}^{k} V_s \quad \text{(3-Sprint rolling average)}$$

### 3.3.2 Number of Sprints to Complete a Release

Given a **remaining Product Backlog size** $B_{rem}$ in story points:

$$\text{Sprints to Release} = \left\lceil \frac{B_{rem}}{V_{avg}} \right\rceil$$

**Step-by-step worked example (KTU-style):**

> A team has completed 3 Sprints with velocities **20, 25, 22** story points. The remaining Product Backlog is **134 story points**.
> 
> **Step 1:** Compute average velocity.
> 
> $$V_{avg} = \frac{20 + 25 + 22}{3} = \frac{67}{3} \approx 22.33 \text{ SP/Sprint}$$
> 
> **Step 2:** Compute number of Sprints.
> 
> $$\text{Sprints} = \left\lceil \frac{134}{22.33} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.00 \rceil = 6 \text{ Sprints}$$
> 
> **Step 3:** Convert to calendar time.
> 
> $$\text{Calendar Time} = 6 \text{ Sprints} \times 2 \text{ weeks/Sprint} = 12 \text{ weeks} \approx 3 \text{ months}$$

---

## 3.4 Sprint Burndown Chart Derivation

The **Sprint Burndown Chart** plots remaining work (y-axis) against time (x-axis) within a single Sprint.

### 3.4.1 Ideal Burndown Line

$$\text{Ideal Remaining}(t) = \text{Total Work} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{t}{D} \right)$$

where $D$ is the Sprint duration in days and $t$ is the current day ($0 \le t \le D$).

**Worked Example (2-week / 10-day Sprint, 40 SP total):**

$$\text{Ideal Remaining}(t) = 40 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{10}\right)$$

| Day $t$ | Ideal Remaining (SP) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 40 |
| 2 | 32 |
| 4 | 24 |
| 6 | 16 |
| 8 | 8 |
| 10 | 0 |

### 3.4.2 Actual Burndown (per developer updates)

$$\text{Actual Remaining}(t) = \text{Total Work} - \sum_{d=1}^{t} \text{Work Done}_d$$

**Interpretation Rules (KTU Exam Tip):**
- If **actual line is above ideal** → team is **behind schedule** → adaptation needed.
- If **actual line is below ideal** → team is **ahead** → may pull more items.
- A **flat line** indicates a **blocker** → SM must intervene.

---

## 3.5 Product Backlog Ordering Algorithm (Conceptual)

The PO does not arbitrarily order the backlog. KTU's recommended model is **WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First)** or simpler value-based ordering:

$$P_{priority}(i) = w_1 \cdot \text{Business Value}_i + w_2 \cdot \text{Risk Reduction}_i - w_3 \cdot \text{Job Duration}_i$$

where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weights chosen by the PO.

A common simplified form:

$$\text{WSJF} = \frac{\text{Business Value} + \text{Time Criticality} + \text{Risk Reduction}}{\text{Job Size}}$$

Items with the **highest WSJF** are placed at the top of the Product Backlog.

---

## 3.6 Sprint Review vs. Sprint Retrospective — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This distinction is the most-asked 7-mark question in KTU board exams.** Memorize it.

| Dimension | Sprint Review | Sprint Retrospective |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Inspect the **product** (Increment) | Inspect the **process** (team's way of working) |
| **Question answered** | *"Did we build the right thing?"* | *"Did we build the thing right? How can we improve?"* |
| **Attendees** | Whole Scrum Team **+ Stakeholders** | Whole Scrum Team **only** (private) |
| **Output** | Updated **Product Backlog** (new/changed items) | **Actionable improvement items** for next Sprint |
| **Focus** | The **Increment** | The **team's relationships, tools, workflow** |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Activities to Artifacts

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield synthesis for the 14-mark KTU questions.

| Activity / Event | Inputs | Outputs / Outcomes | Artifact Touched |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sprint Planning** | Product Backlog, Team Velocity, Capacity | Sprint Goal, Sprint Backlog, Task Plan | All three |
| **Backlog Refinement** | Product Backlog (top items) | Refined, estimated, smaller PBIs | Product Backlog |
| **Daily Scrum** | Yesterday's progress, today's plan, blockers | Updated Sprint Backlog | Sprint Backlog |
| **Development Work** | Sprint Backlog tasks | Working code, tests, designs | Increment (in progress) |
| **Sprint Review** | Increment, Stakeholder feedback | New/updated PBIs, new priorities | Product Backlog |
| **Sprint Retrospective** | Team observations, metrics | Improvement actions for next Sprint | Team's process |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🎨 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Scrum Lifecycle — Sprint Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Product Backlog Exists])
    sprintPlanNode[PO Presents<br/>Ordered Backlog]
    sprintGoalNode[Define<br/>Sprint Goal]
    selectPBIsNode[Select PBIs<br/>for Sprint]
    createBacklogNode[Create<br/>Sprint Backlog]
    sprintNode[/Sprint Begins<br/>Day 0/]
    dailyNode{Daily Scrum<br/>15 min}
    devNode[Developers Build<br/>Increment]
    reviewNode[Sprint Review<br/>2 hrs]
    retroNode[Sprint Retrospective<br/>1.5 hrs]
    doneNode{Goal<br/>Achieved?}
    shipNode[Release Increment]
    updateNode[Update<br/>Product Backlog]
    endNode([New Sprint<br/>Starts Immediately])

    startNode --> sprintPlanNode
    sprintPlanNode --> sprintGoalNode
    sprintGoalNode --> selectPBIsNode
    selectPBIsNode --> createBacklogNode
    createBacklogNode --> sprintNode
    sprintNode --> dailyNode
    dailyNode --> devNode
    devNode --> dailyNode
    devNode --> reviewNode
    reviewNode --> doneNode
    doneNode -->|Yes| shipNode
    doneNode -->|Goal Obsolete| updateNode
    shipNode --> retroNode
    updateNode --> retroNode
    retroNode --> endNode
    endNode --> sprintPlanNode
```

---

## 4.2 Scrum Roles & Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stakeholdersBlock[Stakeholders]
        custNode[Customers and Users]
    end

    subgraph scrumTeamBlock[Scrum Team]
        poNode[Product Owner]
        smNode[Scrum Master]
        devNode[Developers 3 to 7]
    end

    custNode -->|Feedback and Needs| poNode
    poNode -->|Ordered Backlog and Vision| devNode
    poNode -->|Acceptance Criteria| devNode
    smNode -->|Removes Impediments| devNode
    smNode -->|Coaches and Facilitates| poNode
    smNode -->|Coaches Organization| orgNode[Organization]
    devNode -->|Shippable Increment| poNode
    devNode -->|Blockers| smNode
    poNode -->|Approved Increment| custNode
```

---

## 4.3 Sprint Planning — Two-Part Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    partOneNode[Sprint Planning Topic One]
    partOneQNode[WHY are we doing this Sprint?]
    sprintGoalOutNode[Output: Sprint Goal]
    selectPBIOutNode[Output: Selected PBIs]

    partTwoNode[Sprint Planning Topic Two]
    partTwoQNode[HOW will we do the work?]
    taskDecompNode[Decompose PBIs into Tasks]
    sprintBacklogOutNode[Output: Sprint Backlog]

    partOneNode --> partOneQNode
    partOneQNode --> sprintGoalOutNode
    partOneQNode --> selectPBIOutNode
    selectPBIOutNode --> partTwoNode
    partTwoNode --> partTwoQNode
    partTwoQNode --> taskDecompNode
    taskDecompNode --> sprintBacklogOutNode
```

---

## 4.4 Backlog Refinement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawItemNode[Raw PBI from Stakeholder]
    clarifyNode[PO Writes User Story<br/>and Acceptance Criteria]
    estimateNode[Developers Estimate<br/>Story Points]
    decomposeNode[Decompose if greater than 8 SP]
    prioritizedNode[Place in Ordered Backlog]
    readyNode{Ready for<br/>Sprint?}
    notReadyNode[Move to Refinement Again]
    selectNode[Pull into Sprint Planning]

    rawItemNode --> clarifyNode
    clarifyNode --> estimateNode
    estimateNode --> decomposeNode
    decomposeNode --> readyNode
    readyNode -->|No| notReadyNode
    notReadyNode --> clarifyNode
    readyNode -->|Yes| prioritizedNode
    prioritizedNode --> selectNode
```

---

## 4.5 Product vs. Release Planning Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    visionNode[Product Vision]
    prodGoalNode[Product Goal]
    roadmapNode[Release Roadmap]
    release1Node[Release 1 Scope]
    release2Node[Release 2 Scope]
    release3Node[Release 3 Scope]
    s1Node[Sprint 1]
    s2Node[Sprint 2]
    s3Node[Sprint 3]
    s4Node[Sprint 4]
    s5Node[Sprint 5]
    s6Node[Sprint 6]
    incNode[Shippable Increments]

    visionNode --> prodGoalNode
    prodGoalNode --> roadmapNode
    roadmapNode --> release1Node
    roadmapNode --> release2Node
    roadmapNode --> release3Node
    release1Node --> s1Node
    release1Node --> s2Node
    release1Node --> s3Node
    release2Node --> s4Node
    release2Node --> s5Node
    release3Node --> s6Node
    s1Node --> incNode
    s2Node --> incNode
    s3Node --> incNode
    s4Node --> incNode
    s5Node --> incNode
    s6Node --> incNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions require a precise, 3-4 sentence answer. Do not write essays. Definitions and key terms are graded strictly.

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three pillars of empirical process control in Scrum.**

**Model Answer:**
The three pillars of empirical process control in Scrum are:

1. **Transparency:** Significant aspects of the process must be defined and visible to those who perform and receive the work. This is achieved through shared artifacts (Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment) and a common Definition of Done.

2. **Inspection:** Scrum artifacts and the progress toward the agreed goal are inspected frequently and diligently, but not so frequently that inspection gets in the way of the work. Inspections are embedded in events like the Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective.

3. **Adaptation:** If any aspect of the process deviates outside acceptable limits, or if the resulting product is unacceptable, the process or the material being processed must be adjusted. Adjustments are made as soon as possible to minimize further deviation.

These pillars enable Scrum to deal with complex, unpredictable problems by maintaining a continuous feedback loop. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Definition of Done (DoD) and Acceptance Criteria (AC) in Scrum.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Definition of Done (DoD) | Acceptance Criteria (AC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Applies to **all** Product Backlog items / increments | Applies to a **single** PBI |
| **Owner** | Defined and owned by the **Scrum Team** | Defined by the **Product Owner** (with team input) |
| **Purpose** | Ensures a **baseline quality** contract | Ensures the **specific functionality** meets business needs |
| **Example** | Code reviewed, unit-tested, integrated, deployed to staging | "Login must work with Google OAuth and return a JWT" |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions feature **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two provided. Both choices below are designed to mirror the KTU 2024 Scheme paper pattern: each has two sub-parts worth **7 marks** each, with at least one 7-mark question involving application/calculation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five events of Scrum**, specifying the time-box, participants, and purpose of each event. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Scrum Team has completed **5 Sprints** with the following completed story points: **18, 22, 20, 25, 23**. The Product Backlog has **210 story points** remaining, and a future Release is planned to deliver **at least 140 of these points**. 
 - **(i)** Calculate the average team velocity.
 - **(ii)** Determine the number of Sprints required to complete the planned Release scope.
 - **(iii)** How many calendar weeks will this take, assuming a 2-week Sprint? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — The Five Scrum Events (7 marks)**

The five events in Scrum are:

**1. The Sprint** *(2 marks)*
- **Time-box:** ≤ 1 calendar month (KTU standard: **2 weeks / 10 working days**).
- **Purpose:** It is the *heartbeat of Scrum* — a fixed time-box during which a "Done", usable, and potentially releasable Increment is created. A new Sprint starts immediately after the conclusion of the previous one.

**2. Sprint Planning** *(2 marks)*
- **Time-box:** ≤ 8 hours for a 1-month Sprint (~4 hours for a 2-week Sprint).
- **Participants:** Whole Scrum Team (PO, SM, Developers).
- **Purpose:** Defines *what* can be delivered in the Sprint and *how* that work will be achieved. Has two topics: (i) Why is this Sprint valuable? → Sprint Goal. (ii) What can be Done? → Selection of PBIs and plan.

**3. Daily Scrum** *(1 mark)*
- **Time-box:** **15 minutes**, held at the same time and place each day.
- **Participants:** Developers (PO and SM may attend but do not disrupt).
- **Purpose:** Synchronize activities, inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal, plan work for the next 24 hours, surface impediments.

**4. Sprint Review** *(1 mark)*
- **Time-box:** ≤ 4 hours for a 1-month Sprint (~2 hours for a 2-week Sprint).
- **Participants:** Whole Scrum Team **+ Stakeholders**.
- **Purpose:** Inspect the Increment produced and adapt the Product Backlog based on stakeholder feedback.

**5. Sprint Retrospective** *(1 mark)*
- **Time-box:** ≤ 3 hours for a 1-month Sprint (~1.5 hours for a 2-week Sprint).
- **Participants:** Whole Scrum Team **only** (private).
- **Purpose:** Inspect and adapt the team's process, tools, relationships. Output: actionable improvement items for the next Sprint.

---

#### **Part (b) — Velocity & Release Planning (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the average velocity** *(2 marks)*

$$V_{avg} = \frac{18 + 22 + 20 + 25 + 23}{5} = \frac{108}{5} = 21.6 \text{ SP/Sprint}$$

**Step 2: Compute number of Sprints for the Release** *(3 marks)*

$$\text{Sprints}_{\text{release}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Planned Scope}}{V_{avg}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{140}{21.6} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.48 \rceil = 7 \text{ Sprints}$$

**Step 3: Convert to calendar time** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Calendar Weeks} = 7 \text{ Sprints} \times 2 \text{ weeks/Sprint} = 14 \text{ weeks} = 3.5 \text{ months}$$

**Conclusion:** The team will require **7 Sprints (14 calendar weeks / 3.5 months)** to complete the planned Release scope of 140 story points.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **three Scrum artifacts** along with their **commitments**. For each artifact, describe the contents, purpose, and the role responsible for its management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-week Sprint has a **total Sprint Backlog of 40 story points**. The team's **ideal capacity** is 8 developer-days per day of the Sprint (assume 10 working days). 
 - **(i)** Derive the equation for the **ideal burndown line** and tabulate the ideal remaining work for Days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10.
 - **(ii)** If by Day 4 the team has completed only **8 story points** of work, what does the actual remaining work look like, and what is the **gap from ideal**? What action should the Scrum Master take? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Three Scrum Artifacts (7 marks)**

**1. Product Backlog** *(3 marks)*
- **Commitment:** **Product Goal** — the long-term objective for the Scrum Team.
- **Contents:** Ordered, emergent list of *what* is needed to improve the product. Includes features, enhancements, bug fixes, technical work.
- **Purpose:** Single source of *what* the team works on. Emergent = continuously refined.
- **Managed by:** **Product Owner** (sole authority on ordering, content, and visibility).

**2. Sprint Backlog** *(2 marks)*
- **Commitment:** **Sprint Goal** — the single objective for the Sprint.
- **Contents:** The Sprint Goal, the set of Product Backlog Items selected for the Sprint, plus an actionable plan for delivering the Increment.
- **Purpose:** Real-time, highly visible picture of the work the Developers are doing during the Sprint.
- **Managed by:** **Developers** (self-managing; updated daily).

**3. Increment** *(2 marks)*
- **Commitment:** **Definition of Done (DoD)** — a formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the required quality measures.
- **Contents:** The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint, integrated with the value of all previous Sprints' increments.
- **Purpose:** A concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal; must be *usable* and meet DoD.
- **Managed by:** **Developers** (build it); **PO** (validates it meets AC).

---

#### **Part (b) — Burndown Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Derive the Ideal Burndown Equation** *(2 marks)*

The ideal line is a straight line from total work at Day 0 to zero at Day $D$:

$$\text{Ideal Remaining}(t) = W_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{D}\right)$$

With $W_{total} = 40$ SP and $D = 10$ days:

$$\text{Ideal Remaining}(t) = 40 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{10}\right)$$

**Step 2: Tabulate Ideal Burndown** *(2 marks)*

| Day $t$ | Ideal Remaining (SP) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 40 |
| 2 | 32 |
| 4 | 24 |
| 6 | 16 |
| 8 | 8 |
| 10 | 0 |

**Step 3: Actual Remaining at Day 4** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Actual Remaining}(4) = W_{total} - \text{Work Done}(4) = 40 - 8 = 32 \text{ SP}$$

**Step 4: Gap from Ideal and Action** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{Gap} = 32 - 24 = 8 \text{ SP behind ideal}$$

**Scrum Master's Action:**
- Facilitate the **Daily Scrum** to surface blockers.
- **Remove impediments** causing the slippage (e.g., unclear requirements, environment issues, missing dependencies).
- **Do NOT reassign work** or extend the Sprint (Sprint duration is fixed).
- Help the team re-plan the remaining work; if Sprint Goal becomes unattainable, discuss with the PO whether to cancel the Sprint.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Scrum Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Review with Retrospective:** Review inspects the *product*; Retrospective inspects the *process*. Examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Missing the commitment for each artifact:** The Scrum Guide binds Product Backlog→Product Goal, Sprint Backlog→Sprint Goal, Increment→DoD. Writing only the artifact name without the commitment costs **1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Calling the Scrum Master a "Project Manager":** This is the most penalized error. The SM is a *servant-leader* with no authority over the team. The team is *self-managing*.
> 
> 4. **Wrong time-boxes:** Daily Scrum is **15 minutes** — not 30, not 60. Sprint Planning for a 2-week Sprint is **≤ 4 hours**, not 8.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting Burndown/Burnup math:** For any question involving velocity or release planning, *show the formula, substitution, and final answer*. Examiners give **partial marks only** for the formula, not the answer.
> 
> 6. **Mis-stating "who can cancel a Sprint":** Only the **Product Owner** can cancel a Sprint, and only when the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete. The Scrum Master **cannot** cancel it.
> 
> 7. **Writing "Definition of Done" for a single item:** DoD applies to the **Increment as a whole**, not to individual PBIs. Acceptance criteria is the per-item contract.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this checklist. It is your last-minute KTU board exam sheet.**

### 🧱 The Three Accountabilities (Roles)
- ☐ **Product Owner** — owns Product Backlog, maximizes value, *sole person* to cancel Sprint.
- ☐ **Scrum Master** — *servant-leader*, removes impediments, facilitates events; **NOT a project manager**.
- ☐ **Developers** — cross-functional (3–9 in number), self-managing, build the Increment.

### 📦 The Three Artifacts & Commitments
- ☐ **Product Backlog** → committed to **Product Goal**.
- ☐ **Sprint Backlog** → committed to **Sprint Goal**.
- ☐ **Increment** → committed to **Definition of Done (DoD)**.

### 🗓️ The Five Events (Time-Boxes for 2-Week Sprint)
- ☐ **Sprint** — container (2 weeks).
- ☐ **Sprint Planning** — ≤ 4 hrs (Part 1: Why; Part 2: How).
- ☐ **Daily Scrum** — **15 min**, Developers only synchronize.
- ☐ **Sprint Review** — ≤ 2 hrs, inspect Increment with stakeholders.
- ☐ **Sprint Retrospective** — ≤ 1.5 hrs, Scrum Team only, improve process.

### 🧠 Critical Definitions
- ☐ **Sprint Goal:** *One* objective for the Sprint; provides focus and flexibility.
- ☐ **Product Goal:** Long-term objective; each Sprint advances it.
- ☐ **DoD vs. AC:** DoD = team-level quality contract; AC = per-item contract.
- ☐ **Done Increment:** Meets DoD, usable, potentially releasable, additive to all prior.

### 📊 The Key Formulas
- ☐ Velocity: $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{SP Completed}}{\text{Number of Sprints}}$
- ☐ Sprints for Release: $\lceil \text{Scope}/V_{avg} \rceil$
- ☐ Ideal Burndown: $W(t) = W_{total} \cdot (1 - t/D)$

### 🔑 The High-Yield Distinctions
- ☐ **Review = Product**; **Retrospective = Process**.
- ☐ **DoD = Team-wide**; **AC = Item-specific**.
- ☐ **WSJF ordering** = value + criticality + risk, divided by size.
- ☐ **Sprint cannot be shortened**; only the **PO can cancel** it.

### 🎯 Mnemonics
- ☐ **"PO, SM, Dev"** = the three roles.
- ☐ **"P-S-I"** = Product, Sprint, Increment artifacts.
- ☐ **"S-SPDR"** = Sprint, Sprint Planning, Daily, Review, Retrospective events.
- ☐ **"TIA"** = Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation — the three pillars.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Scrum Framework. You are now fully equipped for the KTU PEAIT413 board exam on this topic.** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
