# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Refactoring. State any four code smells with their corresponding refactoring techniques. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Refactoring is a *behavior-preserving* code transformation technique that improves the internal structure of software without altering its external behavior. (Martin Fowler)
- **Four code smells and refactorings (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Duplicated Code →** Extract Method / Extract Class
  2. **Long Method →** Extract Method
  3. **Long Parameter List →** Introduce Parameter Object
  4. **Switch Statement →** Replace Conditional with Polymorphism

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Refactoring* with *Rewriting* or *Optimization*. Rewriting changes behavior; optimization changes performance characteristics. Only refactoring keeps external behavior *identical* and focuses on *internal quality*. Lose 1 mark for this error.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**
- **Continuous Integration (CI):** Developers integrate code into a shared repository frequently (daily); each integration is verified by an automated build and tests. The artifact is produced but **not automatically deployed**.
- **Continuous Delivery (CDel):** Extends CI by ensuring that every successful build is *automatically packaged* and is **always ready for manual one-click deployment** to production.
- **Continuous Deployment (CDep):** The ultimate step — every successful build that passes all gates is *automatically deployed to production* with **zero human intervention**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "CI/CD" without distinguishing Delivery from Deployment. CDel has a *manual approval gate* before production; CDep has *no manual gate*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the SOLID design principles in detail with one example for each. Discuss how they support agile design practices. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) (1.4 marks):**
A class should have only one reason to change, i.e., one responsibility.
```java
// BAD: Two responsibilities
class Employee {
    void calculatePay() { ... }    // business logic
    void saveToDatabase() { ... }  // persistence
}
// GOOD: Split into two classes
class Employee { void calculatePay() { ... } }
class EmployeeRepository { void save(Employee e) { ... } }
```

**Open/Closed Principle (OCP) (1.4 marks):**
Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. Add new behavior via new classes, not by editing existing tested code.
```java
interface Shape { double area(); }
class Circle implements Shape { ... }
class Square implements Shape { ... }
// New requirement: Triangle — just add a class; no edit to existing code.
```

**Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) (1.4 marks):**
Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without breaking the program. Subclass must honor parent's contract (preconditions, postconditions, invariants).

**Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) (1.4 marks):**
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Prefer many small role-specific interfaces over one fat interface.
```java
// BAD: one fat interface
interface Worker { void work(); void eat(); }
// GOOD: split
interface Workable { void work(); }
interface Eatable { void eat(); }
```

**Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) (1.4 marks):**
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions. Use interfaces and dependency injection.
```java
interface MessageSender { void send(String msg); }
class EmailSender implements MessageSender { ... }
class NotificationService {
    private MessageSender sender;        // abstraction
    NotificationService(MessageSender s) { this.sender = s; }
}
```

**How they support Agile design (bonus integration — covered in remaining 0.5 mark if mentioned):**
SOLID principles give Agile teams a *code-level heuristic* for refactoring decisions, enabling emergent design, reducing technical debt, supporting change, and aligning with the Four Rules of Simple Design.

---

#### (b) What is Continuous Integration? Explain the key practices of CI with a neat block diagram. List any two popular CI tools. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Continuous Integration is an Agile practice where developers integrate their work into a shared repository multiple times a day, with each integration verified by an automated build and test suite to detect errors quickly.

**Key Practices (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Single Source Repository:** All code (production, tests, DB scripts, libraries) in one VCS.
2. **Automate the Build:** A single command builds and tests the entire system, including test data.
3. **Self-Testing Build:** Build includes automated unit and integration tests that detect defects.
4. **Frequent Commits:** Every developer commits to the mainline at least daily, reducing merge pain.
5. **Fast Builds:** Keep the build under **10 minutes**; slow builds are skipped by developers.
6. **Test in a Clone of Production:** Use identical hardware, OS, libraries, and middleware.
7. **Make Build Results Visible:** Big visible displays (info radiators) and instant notifications.
8. **Automate Deployment:** One-step deployment to any environment.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
```
Developer 1 ──┐
Developer 2 ──┼──→ Shared Git Repo ──→ CI Server ──→ Build ──→ Test ──→ Deploy Staging
Developer 3 ──┘                              │
                                             ├──→ Slack/Email Notification
                                             └──→ Build Status Dashboard
```

**Two CI Tools (1 mark — 0.5 each):** **Jenkins** (open-source, vast plugin ecosystem) and **GitHub Actions** (native to GitHub, YAML-based pipelines). (Also accept: GitLab CI, CircleCI, Bamboo, Travis CI, TeamCity.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the concept of Refactoring. Discuss any six important refactoring techniques with before/after code snippets. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Refactoring (1 mark):** Refactoring is a *disciplined, behavior-preserving* technique for restructuring existing code to improve its internal structure (readability, maintainability, design) without changing its external observable behavior. It is driven by Fowler's catalog of named techniques and supported by a safety net of automated tests.

**Six Important Refactoring Techniques (6 marks — 1 each):**

**1. Extract Method:**
```java
// BEFORE
void printBill() {
    printHeader();
    double sub = amount;
    double tax = amount * 0.1;
    printFooter(sub, tax);
}
// AFTER
void printBill() {
    printHeader();
    double total = calculateTotal();
    printFooter(total);
}
double calculateTotal() { return amount + amount * 0.1; }
```

**2. Inline Method:**
```java
// BEFORE
int rating() { return (moreThanFiveDeliveries()) ? 2 : 1; }
boolean moreThanFiveDeliveries() { return numberOfLateDeliveries > 5; }
// AFTER
int rating() { return (numberOfLateDeliveries > 5) ? 2 : 1; }
```

**3. Move Method:**
```java
// BEFORE (method uses Account class more than its own)
class Plan { double monthlyCost() { return rate * 5 + account.monthlyUsage(); } }
// AFTER
class Account { double monthlyCost(Plan p) { return p.rate * 5 + monthlyUsage(); } }
```

**4. Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant:**
```java
// BEFORE
double energy(double mass) { return mass * 9.81; }
// AFTER
static final double GRAVITY = 9.81;
double energy(double mass) { return mass * GRAVITY; }
```

**5. Encapsulate Field:**
```java
// BEFORE
public String name;
// AFTER
private String name;
public String getName() { return name; }
public void setName(String n) { name = n; }
```

**6. Replace Conditional with Polymorphism:**
```java
// BEFORE
double pay(Employee e) {
    switch (e.type) {
        case COMMISSIONED: return e.commission;
        case HOURLY: return e.hourlyRate * 40;
        case SALARIED: return e.salary;
    }
}
// AFTER: create abstract Employee with getPay() overridden in subclasses
```

---

#### (b) What is Version Control? Compare Centralized and Distributed Version Control Systems. Explain any five Git commands with their syntax. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Version Control is a system that records changes to files over time, allowing recall of specific versions later, enabling collaboration, history tracking, and rollback. Git is the de facto standard in Agile teams.

**Centralized vs Distributed VCS (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralized (CVCS) | Distributed (DVCS) |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Examples** | SVN, CVS, Perforce | Git, Mercurial |
| **Repository location** | One central server | Every client has full copy |
| **Network dependency** | Required for most operations | Fully functional offline |
| **History** | Server has it; clients have snapshot | All clients have full history |
| **Performance** | Slower (network bound) | Fast local operations |
| **Single point of failure** | Yes (server down = no work) | No (any clone is a backup) |
| **Branching/Merging** | Slow, heavyweight | Lightweight, fast |
| **Collaboration** | Locked, pessimistic | Optimistic, parallel |

**Five Git Commands (3 marks — 0.6 each):**

1. **`git init`** — Initializes a new empty Git repository in the current directory.
2. **`git clone <url>`** — Creates a local copy of a remote repository including its full history.
3. **`git add <file>`** — Stages a modified file, marking it for inclusion in the next commit.
4. **`git commit -m "message"`** — Records the staged snapshot permanently in the local repository.
5. **`git push origin <branch>`** — Uploads local branch commits to the remote repository named `origin`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. Writing "Git is centralized" — **wrong**, Git is a *distributed* VCS. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing `git pull` with `git fetch`. `pull` = `fetch` + `merge`. Cite the difference for full marks.
> 3. Skipping the *behavior-preserving* keyword in the refactoring definition — examiners allot 0.5 marks specifically for this phrase.
> 4. For SOLID, students often forget **L** (Liskov) or merge SRP with ISP. State each *separately* with a clean example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Rapid-Fire Revision — Must-Know Bullets**

- **Agile Design** = *Emergent* design guided by principles, tests, and refactoring — not BDUF.
- **Four Rules of Simple Design (Kent Beck):** Tests pass → Reveals intention → No duplication → Fewest elements.
- **SOLID:** SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP — the five pillars of object-oriented agile design.
- **Supporting Heuristics:** DRY, KISS, YAGNI, FCoI (Favor Composition over Inheritance), LoD (Law of Demeter).
- **Refactoring = behavior-preserving code transformation** (Fowler). *Behavior-preserving* is the key phrase.
- **Refactoring is NOT:** rewriting, adding features, fixing bugs, or performance optimization.
- **Red-Green-Refactor** is the TDD cycle; refactoring is the *third* and most important step.
- **Rule of Three:** Tolerate duplication twice, refactor on the third occurrence.
- **70+ refactoring techniques** grouped into 6 categories: Composing Methods, Moving Features, Organizing Data, Simplifying Conditionals, Making Method Calls Simpler, Dealing with Generalization.
- **Common smells:** Duplicated Code, Long Method, Large Class, Long Parameter List, Feature Envy, Switch Statements, Primitive Obsession, Data Clumps, Speculative Generality, Comments.
- **Continuous Integration (CI)** = frequent commits + automated build + automated tests + fast feedback (< 10 min).
- **CI ≠ CD:** CI = integration only; **CDel** = always deployable (manual gate); **CDep** = auto-deploy to production.
- **CI Pipeline stages:** Commit → Trigger → Checkout → Compile → Test → Static Analysis → Package → Deploy Staging → Notify.
- **Build Tools:** Maven (Java, declarative POM), Gradle (JVM, Groovy/Kotlin DSL), Ant (Java, imperative), Make (C/C++), MSBuild (.NET), npm (JS).
- **Version Control = central nervous system** of Agile — VCS is non-negotiable.
- **CVCS** (SVN, CVS) — single central server, no offline work, slow branches.
- **DVCS** (Git, Mercurial) — every client has full history, offline-capable, fast branches.
- **Git essentials:** `init`, `clone`, `add`, `commit`, `push`, `pull`, `fetch`, `merge`, `rebase`, `branch`, `checkout`, `stash`, `cherry-pick`, `tag`, `revert`, `reset`.
- **Three States of Git:** Modified → Staged → Committed.
- **Branching Models:** Git Flow (releases), GitHub Flow (SaaS), GitLab Flow (environments), Trunk-Based (true CI).
- **Golden Rule of Git:** *Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch.*
- **Code coverage target:** $\geq 80\%$; **Cyclomatic complexity target:** $V(G) \le 10$.
- **CI build benchmark:** feedback within **10 minutes**; commits at least **daily per developer**.
- **Industry insight:** CI accelerates defect feedback by $\approx 1000\times$ (minutes vs days).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 7 Topics in this Module:**
> **"A-D-R-R-C-B-V"** → **A**gile design → **D**esign principles → **R**efactoring need → **R**efactoring techniques → **C**ontinuous Integration → **B**uild tools → **V**ersion control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agility and Quality Assurance: Agile Interaction Design, Agile Approach to Quality Assurance, Test Driven Development, Pair Programming: Issues and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Agility and Quality Assurance: Agile Interaction Design, Agile Approach to Quality Assurance, Test Driven Development, Pair Programming: Issues and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Agile Interaction Design — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Agile Interaction Design (AID) is a user-centered design methodology that integrates the iterative, incremental, and collaborative principles of Agile software development with the human-computer interaction (HCI) design process. It aims to deliver usable, useful, and desirable software by continuously involving end-users, designers, and developers in short, time-boxed design cycles.*

The core philosophy of Agile Interaction Design rests on the **Co-Design Principle** — designers and developers working shoulder-to-shoulder, treating usability defects with the same severity as functional defects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pop-Up Restaurant:**
> Imagine a chef who cooks a dish **before** asking the customer what they want, plates it, and then expects applause. This is the *Waterfall* design approach. 
> Now picture a *Pop-Up Restaurant* where the chef invites **one customer per night**, cooks a small tasting menu, watches their reaction, and adjusts tomorrow's menu accordingly. This is **Agile Interaction Design** — every iteration is a small, edible, evaluable design prototype. The customer is the user, the chef is the design-developer pair, and each night is a *Sprint*.

### 1.3 Agile Approach to Quality Assurance — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *The Agile Approach to QA is a philosophy where quality is treated as a built-in, continuous, team-wide responsibility rather than a final-stage gate. Testing activities are integrated into every iteration of the development cycle, ensuring that defects are detected and resolved at the earliest possible moment — the famous "Shift-Left" principle.*

### 1.4 Test-Driven Development (TDD) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *Test-Driven Development is a short-iteration software development practice where the developer writes an automated unit test **before** writing the production code that satisfies it. The cycle follows three strict phases: **Red** (write a failing test), **Green** (write minimal code to pass), and **Refactor** (clean up the code while keeping tests green).*

### 1.5 Pair Programming — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *Pair Programming is an Extreme Programming (XP) engineering practice where two programmers work collaboratively at a single workstation. One acts as the **Driver** (writes the code), and the other as the **Navigator/Observer** (reviews each line in real-time, thinks strategically, and catches errors). The roles switch frequently, typically every 15–30 minutes.*

### 1.6 Quick-Reference Matrix of the Four Sub-Topics

| Sub-Topic | Core Question Answered | Primary Agile Value | Iteration Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| Agile Interaction Design | *How do we build the right UI/UX?* | Customer Collaboration | 1–2 weeks |
| Agile QA | *How do we build it right?* | Working Software | Continuous |
| Test-Driven Development | *How do we prove it works?* | Responding to Change | Minutes (Red-Green-Refactor) |
| Pair Programming | *How do we build it together?* | Individuals & Interactions | Hours (per task) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Agile Interaction Design — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

The lifecycle of an Agile Interaction Design cycle can be decomposed into **six iterative steps**:

1. **User Story Mapping** — The product backlog is decomposed into epics and user stories that describe user value, not technical tasks.
2. **Persona Development** — Fictional but research-driven archetypes (e.g., "Biju, the 35-year-old KTU B.Tech student") representing key user groups.
3. **Scenario Enactment** — Designers act out the persona's journey through the proposed interface to identify friction points.
4. **Low-Fidelity Prototyping** — Paper sketches, wireframes, or whiteboard mock-ups (cheap, disposable, fast to modify).
5. **User Testing & Feedback Loops** — Actual users interact with the prototype; feedback is captured in 1–2 hour sessions.
6. **Iteration & Convergence** — Designs are refined sprint-by-sprint until the usability metrics (success rate, time-on-task) reach acceptable thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this "Agile"?** — The feedback loop is *compressed* from months (traditional UCD) to days. The artefact is *just barely enough* (a sketch) and the design *evolves* with the code.

### 2.2 Agile Approach to Quality Assurance — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

The Agile QA philosophy is built on **five pillars**, often tested in KTU exams:

| # | Pillar | Mechanism | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shift-Left Testing** | Tests written alongside requirements | "Fail Fast" |
| 2 | **Whole Team Ownership** | Every team member writes/runs tests | "Quality is everyone's job" |
| 3 | **Continuous Integration** | Every commit triggers automated build + test | "Green Build" |
| 4 | **Test Automation Pyramid** | More unit tests, fewer UI tests | "Test Pyramid" |
| 5 | **Definition of Done (DoD)** | A story is not "Done" until tested, integrated, and documented | "Done Means Done" |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Agile Testing Quadrant** (Crispin & Gregory) is a frequently asked KTU question:
> - **Q1 (Unit/Component)** — supports the team (TDD)
> - **Q2 (Functional/Business)** — supports the team (Acceptance TDD)
> - **Q3 (Exploratory/Usability)** — critiques the product
> - **Q4 (Performance/Security/Load)** — critiques the product

### 2.3 Test-Driven Development — The Red-Green-Refactor Engine

**Step-by-step logic of the TDD cycle:**

- **Step 1 — RED:** Write the smallest possible test that fails. The test name documents the intended behaviour.
- **Step 2 — GREEN:** Write the *minimum* amount of production code to make the test pass. No gold-plating, no premature optimisation.
- **Step 3 — REFACTOR:** Improve the internal structure (remove duplication, improve names, simplify logic) **only** while all tests remain green. This is the only safe time to refactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The TDD Mantra:** *Never write a line of functional code without a broken test.* This forces every line of code to be *testable by design* — a side-effect that yields highly modular, loosely-coupled architectures (Dependency Injection, Pure Functions, etc.).

### 2.4 Pair Programming — The Driver-Navigator Dynamic

- **Driver:** Hands on the keyboard, focused on the tactical execution — syntax, variable names, current method's logic.
- **Navigator:** Hands off the keyboard, focused on the strategic context — does this fit the design? Is there a simpler algorithm? Are we missing edge cases?
- **Mandatory Role-Switch:** Every 15–30 minutes to prevent fatigue and maintain engagement (a KTU-favoured detail).
- **Pairing Styles:** Strong-Style Pairing (the Navigator has the idea, the Driver types it), Ping-Pong Pairing (in TDD — A writes test, B writes code, A writes next test, etc.).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Concept Metrics)

> [!TIP]
> The following metrics are *commonly* asked in 14-mark KTU questions. Memorize the formulas, not just the definitions.

$$
\text{Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)} = \frac{D_{\text{pre-release}}}{D_{\text{pre-release}} + D_{\text{post-release}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Test Coverage \%} = \frac{L_{\text{executed}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}
$$

$$
\text{Code Churn \%} = \frac{\text{LOC added + LOC deleted}}{\text{Total LOC}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In TDD, empirical studies (Koch, 2003 — *frequently cited in KTU answer keys*) report defect reduction of **40–90%** compared to test-last approaches, with an initial time overhead of **15–35%** that is recovered within 1–2 iterations.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Practices

- **Banking & FinTech:** TDD + Pair Programming is mandated by RBI-empanelled auditors for core banking systems to prevent transaction-loss defects.
- **Healthcare (FDA/IEC 62304):** Agile QA + TDD provides the documented evidence trail required for Class B/C medical software.
- **E-Commerce:** Agile Interaction Design is the *secret weapon* behind A/B-tested checkout flows (Amazon, Flipkart) — every button placement is sprint-tested.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Pair Programming is used as a *verification method* for DAL-B and above software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Four Sub-Topics

| Comparison Axis | Agile Interaction Design | Agile QA | TDD | Pair Programming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Build the *right* UX | Build it *right* | Build it *correctly* | Build it *together* |
| **Origin Practice** | User-Centered Design (UCD) | XP & DevOps | XP (Kent Beck, 2003) | XP (Kent Beck) |
| **Cadence** | Per Sprint (1–4 wks) | Continuous | Minutes | Hours per task |
| **Artefact Produced** | Wireframe / Prototype | Test Suite | Unit Test + Code | Production Code |
| **Measurable Benefit** | ↑ Task Success Rate | ↓ Defect Escape Rate | ↑ Test Coverage | ↓ Code Review Defects |
| **Biggest Risk** | "Big Design Up Front" relapse | Skipping regression tests | Test-first becomes test-only | Skill-imbalance / fatigue |

### 3.2 Derivative Logic — Why TDD Reduces Defect Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mathematical Intuition Behind TDD's Defect Reduction:**
> Let $D_{\text{traditional}}$ be the defects introduced in test-last coding and $D_{\text{TDD}}$ the defects in test-first. The empirical reduction factor is:

$$
\text{Reduction Factor (RF)} = \frac{D_{\text{traditional}} - D_{\text{TDD}}}{D_{\text{traditional}}}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation of why RF is high:**

- **Step 1 — Test as Specification:** Writing the test first forces the developer to *think* about the contract *before* the implementation. This eliminates the "happy-path-only" bias.

$$
\text{Contract Clarity} \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Ambiguous Code} \downarrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Defect Injection} \downarrow
$$

- **Step 2 — Granular Commits:** TDD produces very small, atomic commits (one test, one code change). Smaller diffs are *statistically* less defect-prone.

$$
\text{Defect Density} \propto \text{Commit Size} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Small Commits} \Rightarrow \text{Low Defect Density}
$$

- **Step 3 — Regression Net:** Every TDD test becomes a permanent guard rail. When new code is added, the *entire* suite runs.

$$
\text{Regression Coverage}_{\text{TDD}} \to 100\% \quad \text{of unit-tested paths}
$$

- **Step 4 — Combine the three factors:**

$$
D_{\text{TDD}} = D_{\text{traditional}} \times (1 - \text{ContractClarityEffect}) \times (1 - \text{GranularityEffect}) \times (\text{RegressionRecoveryFactor})
$$

- **Step 5 — Empirical plug-in (from published industry data):**

$$
\text{ContractClarityEffect} \approx 0.30, \quad \text{GranularityEffect} \approx 0.25, \quad \text{RegressionRecoveryFactor} \approx 0.40
$$

- **Step 6 — Final numerical estimate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{TDD}} &\approx D_{\text{traditional}} \times (1 - 0.30) \times (1 - 0.25) \times 0.40 \\
&\approx D_{\text{traditional}} \times 0.70 \times 0.75 \times 0.40 \\
&\approx D_{\text{traditional}} \times 0.21
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Step 7 — Conclusion:** Roughly a **79% defect reduction**, which is consistent with the 40–90% range reported in empirical literature and is the **exact number KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison — TDD vs Traditional Test-Last

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{traditional}} &= T_{\text{dev}} + T_{\text{test}} + T_{\text{fix}} \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{TDD}} &= T_{\text{test-write}} + T_{\text{dev}} + T_{\text{refactor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- In *traditional*, $T_{\text{fix}}$ is large because defects are found late (in QA or worse, in production).
- In *TDD*, $T_{\text{fix}}$ is near zero because defects are caught within the *same minute* they are introduced.
- Empirically, $\frac{T_{\text{test-write}} + T_{\text{refactor}}}{T_{\text{test}} + T_{\text{fix}}} < 1$ after **2–3 sprints** of practice.

### 3.4 Issues & Challenges — The Full Risk Register

> [!NOTE]
> **The 14-mark KTU question on Pair Programming explicitly asks for *issues and challenges*.** The following table is your gold mine.

| # | Challenge | Root Cause | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **2× Personnel Cost** | Two engineers, one keyboard | Use for high-risk, novel modules only |
| 2 | **Personality Clash** | Ego, introversion, cultural differences | Rotate pairs weekly; team-charter norms |
| 3 | **Skill Imbalance** | Expert + Novice → Novice gets bored OR expert patronises | "Strong-Style" pairing; structured mentorship |
| 4 | **Driver Fatigue** | Sustained intense focus → errors after 4 hrs | Pomodoro (25 min) + mandatory breaks |
| 5 | **Knowledge Silo Risk** | "I always drive with X" → bus-factor of 2 | Enforce **N-pairing rule** (everyone pairs with everyone monthly) |
| 6 | **Introvert Resistance** | Cultural barrier in KTU classrooms | Start with mob programming for onboarding |
| 7 | **Over-Reliance on Pair** | Code reviews get skipped | Retain pull-request reviews for distributed teams |
| 8 | **Task Suitability** | Trivial boilerplate doesn't need pairing | Reserve pairing for complex logic / refactors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Agile Quality Ecosystem — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Product Backlog] --> B[Sprint Planning]
    B --> C[User Story + Acceptance Criteria]
    C --> D[Agile Interaction Design Sprint]
    D --> E[Low-Fi Prototype + User Test]
    E --> F[Definition of Ready Met]
    F --> G[Test Driven Development Red]
    G --> H[TDD Green Minimal Code]
    H --> I[TDD Refactor]
    I --> J[Pair Programming Continuous Review]
    J --> K[Continuous Integration Build]
    K --> L{All Tests Green?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Acceptance Test Sprint Demo]
    L -- No --> G
    M --> N[Definition of Done Verified]
    N --> O[Shippable Increment]
```

### 4.2 The TDD Red-Green-Refactor State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RedPhase
    RedPhase: RED Phase - Write Failing Test
    RedPhase --> GreenPhase: Run test - see failure
    GreenPhase: GREEN Phase - Write Minimal Code
    GreenPhase --> RefactorPhase: Run test - see pass
    RefactorPhase: REFACTOR Phase - Clean Code
    RefactorPhase --> RedPhase: Next Behaviour or Edge Case
    RefactorPhase --> DoneCheck: All Behaviours Covered
    DoneCheck: All Tests Green + Code Clean
    DoneCheck --> [*]
```

### 4.3 The Pair Programming Role-Switching Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver] -- 15 to 30 minutes --> B[Navigator]
    B -- Switch Roles --> A
    A --> C[Write Code Tactical]
    A --> D[Focus on Syntax and Logic]
    B --> E[Strategic Review]
    B --> F[Spot Edge Cases and Bugs]
    B --> G[Think Big Picture Design]
    C --> H[Shared Mental Model]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Higher Quality Working Software]
```

### 4.4 The Agile Interaction Design Sprint Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Sprint Kickoff]) --> P1[Persona Review and Update]
    P1 --> P2[User Story Mapping Session]
    P2 --> P3[Paper Wireframe Sketch]
    P3 --> P4[Low Fidelity Prototype]
    P4 --> P5[Usability Test with 3 to 5 Users]
    P5 --> Decision{Meets SUS Score Above 68?}
    Decision -- No --> P3
    Decision -- Yes --> P6[High Fidelity Mockup]
    P6 --> P7[Developer Handoff with Annotated Spec]
    P7 --> End([Sprint Review Demo])
```

### 4.5 Agile Testing Quadrant — Crispin and Gregory

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Agile Testing Quadrant
    x-axis "Supports the Team" --> "Critiques the Product"
    y-axis "Business Facing" --> "Technology Facing"
    quadrant-1 "Q1 - Technology, Team"
    quadrant-2 "Q2 - Business, Team"
    quadrant-3 "Q3 - Business, Product"
    quadrant-4 "Q4 - Technology, Product"
    Q1: [0.2, 0.2]
    Q2: [0.2, 0.8]
    Q3: [0.8, 0.2]
    Q4: [0.8, 0.8]
```

> [!TIP]
> If the rendering platform does not support Mermaid quadrant charts, the equivalent logical mapping is:
> - **Q1 (Tech, Team):** Unit tests, component tests
> - **Q2 (Business, Team):** Functional tests, story tests, acceptance TDD
> - **Q3 (Business, Product):** Exploratory testing, usability, user acceptance
> - **Q4 (Tech, Product):** Performance, load, security, scalability
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define Agile Interaction Design. List any four of its key characteristics.**

*Model Answer (for 3 marks):*
Agile Interaction Design is a user-centered design methodology that integrates iterative Agile principles with the HCI design process. **Four key characteristics:**
1. Iterative, time-boxed design cycles aligned with sprints.
2. Continuous user involvement through short usability test loops.
3. Collaborative co-design between designers and developers (no "throw over the wall").
4. Use of just-enough artefacts — sketches, wireframes, low-fi prototypes — over heavy documentation.
5. *Bonus point:* Acceptance criteria drive both design and test.

---

**Q2. What is the "Red-Green-Refactor" cycle in Test-Driven Development? Briefly explain each phase.**

*Model Answer (for 3 marks):*
TDD follows a strict three-phase micro-cycle:
- **RED Phase:** A new automated unit test is written that defines a desired behaviour or improvement. The test must initially *fail* because the production code does not yet exist or is incomplete. This phase proves the test is actually checking something.
- **GREEN Phase:** The developer writes the *minimum* amount of production code required to make the failing test pass. No extra features, no premature optimisation — just enough to turn the bar green.
- **REFACTOR Phase:** With the safety net of a green test suite, the developer cleans up the code — removes duplication, improves naming, simplifies logic — *without* changing external behaviour. All tests must remain green throughout.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Pattern: Internal Choice Provided]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** The Choice — *TDD + Pair Programming Deep Dive*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Test-Driven Development methodology in detail. Discuss the three phases with a suitable example, and list **five advantages** and **three limitations** of TDD.

*Model Answer:*

**Definition (1 mark):** TDD is an iterative software development practice in which automated unit tests are written *before* the production code, following the Red-Green-Refactor cycle.

**The Three Phases (4 marks — 1.33 marks each):**
1. **RED — Write a Failing Test:** The developer first writes a unit test for the *next* small piece of functionality. The test fails because no code exists. Example: For a `calculateDiscount(price, percentage)` function, the test is `assert calculateDiscount(100, 10) == 90`. Running it yields a "function not defined" error — red bar.
2. **GREEN — Make It Pass:** The developer writes the *bare minimum* code to satisfy the test, e.g., `return price - percentage`. The test passes — green bar. No extra logic, no error handling beyond what the test demands.
3. **REFACTOR — Clean Up:** With the test green, the developer improves the code (renames variables, extracts helper methods, removes duplication). Example: Replace `return price - percentage` with `return price * (1 - percentage/100)`. The test must still pass.

**Five Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Forces modular, testable design (high cohesion, low coupling).
2. Provides a living regression test suite — safety net for future changes.
3. Documents intent — tests serve as executable specifications.
4. Reduces post-release defect rate by 40–90% (Koch, 2003).
5. Lowers debugging time — defects are caught within minutes of injection.

**Three Limitations (0.5 marks):**
1. Steep learning curve; slow initially (15–35% time overhead).
2. Not suitable for UI-heavy or one-off throwaway prototypes.
3. Requires disciplined team culture — easy to skip steps under deadline pressure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss Pair Programming in detail. Explain the Driver-Navigator roles, the typical work cycle, and **six significant issues/challenges** with their mitigations.

*Model Answer:*

**Definition (1 mark):** Pair Programming is an XP practice where two developers work at a single workstation — one driving (typing), one navigating (reviewing and thinking strategically).

**Driver-Navigator Roles (2 marks):**
- **Driver** — Tactical focus. Hands on the keyboard. Concerned with current line, syntax, variable naming, immediate test passing.
- **Navigator** — Strategic focus. Hands off the keyboard. Reviews each line as it is written, considers the bigger design, spots edge cases, and thinks about the next test. The Navigator also serves as a real-time code reviewer.

**Work Cycle (1 mark):** Roles switch every **15–30 minutes** to prevent fatigue and ensure both partners remain engaged. Pairs may be rotated daily or weekly to spread knowledge across the team.

**Six Issues & Challenges (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Challenge | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Doubled personnel cost** | Use pairing selectively for complex / high-risk modules. |
| 2 | **Personality & ego clashes** | Establish team-charter norms; rotate pairs. |
| 3 | **Skill imbalance** (expert + novice) | Adopt strong-style pairing; structure as a teaching session. |
| 4 | **Driver fatigue** from sustained focus | Use Pomodoro (25 min) + mandatory breaks. |
| 5 | **Knowledge silo** ("always pair with X") | Enforce N-pairing rule — every dev pairs with every other dev monthly. |
| 6 | **Introvert resistance** & cultural discomfort | Start with mob programming, gradually transition to pairs. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> *Most students lose 2–3 marks on Part B by listing challenges **without** mitigations. KTU 2024 scheme values the "Issue → Mitigation" pairing. Always present them in a two-column table to earn full marks.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** The Alternative Choice — *Agile Interaction Design + Agile QA*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Agile Interaction Design. Describe the six-step process with a suitable real-world example, and contrast it with traditional Big-Design-Up-Front (BDUF).

*Model Answer:*

**Definition (1 mark):** Agile Interaction Design is a user-centered methodology that integrates iterative Agile development with HCI design, producing usable software through continuous user feedback in short cycles.

**Six-Step Process (4 marks — ~0.65 each):**
1. **User Story Mapping** — Decompose the product backlog into epics and user stories. *Example: For a KTU student portal, an epic "Examination Module" breaks into stories "View timetable", "Download hall ticket", "Check results".*
2. **Persona Development** — Create 2–4 personas. *Example: "Anand, 20, B.Tech CS, smartphone-first user, impatient with slow UI."*
3. **Scenario Enactment** — Walk the persona through the proposed flow on paper.
4. **Low-Fidelity Prototyping** — Sketch wireframes in pen on paper or Balsamiq.
5. **User Testing** — 3–5 representative users attempt the prototype. Time them, record errors, capture SUS score.
6. **Iteration** — Refine based on findings; loop back to step 3 until usability threshold met.

**BDUF vs Agile Interaction Design (2 marks — 1 mark each axis):**

| Dimension | BDUF | Agile Interaction Design |
|---|---|---|
| Timeline | Months of design before code | Days per design iteration |
| Artefact | 200-page UI specification | Sketches + interactive prototype |
| User Feedback | Once, near release | Multiple times per sprint |
| Change Cost | Very high (re-do design) | Low (re-draw sketch) |
| Risk | High (single point of failure) | Distributed across many small experiments |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Agile Approach to Quality Assurance. Explain the **five pillars** of Agile QA, the **Agile Testing Quadrant** (Crispin & Gregory), and the role of the **Definition of Done (DoD)** in ensuring quality.

*Model Answer:*

**Introduction (1 mark):** Agile QA embeds quality into every sprint through continuous testing, automation, and shared team ownership — quality is a *behaviour*, not a *phase*.

**Five Pillars (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Shift-Left Testing** — Tests written alongside requirements, not at the end. Failures are surfaced early when they are 10–100× cheaper to fix.
2. **Whole Team Ownership** — Developers, testers, BAs all participate in test design and execution. No "QA silo".
3. **Continuous Integration & Continuous Testing** — Every commit triggers an automated build + test run. "Green build" is a team-wide visible signal.
4. **Test Automation Pyramid** — Many unit tests (base), fewer integration tests (middle), very few UI tests (top). Maximises speed and reliability.
5. **Definition of Done (DoD)** — A checklist that a story must satisfy before it is "Done": coded, unit tested, code-reviewed, integrated, acceptance-tested, documented, deployed to staging.

**Agile Testing Quadrant (2 marks):** *See Section 4.5 diagram.* Crispin & Gregory's quadrant classifies tests along two axes — *Business-facing vs Technology-facing* and *Supports team vs Critiques product*. Q1 = unit tests, Q2 = functional/acceptance tests, Q3 = exploratory/usability tests, Q4 = non-functional tests (performance, security).

**Role of DoD (1 mark):** DoD is the *quality contract* of the team. It prevents stories from being marked "Done" prematurely. A typical DoD includes: code written, unit tests passing, peer review done, acceptance criteria met, documentation updated, deployed to test environment, and no critical SonarQube violations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B(b):**
> *Students often confuse "Definition of Done" (DoD) with "Acceptance Criteria". Remember:* **Acceptance Criteria** are *story-specific* (belongs to one user story), whereas the **Definition of Done** is *team-wide* (applies to every story). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction in KTU valuations.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Must-Memorize" Five-Point Recap before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ **Agile Interaction Design** = Agile + UCD. Six steps: Story Map → Persona → Scenario → Wireframe → User Test → Iterate. Key contrast with BDUF: *small, cheap, disposable artefacts* over heavy specs.
- ✅ **Agile QA** = Quality is *embedded*, not bolted on. Five pillars: Shift-Left, Whole Team, CI, Test Pyramid, DoD. Remember the **Agile Testing Quadrant** by Crispin & Gregory.
- ✅ **TDD** = Red-Green-Refactor. *Test first, code minimal, refactor safe*. Empirical defect reduction: **40–90%**. Time overhead initially: **15–35%** (recovered by sprint 2–3).
- ✅ **Pair Programming** = Driver + Navigator. Switch every 15–30 min. Cost is **2× personnel**, but you gain real-time review and knowledge transfer. Issues = Skill gap, fatigue, ego, silos. Always pair *issue + mitigation* in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **The KTU-Favoured Buzzwords** that earn easy marks: *Definition of Done, Acceptance Criteria, Shift-Left, Fail Fast, Test Pyramid, Co-Design, Strong-Style Pairing, N-pairing Rule, Continuous Integration, Living Documentation.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Extreme Programming (XP): XP Lifecycle, The XP Team, XP Concepts, Refactoring, Technical Debt, Timeboxing, Stories, Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT413 - AGILE METHODOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Extreme Programming (XP): XP Lifecycle, The XP Team, XP Concepts, Refactoring, Technical Debt, Timeboxing, Stories, Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Extreme Programming (XP)** is a lightweight, agility-focused **software development methodology** formulated by **Kent Beck** in the late 1990s. It is a discipline of software development based on values of **simplicity, communication, feedback, courage, and respect**, organized around a set of **12 core practices** grouped under four categories. XP advocates frequent releases in short development cycles, which is intended to improve productivity and introduce checkpoints at which new customer requirements can be adopted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim-friendly):** *XP is a software engineering methodology that emphasizes technical excellence, fine-grained feedback, and a sustainable pace of development through practices such as pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, refactoring, and small releases.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Formula 1 (F1) Pit Crew**
>
> Think of XP like a top **Formula 1 pit crew** during a Grand Prix race:
>
> - **The Car (Software System)** is constantly being improved during pit stops.
> - **The Crew (XP Team)** works in tightly-coordinated pairs, each with specialized roles (tyre changer, front jack, rear jack, lollipop man).
> - **The Driver (Customer)** communicates desired outcomes (lap times, race strategy) every few laps — that's *On-Site Customer* + *Planning Game*.
> - **Refactoring** = the crew continually tightening bolts and improving parts between laps, even if the car still "works" — they don't wait for it to break.
> - **Small Releases** = the team never holds a fix for too long; a small, deployable improvement is always available.
> - **Sustainable Pace** = no crew works for 24 hours straight — exhaustion causes crashes.
> - **Test-First** = every nut and bolt is checked *before* the car leaves the pit box, not after.
> - **Timeboxing** = each pit stop must be completed within a hard window; if the tyre change isn't ready in 3 seconds, the strategy changes — the **time is sacred, scope flexes**.

This is exactly how XP teams operate: **fast, feedback-driven, technically excellent, and people-respecting**.

### 1.3 Key XP Metrics & Constants (KTU Mandatory)

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Iteration Length** | **1 to 4 weeks** (typically **2 weeks**) | Cadence of XP iteration |
| **Release Cycle** | Every iteration or every few iterations | Small Releases practice |
| **Pair Programming Duration** | Continuous (full day pairing) | Knowledge sharing |
| **Sustainable Work Week** | **40 hours / week (maximum)** | Anti-burnout rule |
| **Code Integration Frequency** | **Multiple times per day** | Continuous Integration |
| **Unit Test Coverage** | **>90%** (ideally 100%) | TDD discipline |
| **Velocity Window** | Last **3 to 5 iterations** | Estimation baseline |

> [!NOTE]
> These constants are universally accepted in KTU 2024 scheme answer keys. Memorize them as bold-marked bullet points.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 5 Core XP Values (Beck's Foundation)

1. **Communication** — Team shares information face-to-face, ideally on-site.
2. **Simplicity** — "What is the simplest thing that could possibly work?"
3. **Feedback** — Take small steps, get concrete feedback, and adjust.
4. **Courage** — Tell the truth about progress, estimates, and failures.
5. **Respect** — Every team member's work is valued; this is the **non-negotiable** root value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"List the 5 values of XP."* — All five must be stated; partial answers lose 1 mark per missing value.

### 2.2 The 12 XP Practices (Categorized)

#### **A. Fine-Scale Feedback Practices**
1. **Pair Programming** — Two programmers, one screen, continuous review.
2. **Planning Game** — Joint release + iteration planning with the customer.
3. **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** — Write the unit test *before* the production code (Red → Green → Refactor).
4. **On-Site Customer** — A real customer representative sits with the team.

#### **B. Continuous Process Practices**
5. **Continuous Integration (CI)** — Integrate and test code **multiple times per day**.
6. **Refactoring** — Restructure code without changing external behavior.
7. **Small Releases** — Release the smallest useful system into production frequently.

#### **C. Shared Understanding Practices**
8. **Simple Design** — No "just-in-case" features; design for *today's* needs.
9. **System Metaphor** — A simple shared story of how the system works.
10. **Collective Code Ownership** — *Anyone* can fix *any* code; no silos.
11. **Coding Standards** — Uniform code style enforced by team consensus.

#### **D. Programmer Welfare Practice**
12. **Sustainable Pace** — Teams work a **constant, sustainable pace**; overtime kills projects.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often write *"Pair programming doubles cost"* as a disadvantage. Wrong — Beck states that **total cost is lower** due to fewer defects, knowledge sharing, and faster onboarding. The cost of *two keyboards* ≠ the cost of *two heads*.

### 2.3 XP Concepts — Engineering the Heart of XP

#### 2.3.1 Refactoring

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Refactoring is the process of changing a software system in such a way that it does not alter the external behavior of the code yet improves its internal structure.* — Martin Fowler, *Refactoring* (1999).

**The "Two Hats" Principle (Fowler):**
- **Adding Function Hat** → add new features; tests must pass.
- **Refactoring Hat** → restructure code; **do not add new features**; tests must still pass.

**Common Refactoring Catalog (KTU High-Yield):**

| Smell | Refactoring Technique |
|---|---|
| Long Method | Extract Method |
| Duplicate Code | Extract Method / Pull Up Method |
| Long Parameter List | Introduce Parameter Object |
| Switch Statements | Replace Conditional with Polymorphism |
| Data Clumps | Extract Class |
| Primitive Obsession | Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant |
| Feature Envy | Move Method |
| Shotgun Surgery | Move Method / Inline Class |

**Why Refactor?**
- Improves design without altering behavior.
- Reduces **Technical Debt**.
- Eases future feature addition.
- Helps understand code (`preparatory refactoring`).

#### 2.3.2 Technical Debt

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Ward Cunningham, 1992):** *Technical Debt is the implied cost of additional rework caused by choosing an easy (limited) solution now instead of using a better approach that would take longer.*

**Metaphor:** If you don't refactor code, you're like a homeowner who skips oil changes. The car still drives, but the engine slowly degrades. Eventually, "interest" accrues and the system becomes unmaintainable.

**Types of Technical Debt (KTU Categorization):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Deliberate Debt** | Conscious "we'll fix later" decisions | Skipping unit tests to ship fast |
| **Accidental / Bit-rot Debt** | Code degrades with time | Old library versions, deprecated APIs |
| **Design Debt** | Poor initial design choices | Tight coupling, no abstractions |

**Cost Equation (Pragmatically Important):**

$$
\text{TotalCost} = \text{DevelopmentCost}_{\text{fast}} + \text{MaintenanceCost}_{\text{withDebt}} + \text{ReworkInterest}
$$

> **The longer the debt remains, the higher the "interest" paid per change.**

#### 2.3.3 Timeboxing

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Timeboxing is the practice of fixing a maximum amount of time for an activity, after which the activity stops regardless of whether objectives were met. The **time is sacred, the scope flexes**.*

**Two KTU-Recognized Modes:**

| Mode | Time Box | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Time Box** | Strictly fixed (e.g., 30 min stand-up) | May reduce |
| **Soft Time Box** | Roughly fixed (e.g., 1 hour) | Can extend slightly if 80%+ done |

**Where XP Uses Timeboxing:**
- Iteration length (typically 2 weeks).
- Daily stand-up / stand-up meeting (15 min).
- Planning Game session.
- Pair programming sub-tasks.

#### 2.3.4 Stories (User Stories)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Format (Bill Wake's "Connextra" template):**
>
> *As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit].*

**Example:**
> *As a **registered user**, I want to **reset my password via email**, so that **I can regain access if I forget it**.*

**INVEST Criteria (Kent Beck / Bill Wake — KTU High-Yield):**

| Letter | Property | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | Independent | Can be developed in any order |
| **N** | Negotiable | Open for discussion, not a contract |
| **V** | Valuable | Delivers value to customer/business |
| **E** | Estimable | Team can size it |
| **S** | Small | Fits in one iteration |
| **T** | Testable | Has clear acceptance criteria |

**Story Hierarchy (Epics, Themes, Stories, Tasks):**

$$
\text{Epic (large)} \;\to\; \text{Theme} \;\to\; \text{Story} \;\to\; \text{Task (smallest)}
$$

#### 2.3.5 Velocity

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Velocity is the amount of work (measured in story points, ideal engineering hours, or story count) a team can complete in a single iteration.*

**Purpose:**
- The team uses velocity to **predict** how much work it can do in the next iteration.
- Customers use it to **plan release dates**.
- Management uses it to **detect process degradation**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Full High-Yield Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Velocity** (per iteration) | $V = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} SP_i}{N}$ | Story Points / Iteration | Capacity measurement |
| 2 | **Release Date Estimation** | $\text{Iterations Required} = \dfrac{Total\ SP}{V}$ | Iterations | Project planning |
| 3 | **Time to Release** | $T_{release} = I_{req} \times L_{iter}$ | Weeks/Days | Project deadline |
| 4 | **Burn Rate** | $R_b = \dfrac{Story\ Points\ Remaining}{Iterations\ Remaining}$ | SP/Iter | Mid-iteration tracking |
| 5 | **Iteration Commitment** | $C_{iter} \le \bar{V}_{3\text{-}5\ iter}$ | Story Points | What team can take on |
| 6 | **TDD Cycle** | $\text{Red} \to \text{Green} \to \text{Refactor}$ | — | Engineering loop |
| 7 | **Sustainable Pace** | $W_{max} \le 40$ hours/week | Hours | Anti-burnout |
| 8 | **Pair Programming Gain** | $G_{effective} = 1.15$ to $1.5 \times W_{solo}$ | Multiplier | Defect reduction |
| 9 | **Refactor ROI** | $ROI_{refactor} = \dfrac{\Delta\ Maintenance\ Time}{Refactor\ Effort}$ | Ratio | When to refactor |
| 10 | **Tech Debt Interest** | $I_{debt} = D \times t$ where $D$ is debt, $t$ is time | — | Cumulative cost |

> **Note for the student:** The exam will *not* ask you to "memorize the formula" — but the *first three* formulas are absolute gold for the 14-mark question on release planning.

### 2.5 Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Refactoring + TDD** is the foundation of modern DevOps pipelines (CI/CD).
- **Velocity** is the basis of every Scaled Agile (SAFe) PI Planning session.
- **Technical Debt** underpins SonarQube's debt ratio metric (used in 60%+ Fortune 500 firms).
- **Timeboxing** is the core mechanism of **Pomodoro Technique**, **Scrum sprints**, and **Sprint Reviews**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section is **mandatorily exhaustive** — every algebraic and logical transition is written out. Each step is anchored to XP's real-world engineering practice.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Velocity Calculation (Iterative Average)

We want to compute a **stable, predictive velocity** for an XP team that has completed **N** iterations.

**Step 1 — Record completed story points per iteration.**

Let $SP_1, SP_2, SP_3, \ldots, SP_N$ represent the story points delivered in iterations 1 through $N$.

**Step 2 — Express total work delivered.**

The total work delivered across all iterations is simply the sum of completed story points:

$$
\text{TotalWork} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} SP_i
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average velocity.**

The velocity $V$ is the *average* story points per iteration, i.e., total work divided by number of iterations:

$$
V = \frac{\text{TotalWork}}{N} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} SP_i}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply smoothing (last-3 to last-5 iterations).**

KTU boards accept the **rolling average** of the last 3 to 5 iterations as a *better predictor* than the lifetime average (which is biased by early, slower iterations):

$$
V_{rolling} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=N-k+1}^{N} SP_i \quad \text{where} \; k \in \{3, 4, 5\}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Release Date Estimation

**Given:**
- Total estimated story points in the project backlog: $S_{total}$
- Team's stable velocity: $V$ (story points / iteration)
- Iteration length: $L_{iter}$ (days or weeks)

**Step 1 — Compute number of iterations required.**

Number of iterations is the total work divided by the rate of delivery:

$$
I_{required} = \frac{S_{total}}{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Round up to the next whole iteration.**

You cannot deliver a *fraction* of an iteration; the team must round up to the next integer:

$$
I_{release} = \lceil I_{required} \rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to calendar time.**

Multiply the required iterations by the length of each iteration:

$$
T_{release} = I_{release} \times L_{iter}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example.**

A team has a velocity $V = 12$ SP/iteration, a backlog of $S_{total} = 100$ story points, and a 2-week iteration ($L_{iter} = 2$ weeks).

$$
I_{required} = \frac{100}{12} \approx 8.33
$$

Round up to 9 iterations:

$$
I_{release} = \lceil 8.33 \rceil = 9 \; \text{iterations}
$$

Total release time:

$$
T_{release} = 9 \times 2 = 18 \text{ weeks}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Iteration Commitment Decision

**Given:**
- Rolling velocity $V_{rolling} = 10$ SP
- Total remaining work in iteration backlog: $W_{iter}$
- Available team capacity for the iteration: $C_{team}$ (in SP)

**Decision Rule:**

The team should commit to a workload $W_{commit}$ that is the *lesser* of (a) the customer's requested stories and (b) the team's proven capacity:

$$
W_{commit} = \min\!\left(\, W_{customer},\; V_{rolling} \,\right)
$$

This protects the team from overcommitment — a key XP rule. If $W_{customer} > V_{rolling}$, the *scope is cut* (Timeboxing rule), not the iteration length.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Technical Debt Quantification (Interest Model)

**Conceptual model (after Cunningham & Fowler):**

Let $D$ be the principal (initial debt) and $r$ be the *interest rate* per iteration (cost-of-change multiplier due to debt).

**Step 1 — Principal debt accumulated.**

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{cuts}} \text{CostOfCutAtIteration}_i
$$

where each "cost of cut" is the time saved by writing hacky code now.

**Step 2 — Interest accrued per iteration.**

After $t$ iterations, the cumulative cost of working with the debt is:

$$
I_{cumulative}(t) = D \times (1 + r)^t - D
$$

**Step 3 — Payoff by refactoring.**

Refactoring is a *principal payment*. After paying $P$ (effort) the new debt is:

$$
D_{new} = D - P
$$

**Step 4 — Break-even condition.**

Refactoring is justified if the saved future interest exceeds the refactoring cost:

$$
P_{refactor} < I_{cumulative}^{saved}
$$

That is, if the time saved over future iterations (no interest on the now-paid portion) exceeds the time spent refactoring, refactor — otherwise wait.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: XP vs Other Methodologies (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | XP | Scrum | Waterfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iteration Length** | 1–4 weeks | 2–4 weeks | None (single pass) |
| **Customer Presence** | **On-site, full-time** | Periodic reviews | At start + acceptance |
| **Changes Welcomed?** | **Yes, anytime** | Allowed in backlog | Formally controlled |
| **Engineering Practices** | **12 explicit practices** | Scrum leaves it to team | None mandated |
| **Documentation** | Minimal ("code is design") | Minimal | Heavy |
| **Team Size (ideal)** | **2 to 12 programmers** | 5–9 | Variable |
| **Pace Rule** | **40 hrs/week (sustainable)** | Similar, but less explicit | Often overtime |
| **Velocity Tracking** | **Yes (story points / week)** | Yes (story points / sprint) | Effort estimation only |

### 3.6 XP Iteration Math Summary (Comprehensive Table)

| Concept | Symbol | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity | $V$ | $\sum SP_i / N$ | Work done per iteration |
| Iterations Needed | $I$ | $\lceil S_{total} / V \rceil$ | Time-to-release in iterations |
| Calendar Time | $T$ | $I \times L_{iter}$ | Wall-clock time |
| Iteration Burn | $B_i$ | $V_i - C_i$ | Over (+) / under (-) commitment |
| Cumulative Velocity Trend | $V_t$ | $\dfrac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} V_i$ | Mature velocity estimate |
